1.8: ego eimi to Alpha kai to Omega legei Kyrios ho Theos ho on kai ho en kai ho erchomenos ho Pantokrator: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega, says the Lord God, who is and who was and who is to come, the Almighty.' Alpha kai Omega: the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet — a comprehensive inclusio encompassing all of reality. The phrase is applied both to God the Father (1:8; 21:6) and to Jesus (22:13: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end') — the shared title making the Christological identification with YHWH explicit. Pantokrator (Almighty): the LXX translation of Hebrew El Shaddai and YHWH Tsvaot (LORD of hosts) — here applied to both Father and Son as the supreme governing power over all creation.
1.17: ego eimi ho protos kai ho eschatos kai ho zon kai egenomen nekros kai idou zon eimi eis tous aionas ton aionon: 'I am the first and the last, and the living one. I died, and behold I am alive forevermore.' The phrase ho protos kai ho eschatos (first and last) is YHWH's self-identification in Isa 41:4; 44:6; 48:12 — the exclusive divine claim to be both origin and terminus of all history. Jesus applies this YHWH-title to himself in combination with the resurrection claim: 'I died and behold I am alive.' The resurrection is the proof that the crucified one is the living YHWH — the divine identity claim vindicated by rising from death.
1.1: apokalypsis Iesou Christou: 'Revelation/disclosure of Jesus Christ.' Apokalypsis (G0602) names the genre: an unveiling of what is hidden. The genitive is double: Jesus Christ is both the source (revelation given by/through him) and the content (revelation about him). Dei genesthai en tachei — must happen soon: dei (divine necessity, the language of God's sovereign plan) + en tachei (in swiftness/soon; not 'soon' in the sense of within a calendar, but with urgency — the apocalyptic 'soon' of imminence). Esēmanen (signified/made known by signs): from sēmeion (sign/symbol), implying the book communicates through symbolic-visionary language, not straightforward prediction.
1.2: emartyresin ton logon tou theou kai tēn martyrian Iesou Christou: 'bore witness to the word of God and to the testimony of Jesus Christ.' Emartyresin (aorist): John's witness is a completed act — he recorded everything he saw. The double genitive martyria Iesou is programmatic: it means both the testimony about Jesus (objective genitive) and the testimony belonging to Jesus/which Jesus himself gives (subjective genitive); the ambiguity is deliberate throughout Revelation.
1.3: makarios ho anaginōskōn kai hoi akouontes: 'Blessed is the one who reads aloud, and blessed are those who hear.' The singular anaginōskōn (one reading aloud) and plural akouontes (those hearing) reflect early Christian practice: one lector reading the scroll in the assembly, many listeners — the book was composed to be performed. Terountes ta en autē gegrammena: keeping/obeying (present participle, ongoing) what is written — hearing alone is not the blessing; the blessing belongs to those who hear and keep.
1.4: ho ōn kai ho ēn kai ho erchomenos: 'the one who is and who was and who is to come.' The phrase is syntactically remarkable: instead of ho esomenos (who will be), John writes ho erchomenos (who is coming). God is not merely 'the one who will exist' but 'the one who is coming' — his futurity is active arrival, not passive existence. The seven spirits before the throne (v. 4) alludes to Isa 11:2's sevenfold Spirit; the number seven = completeness/perfection, so this is the fullness of the Holy Spirit's presence.
1.5: ho martys ho pistos, ho prōtotokos tōn nekrōn, ho archōn tōn basileōn tēs gēs: three titles in a single phrase. Martys (witness): in Revelation, 'witness' carries the weight of one who testifies at cost of life — Jesus is the faithful witness because his testimony led to his death (the semantic development toward 'martyr'). Prōtotokos tōn nekrōn (firstborn of the dead): not born first among the dead but the one who inaugurates resurrection — the 'firstborn' as the one who opens the womb of death (Col 1:18 parallel). Ho agapōnti hēmas: present participle — not 'who loved us' (past) but 'the one who loves us' (ongoing present love).
1.6: epoiēsen hēmas basileian hiereis: 'made us a kingdom, priests.' The aorist epoiēsen marks this as accomplished fact, not future aspiration. The construction alludes to Exod 19:6 LXX (basileion hierateuma, royal priesthood) — what Israel was called to be at Sinai has been constituted in Christ. The MKT follows the reading basileian hiereis (a kingdom, priests) rather than basileion hierateuma (royal priesthood), following Codex Alexandrinus; the meaning is the same: priestly mediation joined to royal authority.
1.7: idou erchetai meta tōn nephelōn: present tense coming — not 'will come' (eleusetai) but 'is coming' (erchetai), the same present used of Christ's coming throughout Revelation; the parousia is in motion. Kopsetai ep auton pasai hai phylai tēs gēs: 'all the tribes of the earth will mourn/wail for him' — kopsomai is the word for funeral lamentation; Zech 12:10-12 uses it for the mourning over the pierced one. Nai kai amēn: double affirmation, combining Greek (nai) and Hebrew/Aramaic (amēn) for emphatic certainty.
1.9: synkoinōnos en tē thlipsei kai basileia kai hypomonē: 'fellow-partaker in the affliction and kingdom and patient endurance.' The three nouns are linked by a single article (tē): affliction, kingdom, and endurance are a single package — the kingdom is already present, but the path through it requires endurance of affliction. Hypomonē (patient endurance) is not passive waiting but active staying under pressure. John is in thlipsis (affliction) on Patmos because of witness — not despite his relationship to the kingdom but as the form that kingdom-life takes under opposition.
1.10: egenomēn en pneumati en tē kyriakē hēmera: 'I came to be in the Spirit on the Lord's day.' Egenomēn (aorist): a moment of transition — John entered a state of Spirit-possession. Kyriakē hēmera (Lord's Day) is the first occurrence of this phrase in the NT; it identifies Sunday as the day of Christian assembly in commemoration of the resurrection. Phōnēn opisthen mou hōs salpiggos: voice behind him like a trumpet — the spatial disorientation (behind, not in front) and the trumpet sound signal divine address; compare Exod 19:16 (trumpet sound at Sinai).
1.11: ho blepeis grapson eis biblion kai pempson tais hepta ekklēsiais: 'What you see, write into a scroll and send to the seven churches.' The commission is visual-to-written: John is commanded to transcribe his vision (ho blepeis, what you are seeing) as a letter to seven named communities. The seven churches are historically identifiable cities in Roman Asia (modern western Turkey) along a roughly circular mail route — the Revelation was a circular letter intended to be read in each assembly.
1.12: hepta lychnias chrysas: seven golden lampstands. The lampstand (lychnia) is the menorah-form of the tabernacle and temple (Exod 25:31-40), redeployed here for seven separate churches. Christ walking en mesō tōn hepta lychniōn (in the midst of the seven lampstands) recalls the priestly role of tending the menorah — Christ as the heavenly priest maintaining/attending to the churches, his seven-fold presence among them.
1.13: homoion huion anthrōpou: 'like a son of man' — homoion + accusative, not the full Danielic title but the allusive vision-form (Dan 7:13; 10:16). The description that follows combines Daniel's heavenly figure with Ezekiel's: endedymenon podērē (clothed to the feet: the priestly/royal long robe), periezōsmenon pros tois mastois zonēn chrysēn (girded at the chest with a golden sash — the position of the sash shifts from the waist, where soldiers wear it, to the chest, the position of seated authority or priestly service).
1.14: hē kephalē autou kai hai triches leukai hōs erion leukon hōs chiōn: head and hair white as wool, white as snow — drawn directly from the Ancient of Days in Dan 7:9 (the divine throne-figure with white like wool). By applying this description to the glorified Christ, Revelation identifies Jesus with the divine throne-figure of Daniel. Hoi ophthalmoi autou hōs phlox pyros (eyes like a flame of fire): from Dan 10:6, the heavenly man with eyes like torches of fire — omniscience and penetrating judgment.
1.15: hoi podes autou homoioi chalkolibanō hōs en kaminos pepyrōmenēs: feet like burnished bronze/electrum, as if refined in a furnace — from Dan 10:6 and Ezek 1:7 (gleaming bronze). Chalkolibanos is a hapax legomenon in the NT, possibly meaning a precious brass alloy or incense-copper; its exact identification is uncertain, but the refined-in-fire imagery signals purification and solidity. Kai hē phōnē autou hōs phōnē hydatōn pollōn: voice like many waters — Ezek 43:2's description of God's voice returning to the restored temple.
1.16: echōn en tē dexia cheiri autou asteras hepta kai ek tou stomatos autou rhomphaia distomos oxeia ekporeuomenē: holding seven stars in his right hand; from his mouth a sharp double-edged sword. Rhomphaia (the large Thracian sword, a broad-bladed weapon) rather than machaira (the short sword) — Revelation consistently uses rhomphaia for the sword from Christ's mouth (1:16; 2:12, 16; 19:15, 21), perhaps to emphasize its imposing, judicial weight. The sword from the mouth = the word of divine judgment: Christ's weapon is speech, not physical force.
1.18: echō tas kleidas tou thanatou kai tou Hadou: 'I hold the keys of Death and Hades.' Echō (present tense): Christ currently and continuously possesses these keys — this is his present status as the risen one. Death and Hades are personified as a paired power throughout Revelation (6:8; 20:13-14), functioning as the last enemies; to hold their keys is to control entry and exit from the realm of the dead. The resurrection is not just Christ's personal survival but his seizure of authority over the entire structure of mortality.
1.19: grapson oun ha eideskon kai ha eisin kai ha mellei genesthai meta tauta: 'Write therefore what you saw and what is and what is to take place after this.' The three-part formula has generated debate about Revelation's structure: (1) what you saw = ch. 1 vision; (2) what is = chs. 2-3 (the churches as they presently are); (3) what is to come = chs. 4-22. Ha mellei genesthai: the events that are about to happen — mellei (is about to) again signals imminence.
1.20: to mystērion tōn hepta asterōn... kai tas hepta lychnias tas chrysas: 'the mystery of the seven stars... and the seven golden lampstands.' Mystērion (mystery): a secret that has been disclosed — the vision's imagery is explained by Christ himself. The seven stars are angeloi (messengers/angels) of the seven churches — whether guardian angels, heavenly representatives, or human leaders (bishops/overseers) is debated; the usage allows both. The lampstands are the churches themselves, visible light-bearers in the world, tended by the one who walks among them.

4.8: hagios hagios hagios Kyrios ho Theos ho Pantokrator ho en kai ho on kai ho erchomenos: 'Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord God Almighty, who was and is and is to come.' The Trisagion (Isa 6:3) is adapted with the temporal formula that characterizes Revelation's divine self-identification. The fourfold repetition of praise from the four living creatures links Revelation's throne-room to Isaiah's call-vision: the same divine holiness that destroyed Isaiah's self-confidence before the vision of YHWH is now the content of ceaseless heavenly worship. John hears the cosmic liturgy that underlies all earthly worship.
4.1: idou thyra ēnoigmenē en tō ouranō: 'Behold, a door was standing open in heaven.' Ēnoigmenē (perfect passive participle): the door had been opened and stands open — not freshly opened at the moment of John's vision but already-open for his entry. Anaba hōde kai deixō soi ha dei genesthai meta tauta: come up here and I will show you what must take place after these things — dei genesthai (must happen) repeats the divine necessity of 1:1. The ascent structure follows Jewish apocalyptic convention (1 Enoch, 2 Enoch, Isa 6) — the seer is taken up to receive the heavenly perspective on earthly events.
4.2: euthēōs egenomēn en pneumati: 'immediately I was in the Spirit.' The second Spirit-transport in Revelation (cf. 1:10) — egenomēn en pneumati (aorist: I came to be in the Spirit) marks a definite transition. Kai idou thronos ekeito en tō ouranō, kai epi ton thronon kathēmenos: the throne as the visual center of the vision — who sits on it is not immediately named but described in terms of precious-stone appearance, deliberately avoiding direct description of the divine form (as in Ezek 1:26-28, which speaks of 'something like' human form on the throne).
4.3: ho kathēmenos homoios horasei lithō iaspidi kai sardios, kai iris kyklothen tou thronou homoios horasei smaragdinō: 'the one seated had the appearance of jasper and carnelian, and around the throne was a rainbow that had the appearance of an emerald.' The description proceeds entirely through simile (homoios: like, resembling) — the divine form is never described directly, only through layers of comparison. Iaspis (jasper): translucent crystal (21:11 calls it 'clear as crystal'); sardios (carnelian): deep red. The rainbow (iris) of emerald green alludes to Ezek 1:28 (the rainbow around the divine glory) — God's covenant promise encircling his throne.
4.4: kyklothen tou thronou thronoi eikosi tessares, kai epi tous thronous eikosi tessaras presbyterous kathēmenous: 'Around the throne were twenty-four thrones, and seated on the thrones were twenty-four elders.' Eikosi tessaras presbyterous: the identity of the twenty-four elders is debated. Three main proposals: (1) the 24 Davidic priestly courses of 1 Chr 24:1-19; (2) the 12 patriarchs + 12 apostles (the whole covenant people); (3) an angelic council. Their white garments and golden stephanoi (victor's wreaths, not royal crowns) favor a redeemed-humanity reading. The position of sitting on thrones signals delegated authority.
4.5: kai ek tou thronou ekporeuontai astrapai kai phōnai kai brontai: 'From the throne come flashes of lightning and rumblings and peals of thunder.' The three-element theophany formula (lightning, voices, thunder) recurs at each major structural marker in Revelation (8:5; 11:19; 16:18), always with escalating intensity — this is the Sinai-theophany pattern (Exod 19:16) transposed to the heavenly throne. Hepta lampades pyros kaiomenai enōpion tou thronou, hai eisin ta hepta pneumata tou theou: the seven torches are identified as the seven Spirits of God — the same Spirit-fullness present in the churches (1:4; 3:1) is here a blazing heavenly presence before the throne.
4.6: kai enōpion tou thronou hōs thalassa hyalinē homoion krystallō: 'Before the throne was something like a sea of glass, like crystal.' The glassy sea combines the bronze sea of Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 7:23-26, the large basin for priestly purification) and the sapphire pavement of Exod 24:10 (where the elders saw God at Sinai). Tessara zōia gemonta ophthalmon emprosthen kai opisthen: four living creatures full of eyes in front and behind — omniscient, all-seeing, combining Ezekiel's four creatures (Ezek 1:5-14) with Isaiah's seraphim (Isa 6:2-3).
4.7: to zōon to prōton homoion leonti, kai to deuteron zōon homoion moschō, kai to triton zōon echon to prosōpon hōs anthrōpou, kai to tetarton zōon homoion aetō petomenō: the four faces — lion, ox (or calf: moschos), human, eagle — correspond to Ezek 1:10's four faces of the heavenly creatures. In patristic tradition these four were linked to the four Evangelists (Irenaeus; Jerome). More precisely, they represent the four royal/divine qualities: lion (king of beasts, power), ox (service, sacrifice), human (intelligence, reason), eagle (divinity, transcendence, heaven).
4.9: kai hotan dōsousin ta zōia doxan kai timēn kai eucharistian tō kathēmenō epi tō thronō: 'And whenever the living creatures give glory and honor and thanksgiving to him who is seated on the throne.' The temporal conjunction hotan (whenever) + future indicatives describes an ongoing, repeated liturgical cycle — the heavenly worship is continual. The three-fold doxa, timē, eucharistia (glory, honor, thanksgiving) forms a liturgical pattern that the twenty-four elders' response (v. 10) expands.
4.10: pesountai hoi eikosi tessares presbyteroi enōpion tou kathēmenou epi tou thronou kai proskyneusousin tō zōnti eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn kai balousin tous stephanous autōn enōpion tou thronou: 'The twenty-four elders will fall down before him who is seated on the throne and worship him who lives forever and ever, and they cast their crowns before the throne.' The casting of stephanoi (crowns/wreaths) before the throne is the decisive gesture: the elders' authority and victory belongs to God, not to themselves. The victor's wreath has no meaning except as returned to its source.
4.11: axios ei, ho kyrios kai ho theos hēmōn, labein tēn doxan kai tēn timēn kai tēn dynamin: 'Worthy are you, our Lord and God, to receive glory and honor and power.' Axios (worthy) is the key term of chs. 4-5: first applied to God in the creation-doxology, then contested in ch. 5 (who is worthy to open the scroll?), then applied to the Lamb (5:9, 12). Ho kyrios kai ho theos hēmōn: 'our Lord and God' — this is precisely the title that the Emperor Domitian demanded for himself (Dominus et Deus noster); the throne-room doxology directly counters the imperial claim.

5.5: enikesen ho leon ho ek tes phyles Iouda e riza David anoixai to biblion: 'The Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered, so that he can open the scroll.' The title-cluster is Christological recapitulation: Leon ek phyles Iouda (Lion-of-Judah: Gen 49:9-10), Riza David (Root of David: Isa 11:1, 10; Rev 22:16), enikesen (has conquered). Then the stunning inversion: the 'Lion' who conquers is revealed as a 'Lamb standing as if slain' (v. 6). John hears Lion, then sees Lamb — the conquering is through the cross, not through military force. This Lion-Lamb inversion is Revelation's central hermeneutic: power is redefined by the slain Lamb who stands.
5.1: kai eidon epi tēn dexian tou kathēmenou epi tou thronou biblion gegrammenon esōthen kai opisthen, katesphraismenon sphragisin hepta: 'I saw in the right hand of him seated on the throne a scroll written inside and on the back, sealed with seven seals.' Esōthen kai opisthen (inside and on the back): an opisthographon — a scroll written on both sides. Such scrolls were rare and indicated a document so full in content it overflowed the standard single-side. Seven seals: divine completeness/perfection of the sealing — the scroll's contents are fully secured and cannot be accessed until the seals are broken by the worthy one.
5.2: kai eidon aggelon ischyron kēryssonta en phōnē megalē; Tis axios anoixai to biblion kai lysai tas sphragas autou: 'I saw a mighty angel proclaiming with a loud voice: Who is worthy to open the scroll and break its seals?' Ischuron aggelon (mighty angel): this figure appears again in 10:1 and 18:21 — a supernaturally powerful heavenly being whose proclamation traverses the entire cosmos (v. 3). Axios (worthy): the scroll-crisis is a worthiness test; no one passes initially, intensifying the revelation of the Lamb's singular qualification.
5.3: kai oudeis edynato en tō ouranō oude epi tēs gēs oude hypokatō tēs gēs anoixai to biblion oude blepein auto: 'No one in heaven or on earth or under the earth was able to open the scroll or to look into it.' The tripartite cosmos (heaven, earth, underworld) is searched exhaustively — no created being anywhere in existence meets the criterion. The totality of the failure intensifies the worthiness of the one who does step forward.
5.4: kai eklaion poly, hoti oudeis axios heurethē anoixai to biblion oude blepein auto: 'I wept greatly because no one was found worthy to open the scroll or to look into it.' Eklaion (imperfect): ongoing weeping, not a single cry. The imperfect captures John's sustained distress while the search proved fruitless. The scroll contains the plan of God for history (chs. 6-22 unfold its contents) — if no one can open it, history has no redemptive resolution. John weeps for the apparent inaccessibility of God's purpose.
5.6: arnion hestēkos hōs esphagmenon, echon kerata hepta kai ophthalmous hepta: 'a Lamb standing, as though it had been slain, with seven horns and seven eyes.' Arnion (little lamb): Revelation's preferred word for Christ (29 times), distinct from amnos (used in John 1:29; 1 Pet 1:19). The diminutive arnion emphasizes smallness and vulnerability. Hōs esphagmenon (as if slain): the wounds of the cross are visible in the glorified body — the Lamb bears permanent marks of sacrifice. Hestēkos (standing, perfect participle): it was slain but stands — resurrection expressed in a participle. Seven horns (omnipotence, via the OT horn-as-power symbol) + seven eyes (omniscience, identified as the seven Spirits of God) = the slain Lamb who is simultaneously omnipotent and omniscient.
5.7: kai ēlthen kai eilēphen ek tēs dexias tou kathēmenou epi tou thronou: 'He came and took it from the right hand of him seated on the throne.' Two aorists in sequence: ēlthen (came) and eilēphen (took). The taking is a decisive act — the Lamb approaches and receives the scroll from God's hand. In the context of the worthiness contest (vv. 2-4), this single action resolves the cosmic crisis: the one worthy to open history's scroll is the slain Lamb, and he takes what belongs to him.
5.8: phialas chrysas gemousas thymiamaton, hai eisin hai proseuchai tōn hagiōn: 'golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of the saints.' The identification is explicit: the incense in the heavenly temple is the accumulated prayers of the community on earth. In the OT, incense was offered on the golden altar before the Most Holy Place (Exod 30:1-10) as a symbol of prayers ascending to God. Here the twenty-four elders and four living creatures hold the very prayers of the church — every prayer uttered on earth has a heavenly counterpart in these bowls. Kitharan (harp): liturgical instrument, the instrument of Levitical temple worship (1 Chr 25:1-8).
5.9: ōdēn kainēn: 'a new song.' The ōdē kainē formula (Ps 96:1; 98:1; 144:9; Isa 42:10) marks the song as celebrating a new redemptive act of God — not repetition of a known melody but improvised praise for an unprecedented deed. Hoti esphagēs kai ēgorasas tō haimati sou (because you were slain and purchased with your blood): the ground of the new song is the cross. Ēgorasas (you purchased): the aorist of agorazō (to buy in the marketplace) — redemption as purchase, the language of manumission. The price was Christ's blood.
5.10: kai epoiēsas autous tō theō hēmōn basileian kai hiereis kai basileusousin epi tēs gēs: 'You made them a kingdom and priests to our God, and they shall reign on the earth.' The repetition of Exod 19:6 (cf. 1:6) now placed in the mouth of the heavenly choir as a completed fact: the cross has constituted the redeemed community as kingdom-priests. Basileusousin epi tēs gēs (they shall reign on the earth): future tense — the eschatological reign has not yet arrived but is secured; the kingdom is real and its earthly expression is coming.
5.11: kai ēkousa phōnēn aggelōn pollōn kyklothen tou thronou kai tōn zōōn kai tōn presbyterōn, kai ēn ho arithmos autōn myriades myriadōn kai chiliades chiliadōn: 'I heard around the throne the voice of many angels, and their number was myriads of myriads and thousands of thousands.' Myriades myriadōn kai chiliades chiliadōn: drawn from Dan 7:10's description of the heavenly court (ten thousand times ten thousand stood before him). The number is maximally indefinite — uncountable multitudes. The witness expands from the four living creatures and twenty-four elders (v. 8) to the full angelic choir.
5.12: axios estin to arnion to esphagmenon labein tēn dynamin kai plouton kai sophian kai ischyn kai timēn kai doxan kai eulogian: 'Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing.' Seven attributes in the doxology (seven = completeness): dynamis, ploutos, sophia, ischus, timē, doxa, eulogia. This sevenfold doxology to the Lamb matches the threefold doxology to God in v. 11 and the fourfold in v. 13 — the deliberate numerical escalation reaching seven for the Lamb signals the fullness of divine honor directed to Christ. Ho esphagmenon (the slain one): the perfect participle — the slaying is a permanent defining characteristic of the Lamb's identity even in glory.
5.13: pasan ktistin hē en tō ouranō kai epi tēs gēs kai hypokatō tēs gēs kai epi tēs thalassēs kai ta en autois: 'Every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea and all that is in them.' The fourfold enumeration surpasses even the triple-cosmos of v. 3 — the sea is added as a fourth domain. The scope is absolute: no creature is excluded from the worship that concludes ch. 5. The doxology to 'him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb' places the Lamb as co-recipient of cosmic worship with God.
5.14: kai ta tessara zōia elegon Amēn: 'And the four living creatures said Amen.' Amēn as the liturgical seal of the cosmic doxology: the four living creatures who represent all creation affirm what every creature has sung. Kai hoi presbyteroi epesan kai prosekynesan: and the elders fell down and worshiped — the same prostration as in v. 8, now as the conclusion of the entire throne-room scene. The chapter ends in worship; Revelation's entire eschatological vision is framed by and resolved into worship of the Lamb.

13.18: de estin sophia ho echon noun psephisato ton arithmon tou theriou arithmos gar anthropou estin kai ho arithmos autou hexakosioi hexekonta hex: '666' — the number of the beast. Arithmos anthropou (number of a human/humanity) — the beast's number is not cosmic but human, not transcendent but sub-divine. Gematria (assigning numerical values to letters) was a common Greco-Roman literary technique. The best-attested solution: NERON KAISER in Hebrew letters (nun=50 + resh=200 + vav=6 + nun=50 + qoph=100 + samek=60 + resh=200 = 666). Nero Caesar as the prototype of the beast — the first Roman persecutor of Christians — makes the political-historical reference legible to John's seven churches (ca. 90-95 CE, under Domitian, the 'second Nero').
13.1: ek tēs thalassēs: from the sea. The sea (thalassa) as the source of evil and chaos is the OT background (Dan 7:3 — four great beasts come from the sea; Isa 57:20 — the wicked are like the tossing sea; Ps 74:13 — God crushing the sea-monster). Daniel's four beasts (lion, bear, leopard, terrible beast) represent successive empires; Revelation's single beast combines all four into the ultimate totalizing empire. Ten horns and seven heads with ten diadems: the beast's crown (diadema = royal crown, symbol of authority) count exceeds the dragon's seven (12:3), signaling that the beast surpasses the dragon in earthly political power.
13.2: to thērion ho eidon ēn homoion pardali: the beast combines leopard, bear, and lion — precisely the animals of Dan 7:3-7 but in reverse order (Daniel: lion → bear → leopard → terrible beast; Revelation lists them in reverse). The reversal may be chronological: Revelation's beast is the final empire, incorporating all previous empires into itself. Ho drakōn edōken autō tēn dynamin autou kai ton thronon autou kai exousian megalēn: the dragon gave it his power, throne, and great authority. The beast's imperial power is explicitly derived — it is not autonomous but delegated from the dragon, who himself has only usurped power that belongs to God.
13.3: hōs esphagmenēn eis thanaton: as if slaughtered to death — esphagmenēn is the same perfect passive participle used of the Lamb in 5:6 (hōs esphagmenon). The beast parodies the Lamb: slain-and-healed vs. slain-and-risen. The deadly wound healed (hē plēgē tou thanatou autou etherapeuthe) inverts the resurrection: the Lamb was killed and lives (1:18); the beast appears killed and revives. The Nero redivivus legend (Nero would return from death to reclaim power) likely underlies this imagery — Domitian as the second Nero, the beast revived. Hē holē gē ethaumasen opisō tou thēriou: the whole earth marveled and followed (opisō, behind, in the wake of) the beast — wonder as the basis for worship.
13.4: Tis homoios tō thēriō kai tis dynatai polemēsai met autou: "Who is like the beast, and who can make war against him?" — a deliberate parody of the divine acclamation in Exod 15:11 (Who is like you among the gods, O LORD?) and Ps 89:6 (Who is like the LORD?). The beast's apparent invulnerability generates a pseudo-monotheism: if no one can defeat him, he must be divine. The Roman imperial cult operated precisely on this logic — Caesar's universal power as evidence of divine status. Revelation unmasks this as dragon-worship (tō drakōnti prosekunēsan).
13.5: stoma laloun megala kai blasphēmias: a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies — the exact description of Daniel's little horn in Dan 7:8, 11, 20. The beast is identified as the Daniel-beast. Edothē autō exousia poiēsai mēnas tesserakonta [kai] dyo: authority was given (edothē — divine passive throughout ch. 13) for 42 months. The divine passive (edothē = it was given by God) is theologically critical: even the beast's authority is divinely permitted, not self-generated. God governs even the dragon's agents. 42 months = the same period as 12:6, 14 — the whole era of the beast's dominion is within God's sovereign time-frame.
13.6: blasphēmein to onoma autou kai tēn skēnēn autou: to blaspheme his name and his dwelling (skēnē). In Roman imperial theology, Domitian's title Dominus et Deus (Lord and God) directly appropriated the divine name. The blasphemy against God's skēnē (tabernacle/dwelling) and those who dwell in heaven targets the whole heavenly order: both the divine presence and the heavenly community. The beast's speech is the inversion of the Trisagion (4:8): where the heavenly creatures cry "Holy, holy, holy," the beast blasphemes.
13.7: edothē autō poiēsai polemon meta tōn hagiōn kai nikēsai autous: it was given to him (edothē — divine passive again) to make war on the saints and conquer (nikēsai) them. The apparent paradox: the beast conquers the saints, yet the saints conquer the beast (12:11). The resolution: the beast conquers physically (martyrdom) while the saints conquer spiritually (faith through death). The divine passive confirms that even the beast's persecution of the saints is within God's sovereign permission — a pastoral word to persecuted believers. Edothē autō exousia epi pasan phylēn kai laon kai glōssan kai ethnos: universal authority — the fourfold formula (tribe, people, tongue, nation) that in Revelation marks the scope of either the gospel (14:6) or the beast's dominion.
13.8: hou ou gegraptai to onoma autou en tō bibliō tēs zōēs tou Arniou tou esphagmenou apo katabolēs kosmou: everyone will worship whose name was not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world. The phrase "from the foundation of the world" is grammatically ambiguous: it can modify "slain" (the Lamb's sacrifice is eternal in God's plan, before creation) or "written" (election before creation). Both readings are theologically defensible and both are affirmed in Scripture (1 Pet 1:19-20 supports the former; Eph 1:4 and Rev 17:8 support the latter). The book of life belongs to the Lamb: election and atonement are inseparable — those written are those covered by the slain Lamb's blood.
13.9: ei tis echei ous akousatō: "If anyone has an ear, let him hear." The verbatim refrain of the seven letters (2:7, 11, 17, 29; 3:6, 13, 22) — each letter ends with this call to attend to the Spirit's word. Its insertion here signals that the vision of the beast is not mere symbolic spectacle but a pastoral word addressed to the churches. What the Seer sees in vision is what the churches experience historically: persecution, economic pressure, the demand for beast-cult worship. The call to hear is a call to discern their own situation in the prophetic vision.
13.10: ei tis eis aichmalōsian eis aichmalōsian hypagei: if anyone is for captivity, into captivity he goes. The saying mirrors Jer 15:2 (those for captivity, to captivity; those for the sword, to the sword). The application in context: those appointed for tribulation must endure it (hōde estin hē hypomone kai hē pistis tōn hagiōn = here is the patient endurance and faith of the saints). Hypomone (patient endurance, steadfast perseverance) is the key virtue of the Apocalypse — appearing at 1:9; 2:2-3, 19; 3:10; 13:10; 14:12. It is not passive resignation but active faithfulness maintained under eschatological pressure.
13.11: allo thērion ek tēs gēs: another beast, from the earth (not the sea). The second beast (later called the false prophet: 16:13; 19:20; 20:10). Dyo kerata homoia arniō (two horns like a lamb): a wolf in lamb's clothing — its appearance mimics the Lamb of ch. 5, but elalei hōs drakōn (it spoke like a dragon). The mouth reveals the nature: Jesus warned that false prophets come in sheep's clothing (Matt 7:15). The false prophet's two horns may evoke prophetic authority (horns = power); its lamb-like appearance = religious legitimacy.
13.12: poiei tēn gēn kai tous en autē katoikountas hina proskunēsousin to thērion to prōton: it causes the earth to worship the first beast. The false prophet's function is entirely doxological — it redirects worship. This is the demonic inversion of the Holy Spirit's role, who glorifies Christ (John 16:14): the false prophet glorifies the beast. Hē plēgē tou thanatou autou etherapeuthe: the healed death-wound again — the false prophet uses this miracle as the basis for coercion to worship.
13.13: semeia megala: great signs (sēmeia). The false prophet makes fire come down from heaven (ek tou ouranou katababainein) in front of everyone — echoing Elijah calling down fire on the prophets of Baal (1 Kgs 18:38) and on soldiers of Ahaziah (2 Kgs 1:10-12). The false prophet mimics the greatest OT prophetic miracle to legitimize the beast-cult. Jesus warned that false christs and false prophets will perform great signs and wonders to deceive even the elect (Matt 24:24; Mark 13:22) — Revelation shows this happening with the beast's prophet.
13.14: plana tous katoikountas epi tēs gēs dia ta sēmeia: it deceives (planaō — leads astray) those who dwell on the earth by means of the signs. Planaō is the core deception verb in Revelation — the dragon is "the one who leads the whole world astray" (12:9). The false prophet extends this deception through miraculous authentication. Legōn tois katoikousin epi tēs gēs poiēsai eikona tō thēriō: telling those who dwell on the earth to make an eikōn (image) of the beast. Roman emperor worship included statues of the emperor receiving divine honors — the eikōn is the cultus statue that becomes the focus of imperial worship.
13.15: dounai pneuma tē eikoni tou thēriou: to give pneuma (breath/spirit) to the image. Pneuma here = breath or life-spirit. The false prophet animates the idol — whether through ventriloquism, priestly manipulation, or demonic agency. This action parodies God breathing life into Adam (Gen 2:7: enephysēsen eis to prosōpon autou pnoēn zōēs). The idolatrous image receives a counterfeit pneuma from the false prophet rather than the life-breath that the true God alone can give. Hina hosoi ean mē proskunēsōsin tē eikoni tou thēriou apokanthōsin: those who refuse to worship the image are killed — the ultimate expression of the beast-cult's totalitarian claim.
13.16: poiē pantas: it causes all — the fourfold social spectrum (small and great, rich and poor, free and slave) is exhaustive: no social category is exempt from the economic-religious pressure. Charagma (mark, brand) — the technical Greek term for an official stamp, seal, or brand used on imperial documents, coins, and animals/slaves. The imperial seal on commercial documents and the use of coins bearing the emperor's image (with divine titles) made economic participation in Roman society a constant low-grade act of emperor acknowledgment. Revelation's charagma intensifies this to a formal religious mark — the satanic parody of the seal of God on the foreheads of the 144,000 (7:3; 9:4; 14:1).
13.17: hina mē tis dynētai agorasai ē pōlēsai ei mē ho echōn to charagma: that no one can buy (agorasai) or sell (pōlēsai) without the mark. Economic exclusion as persecution — the beast's economic control makes daily survival contingent on beast-cult participation. The verb agorazō (to buy) is theologically loaded in Revelation: it is also the redemption verb — the Lamb purchased (ēgorasas) the saints (5:9; 14:3-4). Those who refuse to be economically "bought" by the beast have been already "bought" by the Lamb. The two purchases are mutually exclusive.

21.3: idou he skene tou theou meta ton anthropon kai skemnosei met auton: 'Behold the dwelling place (skene) of God is with people, and he will dwell (skenosei) with them.' The skene-language deliberately echoes the wilderness tabernacle (Mishkan) and anticipates John 1:14 where the Word eskenosen (tabernacled) among us. The eschatological new creation is the realization of what the tabernacle mediated: direct divine presence without the veil, without the intermediary system, without separation — YHWH's face-to-face dwelling with his people. The Sinai covenant promise reaches its telos in the new Jerusalem.
21.1:
kainos ouranos kai kainē gē — kainos (qualitatively new, not chronologically new; contrast neos) is the word Revelation uses throughout the new-creation vision. The distinction matters: this is not a renovated cosmos but a new one of a different order altogether. The same word appears in 2 Cor 5:17 (new creation) and Isa 65:17 LXX.

ho gar prōtos ouranos kai hē prōtē gē apēlthan: the first heaven and earth apēlthan (went away, departed) — the same verb used in 21:4 for the old order passing. The absence of the sea (hē thalassa ouk estin eti) is significant: in ANE cosmology and in OT imagery, the sea represents primordial chaos (Gen 1:2), the domain of the beast (13:1), and the barrier that separates. Its disappearance signals the end of chaos, threat, and separation.
21.2:
tēn polin tēn hagian Ierousalēm kainēn eidon katabainousan ek tou ouranou apo tou theou: the city is katabainousan (descending, present participle) — an ongoing, visionary descent. The new Jerusalem is not a human achievement ascending from below but a divine gift descending from above. The grammar signals its character: this city originates with God, not with human civilization or religion.

hētoimasmenēn hōs nymphen kekosmēmenēn tō andri autēs: prepared as a bride adorned for her husband. The bride metaphor activates the OT covenant-marriage imagery (Isa 61:10; 62:5; Jer 2:2; Ezek 16) where YHWH is husband and Israel is bride. The new Jerusalem as the bride of the Lamb (v. 9) is the eschatological fulfillment of the entire covenant relationship: the People of God perfected and united with the God who covenanted with them.
21.4:
kai exaleipsei pan dakryon ek tōn ophthalmōn autōn: exaleipsei (will wipe out, erase completely; future active) — the same root is used in Acts 3:19 for the erasure of sins. God erases tears as decisively as he forgives guilt: both are obliterated, not merely set aside. The promise quotes Isa 25:8 (YHWH will swallow up death and wipe away tears from all faces) and reverses the mourning of Isa 65:19.

ho thanatos ouk estai eti — ouk ... eti (no longer, not any more): categorical negation with emphasis. Death itself ceases; this is not survival of death but its abolition. Ta prōta apēlthan (the first things have gone away) uses the same apēlthan as v. 1 — the old order departs as a unified whole: death, grief, pain, the sea, the first heaven and earth all belong to the old order that is now gone.
21.5:
idou kaina poiō panta: kaina (qualitatively new) with the present tense poiō (I am making) — the new creation is announced as a present act of the divine will, not a future plan. The one speaking is seated on the throne: in Revelation's theology the throne is shared between God the Father and the Lamb (22:1, 3), so the speaker carries the full divine authority that opened the cosmos in Genesis.

hoti houtoi hoi logoi pistoi kai alēthinoi eisin: the authentication formula (repeated at 22:6; 19:9) — these words are pistoi kai alēthinoi (faithful and true), the divine self-description from Rev 3:14 and 19:11. The words are vouched for by their source: the one who is Faithful-and-True declares that these words share his own character.
21.6:
gegonen: the perfect active of ginomai — "It has happened and stands accomplished." The same verb Jesus uses on the cross (tetelestai in John, but here using ginomai) signals the consummation of redemptive history. The use of the perfect (not the aorist) is precise: the new creation is not just completed but completed with permanent effect.

ego eimi to Alpha kai to Omega, hē archē kai to telos: the Alpha-Omega formula here is attributed to God the Father (as in 1:8); in 22:13 the same three-part formula (Alpha/Omega + First/Last + Beginning/End) is applied to Christ — the shared title making the Christological identification explicit. Hē archē kai to telos (the Beginning and the End) is new in v. 6, expanding the pair from ch. 1; all three formulas converge in 22:13.

tō dipsōnti dōsō ek tēs pēgēs tou hydatos tēs zōēs dōrean: dōrean (freely, as gift, at no cost) — this adverb characterizes the gift as grace: the water of life is not earned or purchased. The thirst imagery activates Isa 55:1 (come to the waters, you who are thirsty; come and buy without money) and John 4:14 (the water I give will become a spring welling up to eternal life).
21.7:
ho nikōn klēronomēsei tauta: ho nikōn (the one who conquers, present participle — continuous overcoming) echoes the promise to the nikōn in each of the seven letters (chs. 2–3). Klēronomēsei (will inherit) — inheritance language recalls the covenant promise to Abraham (Gen 12; 15; 17) and the promised land; here it is eschatological: the whole new creation is the inheritance of those who endure.

kai esomai autō theos kai autos estai moi [ho] huios: the covenant formula applied universally. 2 Sam 7:14 speaks of the Davidic king ("I will be his father and he will be my son"); Ps 89:26-27 applies it to the one who cries "My Father." Revelation applies this father-son adoption language to every overcomer — the promises made to the messianic king are extended to all who are in Christ.
21.8:
hoi deiloi: the cowardly/faithless-under-pressure — placed first in the exclusion list, ahead of murderers and sorcerers. Deilos is used in Mark 4:40 when Jesus rebukes the disciples' fear in the storm ("Why are you so afraid? Have you no faith?"). In Revelation's context of Roman persecution, the cowardly are those who apostatize to avoid suffering — a more acute pastoral concern than abstract vices.

ho thanatos ho deuteros: the second death. Revelation defines it as the lake of fire (20:14). The "second death" is a concept found in Targum literature (Tg. Deut 33:6; Tg. Isa 22:14; 65:6) where it means permanent, irreversible exclusion from God. Revelation adopts and crystalizes the term. The first death (biological) may claim the faithful; the second death (eternal exclusion) cannot (2:11; 20:6).
21.9: deuro, deixō soi tēn nymphen tēn gynaika tou arniou: "Come, I will show you the bride, the wife of the Lamb." This invitation deliberately mirrors 17:1 where the same angel invites John: "Come, I will show you the judgment of the great prostitute." The two visions are paired: Babylon the harlot (ch. 17–18) vs. Jerusalem the bride (ch. 21). The angel offers the same transportation to see radically different destinations — judgment and redemption shown by the same guide.
21.10: kai apēnegken me en pneumati epi oros mega kai hypsēlon: transported en pneumati (in the Spirit) to a great and high mountain. This visionary transportation echoes Ezek 40:1-2 precisely: the Spirit brings Ezekiel to "a very high mountain" to see the new temple city. John's new Jerusalem vision is the fulfillment of Ezekiel's vision — the mountain-top perspective enabling the Seer to receive what Ezekiel received as promise. The Spirit-transportation also echoes Ezek 3:14; 8:3; 11:1, 24.
21.11:
echousan tēn doxan tou theou: "having the glory of God" — the Shekinah, the divine radiance that filled the tabernacle (Exod 40:34-35) and the Solomonic temple (1 Kgs 8:10-11), and that Ezekiel saw depart from the temple at the Babylonian conquest (Ezek 10:18-19; 11:22-23), now permanently dwelling in the new Jerusalem. The city does not merely reflect glory — it has it as its intrinsic characteristic.

hō phōstēr autēs homoios lithō timiōtatō hōs lithō iaspidi krystalloeidei: the city's radiance compared to jasper clear as crystal. Jasper (iaspis) is used both for the appearance of the one on the throne (4:3) and now for the city that houses his presence — the city's appearance mirrors the divine glory itself. The precious stones throughout vv. 19-21 echo Ezek 28:13 (the king of Tyre adorned with precious stones in Eden) and the high-priestly breastplate (Exod 28:17-20).
21.12: echousa teicho mega kai hypsēlon echousa pylōnas dōdeka kai epi tois pylōsin angelous dōdeka kai onomata epigegrammena ha estin [ta onomata] tōn dōdeka phylōn huiōn Israēl: the wall with twelve gates and twelve angels, the gates bearing the names of the twelve tribes. Ezek 48:31-35 already assigned the twelve gates of the ideal city to the twelve tribes. The angels at the gates function as guardians — but also as witnesses to the access structure: the new Jerusalem is entered through the covenant people (the tribes) who represent the full scope of Israel's election.
21.13: apo anatolēs pylōnes treis kai apo borra pylōnes treis kai apo notou pylōnes treis kai apo dysmōn pylōnes treis: three gates on each of the four sides, facing all compass points. This orientation mirrors both Ezek 48:31-34 (same arrangement for the ideal city's gates) and the arrangement of the tribes around the tabernacle in the wilderness (Num 2). The four-directional opening signals the universal accessibility of the new Jerusalem: approach from every direction is possible. No gate is the "back entrance."
21.14: kai to teicho tēs poleōs echōn thremeliou dōdeka kai ep autōn dōdeka onomata tōn dōdeka apostolōn tou arniou: twelve foundations inscribed with the names of the twelve apostles. Gates named for the tribes (OT); foundations named for the apostles (NT). The city is built on both testimonies — the continuity of OT and NT revelation architecturally expressed. Eph 2:20 speaks of the church "built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets"; here that Pauline metaphor becomes literal architecture in the new Jerusalem. The apostles are apostles tou Arniou (of the Lamb) — their authority derives from and belongs to the Lamb.
21.15: ho lalōn met emou eichen metron kalamon chrysoun: a golden measuring rod. Measuring in apocalyptic literature signals divine claiming and protection (Ezek 40; Zech 2:1-5; Rev 11:1-2). That the measuring rod is chrysoun (golden) matches the city's materials — the instrument of measurement is made of what the city is made of, signaling that the city fully meets the divine standard. The angel's measurement is an act of divine affirmation: this city is exactly what God intends.
21.16: kai hē polis tetragonos keitai kai to mēkos autēs hoson [kai] to platos: the city is tetragonos (four-cornered, square — or by extension, cubic) — equal in length, width, and height. 12,000 stadia in each dimension. The cubic shape recalls the Holy of Holies in Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 6:20: twenty cubits in length, width, and height — a perfect cube). The entire new Jerusalem is the Holy of Holies — the inner sanctuary of divine presence expanded to cosmic proportions. What was restricted to the high priest once a year is now the entire dwelling of the redeemed.
21.17: kai emethrēsen to teicho autou hekatōn tessarakonta tessarōn pēchōn metrou anthrōpou ho estin angelou: 144 cubits (= 12 × 12) for the wall — another multiple of 12, the number of completeness in Revelation (twelve tribes, twelve apostles, 144,000). The curious note that the human measurement is also the angel's measurement (metrou anthrōpou ho estin angelou) may signal the unity of human and angelic existence in the new creation: the customary distinction between earthly and heavenly scales no longer applies.
21.18: kai hē endōmēsis tou teichous autēs iaspis kai hē polis chrysos katharos homoios hyalō katharō: endōmēsis is a rare word (possibly a hapax in biblical Greek) for "building material" or "construction." Pure gold transparent as glass is physically impossible — gold is opaque. The description signals that the new creation's materials exceed what the old physics permits: this is not renovated matter but transfigured matter, the stuff of a world where divine glory permeates everything. The transparency of gold implies there is nothing hidden, no opacity between creature and Creator.
21.19: hoi themelioi tou teichous tēs poleōs panti lithō timiō kekosmēmenoi: the twelve foundations adorned with precious stones. The list (jasper, sapphire, agate, emerald, onyx, ruby, chrysolite, beryl, topaz, chrysoprase, jacinth, amethyst) parallels but does not perfectly match the high priestly breastplate of Exod 28:17-20 — eight of the twelve correspond. The LXX order differs from the MT. The connection to the breastplate is deliberate: the high priest bore the names of the twelve tribes over his heart as he entered the sanctuary; the new Jerusalem's foundations bear the apostles' names. The priestly imagery is transferred from one individual mediator to the entire apostolic foundation of the city.
21.20: ho pemptos sardonyx ho hektos sardion ho hebdomos chrysolithos ho ogdoos bēryllos ho ennatos topazion ho dekatos chrysoprason ho hendekatos hyakinthos ho dōdekatos amethystos: the list of the final eight foundation stones. Ancient gemstone terminology is imprecise and identification of the exact minerals is uncertain — Greek and Hebrew stone names do not map neatly onto modern mineralogical categories. What is clear is the visual effect: the twelve foundations are a spectrum of color, light, and brilliance. The cumulative image is not a building inventory but a vision of transcendent beauty — the city adorned as a bride (v. 2) made architectural.
21.21:
kai hoi dōdeka pylōnes dōdeka margaritai, heis hekastos tōn pylōnōn ēn ex henos margaritou: each gate a single pearl. A pearl of sufficient size to serve as a city gate is obviously beyond natural possibility — the image functions as a superlative of purity and singular beauty. Pearls in the ancient world were considered the most precious of gems (Matt 13:46 uses the single great pearl as the image for the kingdom). Each gate is irreducibly one thing — unified, perfect, unbroken.

kai hē plateia tēs poleōs chrysos katharos hōs hyalos diaphanes: the main street of pure gold transparent as glass. Plateia (broad way, main street, public space) is singular — the whole city has one street, or the entire ground level is this open transparent space. The transparency motif recurs: nothing opaque, nothing hidden, divine light permeating the material structure of the city itself.
21.22:
kai naon ouk eidon en autē: "I did not see a temple in it." The deliberate absence. The temple was the locus of divine presence in the old order — the place where heaven and earth intersected, where priests mediated access to God, where sacrifice was offered. Its absence is not a loss but a fulfillment: there is no need for a mediated presence when the presence is direct and total.

ho gar Kyrios ho Theos ho Pantokrator naos autēs estin kai to Arnion: the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple. God and the Lamb together constitute the temple — singular — the co-divine presence that fills the new Jerusalem without boundary or mediation. This fulfills the trajectory from Exod 25:8 (let them make me a sanctuary that I may dwell among them) through John 2:19-21 (the temple he spoke of was his body) to Rev 21:3 (the dwelling of God is with people). The tabernacle, the temple, the incarnate Christ, and the new Jerusalem form one continuous line of divine-presence theology.
21.23:
kai hē polis ou chreian echei tou hēliou oude tēs selēnēs hina phainōsin autē: "the city has no need of the sun or moon to shine on it." This fulfills Isa 60:19-20 verbatim in sense: "No longer will you have the sun for light by day ... for the LORD will be your everlasting light." The sun and moon are part of the old creation's luminous order; in the new creation the divine glory replaces them.

hē gar doxa tou theou ephōtisen autēn kai ho lychnos autēs to Arnion: the glory (doxa, Shekinah) of God is the city's light source, and the Lamb is its lychnos (lamp, luminous body). The same Christ who declared "I am the light of the world" (John 8:12; 9:5) is the lamp of the new Jerusalem. The divine glory and the Lamb's lamp are distinguished but function together — the Trinitarian shape of the new creation's illumination.
21.24:
kai peripatēsousin ta ethnē dia tou phōtos autēs: "the nations will walk by its light." The inclusion of ta ethnē (the nations/Gentiles) as permanent inhabitants and participants fulfills the long prophetic vision of Isa 60:3 (nations shall come to your light) and Zech 14:16 (all nations will go up year after year to worship the King). This is not Gentile conversion to Israel's religion but the eschatological unity of all peoples in the new Jerusalem — the culmination of the Abrahamic promise that all nations would be blessed (Gen 12:3).

kai hoi basileis tēs gēs pherousin tēn doxan autōn eis autēn: the kings of the earth bring their doxa (glory, splendor) into the city. These are the same basileis tēs gēs who throughout Revelation opposed God and the Lamb (6:15; 17:2; 18:3). Their tribute in the new creation reverses their rebellion in the old order: those who resisted now bring honor freely. This is not universalism but the comprehensive sovereignty of the new creation encompassing even those who opposed it.
21.25:
kai hoi pylōnes autēs ou mē kleisthōsin hēmeras: "its gates shall never be shut by day" — the gates of the ancient city were shut at night for security. The new Jerusalem's gates never close because there is no threat, no night, no hostile force that could require them. Open gates signal total security: the city is not fortified against enemies because there are none. The promise fulfills Isa 60:11 (your gates shall be open continually, day and night they shall not be shut).

nyx gar ouk estai ekei: "for there will be no night there." Night in Revelation is associated with darkness, hiddenness, and the activities of evil (1 Thess 5:5-7; John 3:19). Its abolition in the new Jerusalem (repeated in 22:5) is not merely a meteorological observation but a theological one: the conditions for evil, deception, and darkness no longer obtain.
21.26: kai oisousin tēn doxan kai tēn timēn tōn ethnōn eis autēn: "they will bring the glory and honor of the nations into it." Doxa kai timē (glory and honor) — the paired terms used of God's worthiness in doxological contexts (4:9, 11; 5:12-13). That the nations bring doxa implies that what the nations had — their cultures, achievements, excellences — is not destroyed in the new creation but transformed and offered. The new Jerusalem receives the best of human history, transfigured and consecrated.
21.27:
kai ou mē eiselthē eis autēn pan koinon kai [ho] poiōn bdelygma kai pseudos: the double negative ou mē with the aorist subjunctive is emphatic prohibition — nothing common/impure shall enter. Koinon (common, defiling — the cultic term for what violates ritual purity; Acts 10:14-15 uses it for what Peter considered unclean). Bdelygma (abomination) echoes the "abomination of desolation" (Dan 9:27; 11:31; Mark 13:14) — here permanently excluded rather than temporarily installed in the temple.

ei mē hoi gegrammenoi en tō bibliō tēs zōēs tou Arniou: "except those written in the Lamb's book of life." The only qualification for entry is name-inscription by the Lamb — not ethnic identity, moral achievement, or ritual status, but the Lamb's own record. The biblion tēs zōēs (book of life) appears throughout Revelation (3:5; 13:8; 17:8; 20:12, 15) as the definitive eschatological register. It belongs to the Lamb: he holds the criterion of entry.

22.20: nai erchomai tachy (nai erchomai tachy): 'Surely I am coming soon.' The final direct speech of Christ in the canon is a promise of return. Tachy (soon/quickly): the eschatological urgency that has characterized the entire Revelation. The community's response — Amen, erchou Kyrie Iesou (Come, Lord Jesus) — is the Greek form of the Aramaic Maranatha (1 Cor 16:22; Didache 10:6). The canon ends with a promise and a prayer: Christ's 'I am coming' and the community's 'Come.' The entire Christian life is lived in this tension of promise-and-prayer, the already of Christ's victory and the not yet of his return.
22.1: kai edeixen moi potamon hydatos zōēs lampron hōs krystallon ekporeuomenon ek tou thronou tou theou kai tou Arniou: the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, proceeding from the throne of God and of the Lamb. The singular throne shared by God and the Lamb (also v. 3) carries Christological weight: Revelation insists on co-enthronement, not adjacent thrones. The river's source is the co-divine throne, echoing Ezek 47:1-12 (the temple-river flowing east bringing life) and Zech 14:8 (living waters flowing from Jerusalem). In Ezekiel's vision the river flows from the temple; here the throne replaces the temple (21:22) as the source of living water.
22.2:
en mesō tēs plateias autēs kai tou potamou enteuthen kai ekeithen xylon zōēs poioun karpous dōdeka kata mēna: the tree of life (xylon zōēs) standing on both sides of the river, bearing twelve fruits, one each month — monthly fruit, not seasonal. The perpetual and varied fruitfulness signals inexhaustible provision. Xylon zōēs (tree of life) is the Genesis tree (Gen 2:9; 3:22-24) from which humanity was barred after the Fall; the new creation restores unlimited access. The twelve fruits monthly is a temporal abundance that no natural growing season could sustain.

kai ta phyla tou xylou eis therapeia tōn ethnōn: "and the leaves of the tree for the healing of the nations." Therapeia (healing, therapy) applied to the nations — the tree's leaves heal at a national, not merely individual, scale. This picks up Ezek 47:12 (fruit for food and leaves for medicine/healing). The healing of the nations implies some form of ongoing restoration in the new creation, though the precise nature of this healing in an already-perfect world resists precise specification.
22.3:
kai pan katathema ouk estai eti: "and no curse will be there any more." Katathema (curse, accursed thing) — the curse of Gen 3:17-19 (cursed is the ground because of you) is the source of all human suffering, toil, death, and the frustration of creation (Rom 8:20-22). Its abolition is the reversal of the Fall's cosmic consequences. Paul's statement that "Christ became a curse for us" (Gal 3:13) is the atonement event that makes this eschatological abolition possible.

kai ho thronos tou theou kai tou Arniou en autē estai: the throne of God and the Lamb — singular throne, dual subjects. Grammatically striking: two persons, one throne. The Lamb's co-enthronement with the Father in the new Jerusalem is the final statement of Revelation's high Christology. Hoi douloi autou latreusousin autō: his servants will latreuō him — the verb specifically used for religious service and worship (Heb 12:28; Luke 2:37). The new creation is characterized by unmediated, ceaseless worship of the co-enthroned God and Lamb.
22.4:
kai opsontai to prosōpon autou: "they will see his face." The beatific vision — direct, unmediated, face-to-face knowledge of God. In the OT, seeing God's face was dangerous or impossible (Exod 33:20: "you cannot see my face, for no one may see me and live"; Gen 32:30: "I have seen God face to face and yet my life has been spared"). The new creation removes this barrier: the redeemed see the face of God directly because the old order of divine hiddenness has given way to full presence. This is the ultimate fulfillment of the priestly blessing (Num 6:26: "the LORD make his face shine on you").

kai to onoma autou epi tōn metōpōn autōn: his name on their foreheads — the opposite of the mark of the beast on the foreheads of his worshippers (13:16-17). The sealed 144,000 already bore God's seal on their foreheads (7:3; 14:1). In the new Jerusalem this identity-marking is permanent and visible: they belong to God and the Lamb, identified by name as his own. The name on the forehead is ownership, identity, and protection all at once.
22.5:
kai nyx ouk estai eti kai ou chreian echousin phōtos lychnou kai phōtos hēliou hoti Kyrios ho Theos phōtisei ep autous: no night, no lamp, no sun — the Lord God will shine on (phōtisei, illuminate) them directly. Phōtizō (to illuminate) is used of God's eschatological light (Isa 60:19 LXX; Rev 21:23). The abolition of created light sources is not loss but supersession: what the sun and lamp did partially and temporarily, God does fully and permanently.

kai basileusousin eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn: "they will reign for ever and ever." The saints' reign with Christ (20:6) is here made permanent and universal. Dan 7:18, 22, 27 promised that the holy ones of the Most High would receive the kingdom forever; Rev 22:5 declares this fulfilled. The redeemed do not merely dwell in the new creation but reign in it — the restoration of the royal image-bearing vocation of Gen 1:26-28, now fully realized.
22.6: hoi logoi pistoi kai alēthinoi: the authentication formula (also 19:9; 21:5). The repetition at the book's conclusion brackets the entire vision with divine guarantee: what was pledged at the outset of the epilogue is confirmed at its close. Kyrios ho theos tōn pneumatōn tōn prophētōn (the Lord, the God of the spirits of the prophets): a distinctive title found only here in Revelation, identifying God specifically as the inspirer of prophecy. The one who sent his angel to show the prophets what must soon take place is the same God who has been speaking through the prophets all along — continuity between OT prophecy and Revelation's vision asserted in the divine title itself.
22.7:
idou erchomai tachy: "Behold I am coming quickly/soon." The first of three erchomai tachy sayings in the chapter (vv. 7, 12, 20) — a rhetorical triad building to the final exchange with the community in v. 20. Tachy (quickly, soon) has been the eschatological urgency marker throughout Revelation (1:1; 2:16; 3:11; 22:7, 12, 20). The imminent return is not a mistaken prediction but an eschatological stance: the church lives under the horizon of the returning Lord.

makarios ho tērōn tous logous tēs prophēteias tou bibliou toutou: the seventh and final beatitude in Revelation (see 1:3; 14:13; 16:15; 19:9; 20:6; 22:14). Tērōn (keeping, guarding, observing) — present participle: ongoing keeping, not a single act. The beatitude at the book's close mirrors 1:3 (blessed is the one who reads and hears and keeps these words), forming a frame around the entire Apocalypse.
22.8:
kagō Iōannēs ho akouōn kai blepōn tauta: the Seer's self-identification — "I, John, the one hearing and seeing these things." The emphasis on hearing and seeing is Revelation's epistemological claim: this is received revelation (heard and seen), not theological speculation. The same identification occurs at 1:9 where John situates himself in the persecuted community. The repetition here at the book's end confirms the same human witness for both the beginning and the conclusion of the vision.

kai hote ēkousa kai eidon, epesa proskunēsai emprosthen tōn podōn tou angelou tou deiknuontos moi tauta: John falls to worship before the revealing angel — the same error as 19:10 (where the angel gives the same rebuke). The repetition is not careless; it illustrates the persistent human tendency to misdirect worship toward the mediator of revelation rather than the source. The correct response to overwhelming revelatory experience is not prostration before the messenger.
22.9: kai legei moi Hora mē syndoulos sou gar eimi kai tōn adelphōn sou tōn prophētōn kai tōn tērountōn tous logous tou bibliou toutou tō theō proskunēson: "See that you do not do this! I am a fellow servant with you and your brothers the prophets and with those who keep the words of this scroll. Worship God!" The angel's self-description as syndoulos (fellow-servant, co-slave) places him in the same category as John, the prophets, and all who keep the book's words. The hierarchy of worship is absolute: tō theō proskunēson (worship God [alone]) — the angel enforces monotheistic worship by refusing it for himself. Proskyneō (worship, bow before) is the term Revelation reserves for God and the Lamb (4:10; 5:14; 7:11; 11:16; 19:4, 10; 22:8-9).
22.10: Mē sphragisēs tous logous tēs prophēteias tou bibliou toutou: "Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this scroll." This is the direct inversion of Dan 12:4 (sphragison to biblion, "seal the book, Daniel, until the time of the end"). Daniel was told to seal because the fulfillment was distant; John is told not to seal because ho kairos engys (the time is near). The unsealing of John's prophetic scroll signals that the Danielic "time of the end" has arrived in and through Christ — the seals of history are being opened (ch. 6) rather than imposed. The eschatological "near" is the church's permanent posture of readiness, not a chronological prediction about a specific date.
22.11: ho adikōn adikēsatō eti, kai ho rhyparos rhyparasthētō eti, kai ho dikaios dikaiosynēn poiēsatō eti, kai ho hagios hagiasthētō eti: the fourfold declaration of fixity — let the unjust continue unjust, the defiled continue defiled, the righteous continue righteous, the holy continue holy. This is not permission for sin but an announcement of eschatological ultimacy: at the threshold of judgment, moral states are confirmed. The call to repentance is complete; the time for reversals is over. The hardened will not be transformed by more time; the holy will not be endangered by the end's arrival. The final state of each is the continuation of their present direction, accelerated to completion.
22.12: idou erchomai tachy kai ho misthos mou met emou apodounai hekastō hōs to ergon estin autou: the second erchomai tachy (v. 7, 12, 20). Here paired with the judgment: ho misthos mou met emou (my reward/wages are with me) — the eschatological recompense brought by the returning Lord. Apodounai hekastō hōs to ergon autou (to render to each according to their work) cites the standard OT judgment formula (Ps 62:12; Prov 24:12; Isa 40:10) applied to Christ: the one who comes is also the one who judges. This links the parousia and the judgment as one event, not two sequential stages.
22.13: ego to Alpha kai to Omega, ho prōtos kai ho eschatos, hē archē kai to telos: the threefold divine identity formula applied to Christ — the fullest divine self-identification in the NT. Each pair captures a different aspect: Alpha/Omega (the entire alphabet of reality, from first to last letter), First/Last (temporal priority and finality — echoing YHWH's self-identification in Isa 44:6 and 48:12), Beginning/End (archē kai telos — ontological primacy and consummation). In 1:8 the Alpha-Omega title is applied to "the Lord God Almighty" (the Father); here all three pairs are applied to Christ. Revelation's Christological high point: the crucified and risen Jesus is identified with YHWH's own divine self-designation.
22.14:
makarioi hoi plynnontes tas stolas autōn: "Blessed are those who wash their robes." The sixth beatitude in Revelation. An important textual variant: some MSS (including Codex A and the Textus Receptus) read poiountes tas entolas autou (doing his commandments); the UBS/NA28 critical text prefers plynnontes tas stolas autōn (washing their robes), supported by Sinaiticus and Alexandrinus. The robes-washing image echoes 7:14 (they washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb) — the passive work of atonement rather than active commandment-keeping. Both readings convey holiness, but the robes-washing variant locates holiness in Christ's atoning work.

hina estai hē exousia autōn epi to xylon tēs zōēs kai tois pylōsin eiselthōsin eis tēn polin: exousia (authority, right, legal standing) to the tree of life and entry through the gates. The legal term exousia (not merely dynamis = power) signals that the right to enter is forensic: it is granted, not seized. The tree of life access denied in Gen 3:24 is restored by the Lamb's atoning work that makes the robe-washers clean.
22.15: exō hoi kynes kai hoi pharmakoi kai hoi pornoi kai hoi phoneis kai hoi eidōlolatrai kai pas philōn kai poiōn pseudos: outside the city are five categories plus "all who love and practice falsehood." Kynes (dogs): in Jewish purity discourse, dogs were ritually unclean animals associated with those who returned to their vomit (2 Pet 2:22; Prov 26:11) and used metaphorically for those who had abandoned the faith or remained outside the covenant community. Phil 3:2 uses kynas for the circumcision party; here it is the final-state category for those outside the new Jerusalem. The list mirrors 21:8. Pas philōn kai poiōn pseudos (everyone who loves and makes falsehood) — the participial construction indicates characteristic love-of-lying, not occasional lapse; the disposition, not the act alone.
22.16:
ego Iēsous epempsa ton angelon mou martyrēsai hymin tauta epi tais ekklēsiais: Jesus identifies himself as the sender of the angel — the one who commissioned the entire revelation. Hymin (to you, plural) addresses the seven churches collectively. Martyrēsai (to testify, bear witness) — the entire Apocalypse is framed as divine testimony, not human speculation.

ego eimi hē rhiza kai to genos David ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos: two messianic identifications. Hē rhiza kai to genos David (the root and the offspring of David) — root = David's ancestor (Isa 11:10: the root of Jesse as the one the nations seek), offspring = David's descendant (Isa 11:1: a shoot from the stump of Jesse). Jesus is both ancestor and descendant: as divine Son he is the source of the Davidic line; as incarnate Son he is David's descendant. The title in Rev 5:5 (rhiza David) here expanded. Ho astēr ho lampros ho prōinos (the bright morning star): the morning star (Venus) appears just before dawn — a herald of the coming day. Num 24:17 (a star shall come out of Jacob) is the messianic star prophecy; here Christ applies it to himself as the harbinger of the eschatological dawn.
22.17:
kai to Pneuma kai hē Nymphē legousin Erchou: "The Spirit and the Bride say, Come!" Two voices united in the eschatological invitation: the Spirit (divine, the one who has been speaking to the churches throughout chs. 2–3: "what the Spirit says to the churches") and the Bride (the church, the new Jerusalem of ch. 21). Their unified cry Erchou (Come!) is addressed to Christ, the returning Lord.

kai ho akouōn eipatō Erchou, kai ho dipsōn erchasthō, ho thelōn labetō hydōr zōēs dōrean: the congregation joins (let the one who hears say Come), then the invitation opens to all who are thirsty (let the thirsty come, let the willing take the water of life as a gift). The final invitation of the Bible: dōrean (freely, as gift — same word as 21:6). This is the closing of John 7:37-38 (Jesus on the last day of the feast: "If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink") and Isa 55:1 (come to the waters, all you who are thirsty) extended to the entire canon's close.
22.18: martyrō egō panti tō akouonti tous logous tēs prophēteias tou bibliou toutou: martyrō (I testify, I solemnly warn — the word of authoritative witness). The warning against adding or subtracting echoes the Deuteronomy formula (Deut 4:2: "You shall not add to the word that I command you, nor take from it"; Deut 12:32) and the same formula applied to the book of Proverbs (Prov 30:6). Revelation here asserts canonical finality: this prophetic scroll is closed, complete, not to be supplemented or reduced. The gravity of the warning (prosthēsei ho theos ep auton tas plēgas — God will add to him the plagues written in this book) claims Torah-level authority for the Apocalypse.
22.19: kai ean tis aphelē apo tōn logōn tou bibliou tēs prophēteias tautēs, aphelei ho theos to meros autou apo tou xylou tēs zōēs kai ek tēs poleōs tēs hagias: the negative counterpart — taking away from the book results in losing one's share in the tree of life and the holy city. Meros (share, portion) of the tree of life is the inheritance language: the subtracter loses their inheritance in the new Jerusalem. The symmetry of addition and subtraction penalties mirrors the canonical seriousness: the integrity of the prophetic text is coextensive with the integrity of one's standing in the new creation. Textual variant: some MSS read xylou zōēs (tree of life) vs. bibliou zōēs (book of life); the former fits the new Jerusalem imagery of 22:2 and 22:14 better.
22.21: hē charis tou Kyriou Iēsou meta pantōn: "The grace of the Lord Jesus be with all." The Pauline epistolary benediction (cf. 1 Cor 16:23; Gal 6:18; Phil 4:23; 1 Thess 5:28; 2 Thess 3:18; Philem 25) applied to the Apocalypse — fitting, since Revelation is framed as a letter to the seven churches (1:4). Charis (grace) is the final word of the NT canon in the manuscript tradition. The entire Bible ends with grace: not command, not judgment, not prediction, but the free gift of God in Christ extending to "all." Pantōn (all) without qualifier — the grace of the Lord Jesus is offered without restriction.

2.1: tō angelō tēs en Ephesō ekklēsias grapson: 'To the messenger of the church in Ephesus write.' Each letter is addressed to the angelos (messenger/angel) of the church — this figure is either the church's guardian angel or, more likely given the context of human accountability, the leader/bishop who embodies and represents the community. Kratōn tous hepta asteras (holding the seven stars): kratōn is present participle — ongoing, continuous possession and governance. Peripatōn en mesō: walking among (present participle) — active, continuous pastoral presence.
2.2: oida ta erga sou kai ton kopon kai tēn hypomonēn sou: 'I know your deeds, your labor, and your patient endurance.' Oida (perfect, used as present): I have come to know and know now — Christ's knowledge is comprehensive and current. Kopos (labor) is more intense than general ergon (work): it implies toiling effort, wearying exertion. Ou dynē bastasai kakous: you are not able to tolerate evil people — bastasai (bear, carry) suggests they have tested false teachers and found them wanting, bearing the weight of that discernment.
2.3: kai hypomonēn echeis kai ebastasas dia to onoma mou kai ou kekopiakas: 'You have endurance and you have borne burdens for my name and have not grown weary.' The aorist ebastasas (you have borne/carried) describes completed past endurance; ou kekopiakas (you have not grown weary) is perfect — the state of not-having-wearied persists. The Ephesian church's endurance record is strong; the contrast with v. 4 is deliberate.
2.4: alla echō kata sou hoti tēn agapēn sou tēn prōtēn aphēkes: 'But I have this against you: you have abandoned your first love.' Aphēkes (aorist): you let go, you released, you abandoned — a deliberate action, not gradual drift. Tēn agapēn sou tēn prōtēn: the first/original love (prōtēn = first in time, prior love). The Ephesians' doctrinal vigilance (vv. 2-3) has come at the cost of the relational vitality that animated their original faith.
2.5: mnēmoneue pothen peptōkas kai metanoēson kai ta prōta erga poiēson: 'Remember from where you have fallen; repent; and do the first works.' Three aorist imperatives in sequence — remember, repent, do — forming a program of restoration. Peptōkas (perfect): you have fallen and remain in the fallen state. Kinēsō tēn lychnian sou ek tou topou autēs: I will move/remove your lampstand from its place — removal is the consequence of non-repentance; the church ceases to function as a light-bearer.
2.6: alla touto echeis hoti miseis ta erga tōn Nikolaitōn ha kagō misō: 'But you have this: you hate the practices of the Nicolaitans, which I also hate.' Miseis and misō (present tense) — strong active hatred, the same verb applied to both the church and to Christ; the community's revulsion at the Nicolaitan practice is validated by Christ's own attitude. The Nicolaitans are associated with the Balaam-teaching (v. 14-15): eating food sacrificed to idols and sexual immorality, possibly an antinomian group that accommodated to Greco-Roman social norms.
2.7: tō nikōnti dōsō autō phagein ek tou xylou tēs zōēs: 'To the one who overcomes I will give to eat from the tree of life.' Nikōnti (present participle, dative): to the one who is conquering — ongoing victory, not a single past act. The closing formula ho echōn ous akousatō ti to pneuma legei tais ekklēsiais appears at the end of each letter: the Spirit's address to one church is for all seven churches — the letter is simultaneously specific and universal.
2.8: ho prōtos kai ho eschatos, hos egeneto nekros kai ezēsen: 'the First and the Last, who died and came to life.' The YHWH-title from Isa 44:6 (first and last) is linked to the resurrection fact: the one who claims the divine identity-marker is the one who died and rose. Egeneto nekros kai ezēsen: became dead and lived — both aorists, two completed historical events. To the Smyrna church facing poverty and potential death, Christ presents his own death-and-life as the paradigm for what they face.
2.9: oida sou tēn thlipsin kai tēn ptōcheian, alla plousios ei: 'I know your affliction and your poverty — yet you are rich.' The contrast is direct: material poverty (ptōcheia, utter destitution) against spiritual wealth. Blasphēmian tōn legontōn Ioudaious einai heautous kai ouk eisin alla synagōgē tou satana: the slander from those claiming to be Jews but not being so. The phrase synagōgē tou satana (synagogue of Satan) is polemical: those who claim the covenant name but oppose the Messiah have become, functionally, the assembly of the adversary rather than the assembly of God.
2.10: mēden phobou ha melleis paschein: 'Fear nothing of what you are about to suffer.' Melleis paschein (about to suffer): imminent, foreseeable suffering. Deka hēmeras (ten days): a limited, defined period — alluding to the ten-day testing of Daniel and his companions (Dan 1:12-15); the number signals that the trial, however severe, is bounded by God's will. Stephanos tēs zōēs (crown of life): stephanos is the victor's laurel wreath, not the royal diadem (diadēma) — the image is athletic/competitive: those who compete faithfully receive the life-wreath.
2.11: ho nikōn ou mē adikēthē ek tou deuterou thanatou: 'The one who overcomes will not be harmed by the second death.' The double negative ou mē with subjunctive is the strongest Greek negation — absolute certainty of exemption.
