Ho deuteros thanatos (the second death) is Revelation's term for permanent separation from God after final judgment (20:6, 14; 21:8) — the lake of fire. The promise to faithful Smyrna: physical death cannot touch them where it ultimately matters.
2.12: ho echōn tēn rhomphian tēn distomon tēn oxeian: 'the one who has the sharp double-edged sword.' The self-identification of Christ to Pergamum uses the sword-from-mouth imagery of 1:16 — relevant because Pergamum was the capital of Roman Asia and the seat of Roman judicial authority; Christ presents himself as the ultimate judge whose word supersedes Rome's verdict. The Roman proconsul exercised ius gladii (right of the sword) — the power of execution. Christ's sword-of-the-mouth counters this claim.
2.13: ho thronos tou satana: 'the throne of Satan' — Pergamum was the location of the imperial cult in Asia, with a major altar to Augustus/Rome, and the great altar of Zeus (possibly the altar now reconstructed in Berlin). Antipas ho martys mou ho pistos: Antipas my faithful witness — the use of martys (witness) for one who died for the faith marks the semantic evolution toward 'martyr.' Ho katechoumenoi tēn didachēn Balaam: those holding to the teaching of Balaam — a syncretistic accommodation to imperial cult practices.
2.14: tēn didachēn Balaam, hos edidasken ton Balak balein skandalon enōpion tōn huiōn Israēl, phagein eidōlothyta kai porneusai: 'the teaching of Balaam, who taught Balak to put a stumbling block before the children of Israel, so that they might eat food sacrificed to idols and practice sexual immorality.' Skandalon (stumbling block/snare): the metaphor of a trap. Eidōlothyta (idol-food): food offered in sacrifice to Greco-Roman deities — eating it at temple banquets was socially expected in trade guilds; the refusal to eat separated Christians from normal social life. Porneusai: either literal sexual immorality at temple banquets or, metaphorically, spiritual unfaithfulness (syncretism as adultery against God).
2.15: homoiōs echeis kai sy tous kratountas tēn didachēn tōn Nikolaitōn: 'Likewise you also have some who hold the teaching of the Nicolaitans.' Homoiōs (likewise): the Nicolaitan teaching parallels the Balaam-teaching — they are variants of the same accommodation. The church at Pergamum tolerates these teachers; this is the opposite problem from Ephesus, who rightly detected and rejected false apostles (2:2) but lost their first love.
2.16: metanoēson oun; ei de mē, erchomai soi tachy kai polemēsō met autōn en tē rhomphaiai tou stomatos mou: 'Repent therefore; if not, I will come to you quickly and make war against them with the sword of my mouth.' The judgment falls on the false teachers (met autōn, against them), not the whole church — but the church's tolerance of them means Christ's coming-to-judge will affect the community. Erchomai tachy: coming quickly (the same phrase used of the parousia in 3:11; 22:7, 12, 20 — Christ's judicial coming and his final coming use the same language).
2.17: tō nikōnti dōsō autō tou manna tou kekrymmenom kai dōsō autō psēphon leukēn kai epi tēn psēphon onoma kainon gegrammenon: 'To the one who overcomes I will give some of the hidden manna, and a white stone with a new name written on it.' Manna kekrymmenon (hidden manna): Jewish tradition held that Jeremiah hid the golden jar of manna before the exile (2 Macc 2:4-8) for eschatological restoration; the promise reverses the idol-food temptation with true heavenly bread. Psēphos leukē (white stone): possibly an acquittal token (white = innocent in Greek judicial practice), or an invitation tessera for a banquet (social entry token). Onoma kainon: a new name known only to the recipient — personal, intimate identity restored by Christ.
2.18: ho huios tou theou, ho echōn tous ophthalmous autou hōs phloga pyros kai hoi podes autou homoioi chalkolibanō: 'the Son of God, whose eyes are like blazing fire and whose feet are like burnished bronze.' Ho huios tou theou is the only occurrence of this exact title in Revelation — addressing Thyatira, where the imperial cult identified the emperor's son with divine sonship. Christ's sonship displaces the emperor's claim. The fiery eyes (Dan 10:6) signal penetrating omniscience; the bronze feet signal unbreakable authority (the elements from the throne-room vision applied in a context of imminent judgment).
2.19: oida sou ta erga kai tēn agapēn kai tēn pistin kai tēn diakonian kai tēn hypomonēn sou: 'I know your deeds — your love, faith, service, and endurance.' Four virtues in a single catalogue: agapē (love), pistis (faith/faithfulness), diakonia (service/ministry), hypomonē (patient endurance). Thyatira's profile is the strongest of the churches in active virtue. Kai ta eschata pleiōna tōn prōtōn: your recent deeds surpass your earlier ones — the community is growing, not declining.
2.20: Ieza bel hē legousa heautēn prophētin: 'Jezebel, who calls herself a prophetess.' The name Jezebel (Ieza bel) is a pseudonym, alluding to the Phoenician queen of 1 Kgs 16-21 — the historical Jezebel led Ahab into Baal worship and killed the prophets of YHWH. The church at Thyatira tolerates a woman leader who falsely presents herself as a prophetess and promotes exactly the Balaam-pattern of idol-food and sexual immorality. Didaskalei kai plana tous emous doulous: she teaches and misleads my servants — planaō (mislead/deceive) is the word for the wandering of sheep and the deception of false teachers.
2.21: kai edōka autē chronon hina metanoēsē, kai ou thelei metanoēsai ek tēs porneias autēs: 'I gave her time to repent, and she does not want to repent of her sexual immorality.' Edōka chronon (I gave her time): the aorist marks a specific prior opportunity that was not taken. Ou thelei (she does not want/will not): not inability but refusal — the fault is volitional. The seriousness of the judgment that follows (vv. 22-23) is grounded in this refusal after extended opportunity.
2.22: ballō autēn eis klinēn kai tous moicheuntas met autēs eis thlipsin megalēn: 'I will throw her onto a bed of suffering, and those who commit adultery with her into great tribulation.' Klinē (bed): the judgment mirrors the sin — the bed of immorality becomes the bed of illness and suffering. Moicheuntas: committing adultery; both literal (sexual immorality at temple events) and metaphorical (spiritual adultery against God through idolatry). Ean mē metanoēsōsin: unless they repent (aorist subjunctive) — the conditional shows the judgment is not final until the possibility of repentance passes.
2.23: ego eimi ho eraunōn nephrous kai kardias: 'I am the one who searches kidneys and hearts.' Nephrous (kidneys): in Hebrew anthropology, the kidneys are the seat of deep emotion, conscience, and hidden motivation (Jer 17:10; Ps 7:9). The compound nephrous kai kardias (kidneys and hearts) is drawn from the OT divine-judge idiom — the one who searches the hidden depths of human motivation. This is a claim to omniscience about inner dispositions, not just outward behavior.
2.24: ta bathē tou satana: 'the deep things of Satan.' This is evidently the self-description of the false teacher's followers — they boasted of knowing the 'deep things' (possibly a gnosticizing claim to spiritual depth through transgression). The MKT and most modern translations read this as the false teachers' own language, used ironically by Christ. Ou ballō eph hymas allo baros: I will not put any other burden on you — the church members who remain faithful are not required to bear more than holding fast.
2.25: plēn ho echete kratēsate achri hou an hēxō: 'Only hold fast to what you have until I come.' Kratēsate (aorist imperative): seize and hold — a decisive act of grasping. Achri hou an hēxō: until I come — the parousia as the horizon that defines faithful endurance; one holds not forever but until the one who is coming arrives.
2.26: kai ho nikōn kai ho tērōn achri telous ta erga mou, dōsō autō exousian epi tōn ethnōn: 'To the one who overcomes and keeps my works to the end, I will give authority over the nations.' Two present participles (nikōn and tērōn) — overcoming and keeping are ongoing activities, not past accomplishments. Achri telous (to the end): perseverance through the full duration. Exousian epi tōn ethnōn: authority over the nations — sharing in Christ's messianic rule (Ps 2:8-9).
2.27: kai poimanei autous en rhabdō sidēra hōs ta skeuē ta keramika syntribetai: 'He will shepherd them with an iron rod and shatter them like clay vessels.' Poimanei (will shepherd/rule): from poimainō, the shepherd-verb — the iron-rod rule is described with the same word as pastoral care. The LXX of Ps 2:9 uses poimainō for 'rule/break,' preserving the ambiguity of the Hebrew raah. Kathōs kagō eilēpha para tou patros mou: just as I myself have received from my Father — the authority is derived, not autonomous.
2.28: dōsō autō ton astera ton prōinon: 'I will give him the morning star.' The morning star (astēr prōinos) is identified as Christ himself in 22:16 ('I am the root and descendant of David, the bright morning star'). The promise to give the morning star is thus a promise to give Christ himself — the fullest possible eschatological gift. The background may be Num 24:17 ('a star shall come out of Jacob'), the messianic star-prophecy that was applied to the coming ruler in multiple Jewish contexts.
2.29: ho echōn ous akousatō ti to pneuma legei tais ekklēsiais: 'Let anyone who has an ear hear what the Spirit says to the churches.' The closing formula appears in all seven letters. Its movement from singular (addressed to one church) to plural (tais ekklēsiais, the churches) signals that each letter addresses all seven — the community that hears one letter is called to hear all. The Spirit is the one speaking through the glorified Christ: Christology and pneumatology are inseparable in Revelation's vision-structure.

3.1: ho echōn ta hepta pneumata tou theou kai tous hepta asteras: 'the one who holds the seven Spirits of God and the seven stars.' The self-identification shifts from the description in ch. 1 — here the seven Spirits of God (the fullness of the Spirit) are in Christ's possession, linking his authority directly to the Spirit's discernment. Oida sou ta erga, hoti onoma echeis hoti zēis, kai nekros ei: I know your works — you have a reputation for being alive, but you are dead. The contrast is devastating: external reputation (onoma, name/reputation) versus actual spiritual state.
3.2: ginou grēgorōn kai stērison ta loipa ha emellon apothanein: 'Wake up, and strengthen what remains that is about to die.' Ginou grēgorōn: become watchful, a continuous state. Ta loipa (the rest, the remnant): what survives of the community's original vitality. Ou gar heurēka sou ta erga peplērōmena enōpion tou theou mou: I have not found your works complete/full before my God — peplērōmena (perfect passive participle): the works were started but not completed, and the incomplete state persists.
3.3: ean oun mē grēgorēsēs, hēxō hōs kleptēs, kai ou mē gnōs poian hōran hēxō epi se: 'If you will not watch, I will come like a thief, and you will not know at what hour I will come against you.' The thief-coming (hōs kleptēs) is explicitly an unexpected judgment-coming, not the final parousia — Christ comes in historical judgment on unfaithful churches within time. The same hōs kleptēs metaphor appears in Matt 24:43; 1 Thess 5:2; 2 Pet 3:10 for the parousia, but here it is specifically the imminent intra-historical judgment.
3.4: alla echeis oliga onomata en Sardesi ha ouk emolynon ta himatia autōn: 'Yet you have a few names in Sardis who have not soiled their garments.' Onomata (names): individuals; the few faithfully remaining are described by the Hebrew idiom of naming — they are known to God by name. Ouk emolynon ta himatia (did not defile their garments): the metaphor of soiled clothing for moral/spiritual compromise is common in Second Temple literature. Peripathsousin met emou en leukois: they will walk with me in white — the clean garments of the faithful are the eschatological reward.
3.5: ho nikōn houtōs peribaleitai en himatios leukois, kai ou mē exaleipsō to onoma autou ek tēs biblou tēs zōēs: 'The one who overcomes will be clothed in white garments, and I will never blot out his name from the book of life.' The double negative ou mē exaleipsō (I will absolutely never blot out): the strongest negative construction in Greek, providing the most emphatic assurance possible. Exaleipsō (blot out) recalls Moses's intercession (Exod 32:32-33) and Dan 12:1; the promise inverts the threat of erasure with the certainty of permanent inscription.
3.6: ho echōn ous akousatō ti to pneuma legei tais ekklēsiais: Standard closing formula. The repeated formula across all seven letters creates a liturgical rhythm — each letter ends identically, reinforcing that the Spirit's address to one community is for all.
3.7: ho hagios, ho alēthinos, ho echōn tēn kleida Dauid, ho anoigōn kai oudeis kleisei, kai kleiōn kai oudeis anoigei: 'the holy one, the true one, who has the key of David, who opens and no one shuts, who shuts and no one opens.' Hagios and alēthinos (holy and true) are divine attributes applied directly to Christ. The key-of-David language quotes Isa 22:22 (the investiture of Eliakim as royal steward). The two present participles anoigōn and kleiōn in an antithetical pair with oudeis (no one) twice: absolute, unqualified authority over access to the divine household.
3.8: idou dedōka enōpion sou thyran ēnoigmenēn, hēn oudeis dynatai kleisai autēn: 'Behold, I have placed before you an open door that no one is able to shut.' Dedōka (perfect): I have given and it stands given — the open door is an accomplished and ongoing reality. Ēnoigmenēn (perfect passive participle): the door has been opened and remains open. Dynamin mikran (little power/strength): Philadelphia is weak by worldly measures; the open door is given to the weak church, not the strong one.
3.9: idou didō ek tēs synagōgēs tou satana tōn legontōn heautous Ioudaious einai kai ouk eisin alla pseudontai: 'Behold, I will make those of the synagogue of Satan who say they are Jews and are not, but lie.' The future poiēsō autous hina hēxousin kai proskyneousin enōpion tōn podōn sou (I will make them come and bow down at your feet): this is an inversion of the Isaianic promises where nations bow before restored Israel (Isa 49:23; 60:14); the persecutors will acknowledge the vindication of the oppressed community. Kagō ēgapēsa se: that I have loved you (aorist) — the vindication proves the prior love.
3.10: hoti etērēsas ton logon tēs hypomonēs mou, kagō se tērēsō ek tēs hōras tou peirasmou: 'Because you have kept the word of my patient endurance, I will also keep you from the hour of trial.' The symmetry of keeping: the community kept Christ's word; Christ keeps the community. Tēs horas tou peirasmou (the hour of trial): possibly a specific future tribulation period or more generally the eschatological pressure. Ek tēs hōras (from the hour): the preposition ek (out from) is debated — does it mean preservation through the trial or removal before it begins?
3.11: erchomai tachy; kratei ho echeis, hina mēdeis labē ton stephanon sou: 'I am coming quickly. Hold fast what you have, so that no one may seize your crown.' Erchomai tachy: present-tense coming, imminent arrival. Kratei (aorist imperative): seize/hold firm — decisive act of retention. Ton stephanon sou: your crown — the stephanos (victor's wreath) can be seized by another if you do not persevere; crowns in Revelation are conditionally held.
3.12: ho nikōn, poiēsō auton stylon en tō naō tou theou mou: 'The one who overcomes, I will make him a pillar in the temple of my God.' Stylon (pillar): structural element, immovable, permanent — contrasts with Philadelphia's earthquake vulnerability (major earthquakes struck the region ca. 17 CE; pillars that stood were the prize of survivors). Exō ouk exeleusetai eti: he will go out no more — the conqueror's place in the divine temple is permanent, unlike a city that might be evacuated for safety. The threefold writing on the pillar (name of God, name of the city, Christ's new name) inscribes triple identity on the victor.
3.14: ho Amēn, ho martys ho pistos kai alēthinos, hē archē tēs ktiseōs tou theou: 'the Amen, the faithful and true witness, the beginning of God's creation.' Ho Amēn as a title: drawn from Isa 65:16 (elohei amen, God of the Amen/God of truth). Archē tēs ktiseōs (the beginning of creation): this is the most debated phrase in the seven letters. Archē can mean first-in-time (as Arians argued: the first created being) or source/ruler (as orthodox interpreters argued, citing Col 1:15-18's firstborn-of-creation language meaning sovereign over creation). The context — presenting Christ as the one who holds the Seven Spirits and seven stars, who is divine witness and divine Amen — supports the ruler/source reading.
3.15: oida sou ta erga, hoti oute psychros ei oute zestos; ophelon psychros ēs ē zestos: 'I know your works: you are neither cold nor hot; would that you were cold or hot.' The commercial metaphor uses temperature to describe spiritual vitality. Cold water (from Colossae) and hot water (from Hierapolis) both had uses; Laodicea's lukewarm water supply (from mineral springs) was medically unpleasant. The lukewarm church has no distinctiveness — neither refreshing nor curative, merely tepid.
3.16: houtōs, hoti chlioros ei kai oute zestos oute psychros, mellō se emesai ek tou stomatos mou: 'So, because you are lukewarm, and neither hot nor cold, I will spit you out of my mouth.' Emesai (vomit out): strong visceral imagery — the reaction of a body to something that causes nausea. Mellō (I am about to): the expulsion is imminent, not yet enacted — the conditional structure of vv. 19-20 implies repentance can avert it. The severity of the language to the wealthiest of the seven churches is deliberate.
3.17: hoti legeis hoti plousios eimi kai peploutēka kai ouden chreian echō: 'Because you say, I am rich and I have prospered and I need nothing.' Plousios (rich) and peploutēka (I have grown rich, perfect) — Laodicea was one of the wealthiest cities in Asia, known for banking, textile manufacture, and medicine. The perfect tense peploutēka expresses the settled self-confidence of accumulated wealth. The five-fold description of their actual state — wretched, pitiable, poor, blind, naked — inverts the five-fold prosperity they claim.
3.18: symbouleuō soi agorasai par emou chrysion pepyrōmenon ek pyros hina ploutēsēs: 'I counsel you to buy from me gold refined by fire, so that you may be rich.' The commercial metaphor uses marketplace language against Laodicea's commercial pride — they are to buy from Christ. Chrysion pepyrōmenon ek pyros (gold refined/purified by fire): true spiritual wealth earned through testing. Kollyrion (eye salve): Laodicea was famous for Phrygian powder used as a medical eye treatment; the city's pharmacological reputation becomes the ironic vehicle for Christ's offer of genuine spiritual sight.
3.19: egō hosous ean philō elegchō kai paideuō; zēleue oun kai metanoēson: 'Those whom I love, I rebuke and discipline. So be zealous and repent.' Elegchō (rebuke/convict) and paideuō (discipline/train): both terms carry the flavor of parental correction (Prov 3:12; Heb 12:6). The severity of the Laodicea letter is thus reframed as love's most direct form. Zēleue (aorist imperative): be zealous, be fired up — the antidote to lukewarmness is not moderation but ignition.
3.20: idou hestēka epi tēn thyran kai krouō; ean tis akousē tēs phōnēs mou kai anoixē tēn thyran, eiseleusomai pros auton kai deipnēsō met autou kai autos met emou: 'Behold, I have been standing at the door and knocking; if anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in and eat with him, and he with me.' Hestēka (perfect): I have been standing and stand — ongoing presence, not a momentary visit. Krouō (present): continuous knocking. The deipnon (evening meal, the main meal of the day) implies covenant fellowship — shared table in antiquity was the most intimate expression of relationship and commitment. Notably this promise is to individuals within the church (tis, anyone), not the community as a whole; Laodicea is so far gone that Christ knocks for any individual willing to open.
3.21: ho nikōn, dōsō autō kathisai met emou en tō thronō mou, hōs kagō enikekon kai ekathisa meta tou patros mou en tō thronō autou: 'The one who overcomes, I will grant him to sit with me on my throne, as I also overcame and sat down with my Father on his throne.' The highest promise in the seven letters: sharing Christ's throne. Enikekon (perfect): I have conquered and remain in the state of having conquered — the perfect here captures the ongoing ruling status of the risen Christ at the Father's right hand (Ps 110:1; Heb 1:3). The conqueror participates in what Christ has already accomplished: the promise extends Christ's own victory-throne to the faithful community.

6.1: ēnoixen (aorist) — the Lamb's opening of each seal is a distinct completed act, not a continuous process. The Lamb (arnion) is consistently the grammatical subject of the seal-openings throughout ch6, placing his agency at the center of the judgment sequence. The living creature's command "Come!" (erchou) is addressed to the horsemen, not to John — imperative, summoning the next judgment into the historical arena.
6.2: stephanos — a victor's laurel wreath (as in athletic contests), not diadema (the royal crown worn by sovereigns). The distinction matters: the white rider's crown is a conqueror's prize, earned in battle, not sovereign authority by nature. toxon (bow) — no arrow is mentioned; the bow without an arrow may signal the threat of conquest without its immediate execution. nikōn kai hina nikēsē — a present participle ("conquering") followed by an aorist purpose-clause ("in order that he might conquer"): ongoing conquest aimed at total victory.
6.3: The seal-opening formula repeats six times in ch6 with minor variations (hote ēnoixen, "when he opened"). The formulaic repetition creates a judicial rhythm: the Lamb presides, the living creatures summon, the rider executes. The repetition is itself a rhetorical signal of divine control over the sequence — history unfolds in measured acts, not chaos.
6.4: pyrros (fiery red) — the color of blood and fire; Zech 1:8 has a red horse among the patrol horses. machaira — the short sword associated with assassination and civil violence, as opposed to rhomphaia (the large sword of sovereign/divine judgment in Rev 1:16; 2:12). The distinction: the second rider wields the instrument of internal strife and murder, not conquest in formal warfare. sphaxōsin (slay one another) — the reflexive-intensive use: mutual slaughter, not external invasion.
6.5: zygos (pair of scales) — the measurement instrument for rationing; the black horse rider measures out grain, signaling not absolute famine but severe scarcity. Black (melas) in Lam 5:10 is the color of skin blackened by famine. The scales activate the economic register of the judgment: controlled distribution under crisis conditions.
6.6: choinix sitou dēnariou — a choinix of wheat (roughly 1 liter, a day's ration for one person) costs a dēnarion (a day's wage, Matt 20:2). Normal prices in antiquity were 8-12 choinikes per dēnarion, so this represents roughly an 8-12x price increase — severe inflation but not total starvation. mē adikēsēs (do not harm — aorist subjunctive of prohibition) — the oil and wine (luxury goods) are exempted, suggesting the judgment falls hardest on those dependent on grain (the poor) while the wealthy continue relatively unaffected.
6.7: The fourth seal formula is identical to the second and third. John carefully maintains the structural symmetry: the progression from white (conquest) → red (civil war) → black (famine) → pale (death) traces an escalating logic of historical judgment.
6.8: chloros — literally green, the sickly pale color of disease and decay; the only use of chloros for a horse; elsewhere it describes vegetation (grass, 8:7; 9:4). The pallor of a corpse. Thanatos (Death personified) and Hadēs (the underworld realm) are paired — Death kills, Hades receives. rhomphaia, limos, thanatos, thēria (sword, famine, pestilence/death, wild beasts) — the four agents echo Ezek 14:21 exactly (YHWH's four severe judgments on Jerusalem: sword, famine, wild beasts, plague). John alludes to the Ezekiel formula with one substitution.
6.9: hypokatō tou thysiastēriou — "under the altar." In the Mosaic sacrificial system, the blood of the burnt offering was poured at the base (hypo) of the altar (Lev 4:7, 18). The martyrs are positioned as sacrificial blood — their deaths are the ultimate offering, their location at the altar's base making them liturgically present as sacrifices. hesphasmenōn (perfect passive participle of sphazō — slaughtered/killed; the same verb as "the Lamb as though slaughtered" in 5:6) — the martyrs share the vocabulary of the Lamb's own death.
6.10: despota — not Kyrie (covenant-Lord) but despotēs (absolute owner/master), used elsewhere for God in Lk 2:29; Acts 4:24; 2 Pet 2:1; Jude 4. The title of absolute sovereignty is invoked specifically in the context of the prayer for justice — the martyr-souls appeal to the one who owns them completely. ekdikeis (present tense: do you judge and avenge?) — an impatient present, not a future hope: "are you not judging?" tous katoikountas epi tēs gēs — "those who dwell on the earth" becomes Revelation's technical phrase for the world-system opposed to God (used 11 more times).
6.11: stolē leukē — a white robe (stolē, the long robe worn by priests and dignitaries; cf. 7:9,13,14). The gift of the robe signals vindication and priestly dignity. plērōthōsin (aorist passive subjunctive of plēroō) — "until the number might be completed/fulfilled." The martyrs' death-quota must be filled; this is not passive resignation but the framework that interprets ongoing persecution as purposive, not random. The present tense of the martyrdom-mandate ("those about to be killed") implies ongoing persecution at the time of writing.
6.12: seismos megas (great earthquake) — seismic language for divine theophany runs from Exod 19:18 through Isa 29:6 and Joel 2:10 into Revelation's judgment vocabulary. sakkos trichinos (sackcloth made of hair) — the mourning garment; the blackening of the sun in mourning-cloth recalls Isa 50:3 ("I clothe the sky with darkness and make sackcloth its covering"). The cosmic signs of vv12-14 draw on the OT "Day of YHWH" tradition.
6.13: hōs sykē ballei (as a fig tree drops) — the fig tree's unripe winter fruit (olynthous, the late-hanging figs that fall in a winter gale) is the specific image. This precise detail (unripe figs falling off-season in a storm) is from Isa 34:4 (the fall of Edom's host of heaven). The Greek of Revelation reproduces the Isaianic imagery with unusual botanical specificity.
6.14: apechōristhē hōs biblion helissomenon — the sky split apart like a scroll being rolled up; Isa 34:4 again: "the sky will be rolled up like a scroll." Helissomenon (present passive participle — being rolled up, in process) gives the visual of a scroll being wound shut at both ends. ekinēthēsan (aorist passive) — every mountain and island was moved from its place; the world-map is being disassembled.
6.15: The sevenfold list of humanity (kings, great ones, generals, rich, powerful, every slave, every free person) is a merism for all of human social hierarchy — from top to bottom, no one is exempt. The syntax places the powerful first and the powerless last, then collapses the distinction: "every slave and every free person" — the great leveling of the Day of Judgment.
6.16: orgē tou arniou — "the wrath of the Lamb" — the most theologically paradoxical phrase in Revelation. The Lamb (arnion: diminutive, a small lamb) is the symbol of innocent meekness and sacrificial death; yet his orgē (wrath) is the most terrifying prospect facing the world-system's inhabitants. The phrase is unique; nowhere else in the NT is divine wrath attributed specifically to Christ as the Lamb.
6.17: ēlthen (aorist: has come, arrived) — not "is coming" but "has arrived." The great day of wrath is treated as already present, not still future. tis dynatai stathēnai — "who is able to stand?" — an OT formula for surviving divine judgment (Ps 76:7; Mal 3:2: "who can endure the day of his coming? who can stand when he appears?"). The rhetorical question anticipates ch7's answer: the sealed servants of God.

7.1: tessaras anemous — the four winds from the four corners; Zech 6:5 (the four spirits/winds of heaven) and Dan 7:2 (the four winds stirring up the sea from which the beasts arise). The holding back of the four winds is a temporary restraint on cosmic judgment — the angels hold history in suspension before the sealing.
7.2: sphragida Theou zōntos — the seal of the living God. Sphragis (seal/signet) marks ownership and guarantees authenticity; in Ezek 9:4, YHWH marks the foreheads of those who mourn over Jerusalem before the destroying angels go through. Rev 7 is the eschatological counterpart: God's own mark of ownership protects from the coming judgment. anatolē hēliou (rising of the sun, the east) — the direction associated with the returning glory of God (Ezek 43:2; 44:1-2).
7.3: sphragisōmen (aorist subjunctive: until we might seal) — the sealing is a punctiliar act to be completed before harm is permitted. The aorist subjunctive of purpose in a temporal clause stresses the completion of the sealing before the wind-release. epi tōn metōpōn autōn — on their foreheads — Exod 13:9 places the Passover sign as a reminder "on your forehead"; Rev places the ownership-seal on the same location, linking Passover protection with the new-Exodus sealing.
7.4: hekaton tesserakonta tessares chiliades (144,000) — the number is 12 × 12 × 1,000: the arithmetic of symbolic completeness. Twelve is the number of the tribes of Israel (the covenant people); twelve squared (the people in their fullness) multiplied by a thousand (the number of divine abundance). The tribal list that follows (vv5-8) deviates from standard OT lists in significant ways — Judah is first (not Reuben the firstborn); Dan is omitted; Manasseh replaces it — suggesting symbolic rather than literal enumeration.
7.5: Judah is listed first (not Reuben, the firstborn), because the Lamb who opens the seals is from Judah (5:5). The tribal list is reordered around the Lion of Judah: the tribe that produced the Messiah heads the eschatological register. The twelve-thousand-per-tribe structure is perfectly uniform — no tribe has more or fewer — signaling an idealized, symbolic completeness.
7.6: The tribal enumeration (vv5-8) proceeds through the list with mechanical regularity. Dan is absent and Manasseh is added (both son and father of Joseph appear). Early patristic tradition suggested Dan was omitted because Antichrist would come from Dan (Gen 49:17 — "Dan will be a serpent by the roadside"); whether that is the author's intent is uncertain. The completeness of twelve tribes is maintained by the substitution.
7.7: Simeon and Levi — the violent brothers of Gen 34 who were scattered (Gen 49:5-7) — appear in the sealing list. Jacob's curse on them is not the final word: the eschatological community includes even those whose ancestors were under judgment. The tribal list is redemptive rather than merely genealogical.
7.8: The list closes with Benjamin. The twelve tribes together constitute the new Israel — not a literal census but a symbolic statement of the complete covenantal community that will survive the judgment. The sealing answers the question of v17 ("who is able to stand?"): those on whom God's own seal has been placed.
7.9: ochlus polys hon arithmēsai auton oudeis ēdynato — "a great multitude which no one was able to count." This directly contrasts with the 144,000 who were precisely numbered; the two visions are likely the same community seen from different angles — the finite covenant-structure (144,000) and the infinite eschatological reality (innumerable multitude). stolas leukas (white robes) and phoinikes (palm branches) — the palm branches recall the Feast of Booths (Lev 23:40; Neh 8:15) and the triumphal entry (John 12:13), both moments of covenant celebration.
7.10: hē sōtēria tō Theō hēmōn — "salvation belongs to our God." The dative of possession: salvation is not a human achievement but the property of God, who loans it to his people. The parallel "and to the Lamb" makes salvation jointly attributed to God and the Lamb — the same co-attribution seen throughout the doxologies of Revelation (cf. 5:13).
7.11: epesan epi ta prosōpa autōn (fell on their faces) — the standard LXX gesture of prostration before the divine Presence (Ezek 1:28; 3:23; 43:3; 44:4). In Revelation, the twenty-four elders fall prostrate repeatedly (4:10; 5:8,14; 7:11; 11:16; 19:4). The accumulated weight of repetition establishes the posture of creation before the Creator as the fundamental mode of heavenly existence.
7.12: The sevenfold doxology (eulogia, doxa, sophia, eucharistia, timē, dynamis, ischys: blessing, glory, wisdom, thanksgiving, honor, power, might) is framed by matching Amēn. The seven attributes in doxological form recall the throne-room hymns of chs 4-5. The addition of eucharistia (thanksgiving) to the list distinguishes this doxology — the redeemed multitude adds gratitude to the creation's praise.
7.13: apokritheis ... eipen — one of the elders takes the role of interpretive guide, addressing John with a rhetorical question designed to draw out the answer the elder will then supply. This Socratic pedagogical technique (the leader asks, the pupil professes ignorance, the leader explains) is found in apocalyptic literature (Dan 7:16; 4 Ezra 4:52-5:13). John's "Sir, you know" (Kyrie mou, sy oidas) is the appropriate deferential response.
7.14: hoi erchomenoi ek tēs thlipseōs tēs megalēs — "those coming out of the great tribulation" — present participle: they are in the process of emerging, not yet finished. eplynan tas stolas autōn (aorist: they washed their robes) and eleukanan (aorist: made them white) — both completed acts. The paradox: made white in blood. Haimati (blood, dative of means/instrument) — the blood of the Lamb is the cleansing agent that reverses the normal function of blood (which stains). The syntax forces the paradox: "they made them white in blood."
7.15: latreuousin autō hēmeras kai nyktos — "they serve him day and night" — the eschatological reversal of the present situation (where God's servants are persecuted and killed). Latreuō is the technical term for priestly/liturgical service (Heb 9:9; 10:2; 13:10). The redeemed serve as priests in the heavenly temple. skēnōsei ep' autous — "will spread his tent over them" (future); the verb skēnoō echoes both the wilderness Tabernacle and John 1:14 (the Word tabernacled among us); here it is the permanent eschatological dwelling.
7.16: ou peinasousin eti oude dipsēsousin eti — future tenses (both negated by eti, "any longer/still") describing permanent reversal. The "no longer" structure signals that the present experience is hunger and thirst — these are martyrs who suffered real deprivation. The promise reverses their specific earthly experience. ou mē pesē ep' autous ho hēlios — Isa 49:10 ("they will not hunger or thirst; neither the scorching wind nor the sun will beat on them") is quoted almost verbatim; the servant's promise is fulfilled in the great multitude.
7.17: to arnion ... poimanei autous — the Lamb will shepherd them (future poimainō). The word for shepherd the flock is applied to the Lamb — the most compressed theological paradox in the chapter: the sacrificed animal is the shepherd. Behind this stands Ezek 34:23 (David my servant will shepherd them) and Ps 23 (YHWH my shepherd). zōēs (of life) modifying "springs" (not "rivers of life" but pēgas hydatōn zōēs, springs of living water) — echoes Jer 2:13 (YHWH as the spring of living water) and John 4:14; 7:38. exaleipsei ... pan dakryon (will wipe away every tear) — Isa 25:8; the eschatological comfort of YHWH personally wiping tears.

8.1: sigē en tō ouranō hōs hēmimōrion — "silence in heaven for about half an hour." The silence is without precedent in Revelation's preceding heavenly scenes, which have been filled with the voices of living creatures, elders, and myriads of angels. The silence is liturgical — it parallels the practice of silence before solemn pronouncements in Jewish and later Christian worship (Hab 2:20: "YHWH is in his holy temple; let all the earth be silent before him"; Zeph 1:7; Zech 2:13). The "half an hour" is one of Revelation's rare time-specifications, marking the solemnity of what follows.
8.2: hoi hepta angeloi hoi enōpion tou Theou — "the seven angels who stand before God." These are the archangels of Jewish tradition (1 Enoch 20:1-8 names Uriel, Raphael, Raguel, Michael, Saraqael, Gabriel, and Remiel as the seven holy angels "who present the prayers of the saints and enter into the presence of the glory of the Holy One"). The trumpets given to them are judicial instruments — in OT warfare the trumpets were blown before battle (Num 10:9; Josh 6:4-5).
8.3: libano pollo — "a large amount of incense." The incense altar before the throne corresponds to the golden altar of incense in the Tabernacle and Temple, positioned before the Holy of Holies. tais proseuchais tōn hagiōn pantōn — "with the prayers of all the saints." The incense is not merely liturgical decoration but is given to be offered "with" the prayers — the angels' offering amplifies and presents the accumulated prayers of God's people. The grammar makes the incense and prayers co-offerings before God.
8.4: anebē ho kapnos tōn thymiamatōn — "the smoke of the incense went up" — the direction is key: prayers ascend to God. The prayers of the saints have been accumulating (cf. 6:9-11: the martyr-souls crying out, "how long?"); now they ascend as incense, setting in motion the judgment-sequence of the trumpets. The passive structure signals divine reception: God receives the prayers and acts in response.
8.5: eilēphen ton libanōton kai egemisen auton ek tou pyros tou thysiastēriou — the same censer that held the incense-with-prayers is now filled with fire from the altar and hurled down. The movement is exact: prayers ascend, judgment-fire descends. brontai kai phōnai kai astrapai kai seismos (thunder, rumblings, lightning, earthquake) — the Sinai theophany-formula (Exod 19:16-18), which recurs at each of Revelation's great climaxes (11:19; 16:18).
8.6: The seven angels prepare (ētoimasan, aorist: completed preparation) to sound. The verb is not "picked up" but "prepared" — a deliberate act of readiness before execution. The symmetry with ch7 (sealing before trumpets) is structural: the servants of God are sealed before the judgment-trumpets sound, ensuring the protection of 7:3 is in place.
8.7: chalaza kai pyr memigmena en haimati — "hail and fire mixed with blood." The hail recalls Exod 9:22-26 (seventh Egyptian plague); the fire recalls Joel 2:30 ("I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth: blood and fire and columns of smoke"); the blood adds a third element absent from Exod 9. The mixture creates a compound plague-sign that draws on multiple OT registers simultaneously. to triton (a third) — the partial judgment (one-third affected) distinguishes the trumpets from the bowls (total judgment); the scale is warning-not-yet-final.
8.8: hōs oros mega pyrī kaiomenon — "something like a great mountain blazing with fire was thrown into the sea." The simile-hedge (hōs, like) is typical of John's visionary caution: he does not claim to describe the thing itself but its appearance. The blazing mountain recalls Jer 51:25 ("Behold I am against you, O destroying mountain... I will make you a burned-out mountain"). egeneto eis haima — became blood (not "turned blood-red" but ontological transformation, as in the Exodus plague).
8.9: ta echonta psychas (those having souls/lives) — the phrase emphasizes that these are living creatures, not merely organisms; the destruction is of ensouled life, not just biological matter. diephtharesan (aorist passive: were destroyed/corrupted) — the same root as phtheirō (to corrupt, destroy) used for the corruption of the false teachers throughout the epistles. The judgment-vocabulary links the moral and the cosmic.
8.10: astēr megas kaiomenos hōs lampas — "a great star blazing like a torch." The torch-simile (lampas, not lychnia) suggests brilliant, streaming fire rather than a contained flame. epesan ek tou ouranou — "fell from heaven" (aorist); the falling star echoes Isa 14:12 ("How you have fallen from heaven, morning star, son of the dawn") and Luke 10:18 ("I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven"). The connection to fallen angelic power is interpretively possible but not made explicit by the text.
8.11: Apsinthos — wormwood (Artemisia absinthium), an extremely bitter plant; in the OT, wormwood (la'anah) symbolizes divine judgment through bitter experience (Jer 9:15: "I will feed this people with wormwood and give them bitter water to drink"; Lam 3:15,19; Prov 5:4). The name "Wormwood" given to the star is not found in the OT; John creates the designation to match the effect. eplēthysan (aorist passive: were made bitter) — the waters become their punishment.
8.12: eplēgē to triton tou hēliou — "a third of the sun was struck." The passive eplēgē (was struck) uses divine-passive construction; the agent is unstated. The "third" (triton) motif runs through the trumpet sequence — partial rather than total disruption, distinguishing the trumpets from the final bowl-judgments. The darkening of sun, moon, and stars recalls the Egyptian darkness-plague (Exod 10:21) and Joel 2:10 and Amos 8:9 ("I will make the sun go down at noon").
8.13: aetos petomenon en mesouranēmati — "an eagle flying in midheaven." The eagle as a prophetic messenger recalls Deut 28:49 (the eagle as judgment from afar) and Hos 8:1 ("an eagle over the house of the LORD"). ouai ouai ouai — the triple woe, corresponding to the three remaining trumpets. The triplication of the woe-formula is intensification by repetition, a Hebraic rhetorical device. The transition at v13 divides the seven trumpets into two groups: 1-4 (cosmic) and 5-7 (demonic/historical), with the woe-eagle marking the break.

9.1: astēra ek tou ouranou peptōkota eis tēn gēn — "a star that had fallen from heaven to earth." The perfect participle peptōkota (already fallen, in a state of having fallen) distinguishes this from the falling of 8:10 (aorist, the moment of falling). This star is already in its fallen state before John sees it — a being of previously higher status now consigned to the lower realm. The key to the shaft of the Abyss is given to him: the fallen one is granted authority over the prison-realm, not as a possession but as a temporary grant.
9.2: ho phreatos tēs abyssou — "the shaft of the Abyss" (literally: the well/pit of the bottomless pit). Abyssos in LXX translates tehōm, the primordial deep of Gen 1:2 — the undifferentiated chaos before creation. In Jewish apocalyptic it becomes the prison of rebellious spirits (1 Enoch 18:11-16; Jude 6: "chains of darkness"). The smoke rising from the opened Abyss darkens the sun — the opened chaos-realm corrupts the created order from below.
9.3: akrides (locusts) — in the OT, the locust-plague is a recurring image of divine judgment (Exod 10:12-15; Joel 1-2; Amos 7:1). Joel 1:6 describes the locust-army with lion's teeth, and Joel 2:4-5 describes them as horses rushing into battle — language John will reprise in vv7-9. The scorpion-power grants them the ability to inflict pain without killing — a new capacity beyond the historical locust's grain-destruction.
9.4: mē adikēsōsin — "they were commanded not to harm" (aorist subjunctive of prohibition). The specific exclusions (grass, green plants, trees) invert the normal locust pattern — the historical locusts destroy vegetation, these locusts target only unprotected people. The inversion signals that this is not literal entomology but a symbolic description of a spiritual/historical force. sphragida tou Theou (the seal of God) — the protection established in ch7 holds: those sealed by God are exempt from the Abyss-locusts.
9.5: basanismos ... hōs basanismos skorpiou — "torment ... like the torment of a scorpion." The scorpion sting produces intense pain but rarely death; the five months of torment without death is a judgment of prolonged suffering. mēnas pente (five months) — the natural lifespan of a locust (May to September, approximately five months); the duration is calibrated to the creature's biology but the function is symbolic.
9.6: zētēsousin ... thanaton kai ou mē heurōsin — "they will seek death and will by no means find it" (ou mē + future is the strongest Greek negation). The reversal of death's availability — normally an ever-present option — is itself a form of judgment. Compare Job 3:21 (those who long for death that does not come) and Jer 8:3 (death preferred to life). The inability to die transforms what might be merciful release into perpetual torment.
9.7: hōs hippoi hētoimasmenoi eis polemon — "like horses prepared for battle." The extended locust-description in vv7-10 is a series of simile-stacks (hōs, like): crowns like gold, faces like humans, hair like women's, teeth like lions'. John accumulates similes to describe what cannot be directly named — a composite creature from the Abyss that defies normal taxonomic categories. The horse-armor (Joel 2:4-5) is the base, elaborated beyond anything in the OT source.
9.8: trichas hōs trichas gynaikōn — "hair like women's hair." This detail is not in Joel's locust-army; it is John's addition. The significance is debated: some suggest the long, flowing mane of horses; others see it as an additional element of the uncanny, making the locust-creatures both masculine (battle-ready) and feminine (long hair) — a gender-crossing that signals their category-transgressing nature from the Abyss. odonas hōs leontos — Joel 1:6 explicitly gives the locust "teeth of a lion."
9.9: phōnē tōn pterygōn autōn hōs phōnē harmatōn hippōn pollōn trechontōn eis polemon — "the sound of their wings was like the sound of chariots, of many horses rushing into battle." Joel 2:5 ("like the noise of chariots they leap over the mountaintops, like the crackling of fire consuming stubble, like a powerful army drawn up for battle"). John's auditory simile draws directly from Joel's battle-army of locusts, but the addition of chariot-noise multiplies the scale.
9.10: hai ourai autōn homoiai skorpiois — "their tails were like scorpions." The double-ended threat (mouths that wound, tails that sting) makes the creature dangerous in all directions. to adikēsai tous anthrōpous mēnas pente — "to harm people for five months": the same duration restated, now attributed specifically to the tails. The repetition of the five-month limit emphasizes the constraint placed on even demonic power — judgment operates within defined limits.
9.11: Abaddōn ... Apollyōn — the Hebrew name given first (Abaddon, from abad = to perish/be destroyed; appearing in Job 26:6; 28:22; 31:12; Ps 88:11; Prov 15:11 as the realm of destruction beneath Sheol), then the Greek equivalent (Apollyon, the Destroyer). The naming of the king of the Abyss with this word gives a personal identity to the realm of destruction — Abaddon is no longer just a place but a person/power. The Greek name Apollyōn also phonetically evokes Apollo, the Greek sun-god whose symbol was the locust; some scholars read an anti-imperial polemic against the Neronian or Domitian cult here.
9.12: hē ouai hē mia apēlthen — "the first woe has passed" — the perfect tense of departure (apēlthen, aorist actually, indicating completed departure). The structural marker divides the trumpet sequence and prepares for the sixth trumpet's even greater judgment. The announcement that "two more woes are still coming" creates narrative tension and eschatological urgency.
9.13: phōnēn mian ek tōn tessarōn keratōn tou thysiast ēriou tou chrysou — "a voice from the four horns of the golden altar." The horns of the altar were the points to which the blood of the sin offering was applied (Lev 4:7,18,25,30; Exod 29:12); they were also grasped by those seeking sanctuary (1 Kgs 1:50-51; 2:28). The voice from the altar-horns links the judgment of the sixth trumpet to the altar-prayers of 8:3-5 and the martyr-souls of 6:9-10: justice proceeds from the place of sacrifice and prayer.
9.14: luson tous tessaras angelous tous dedemenous epi tō potamō tō megalō Euphratē — "release the four angels bound at the great river Euphrates." The Euphrates is the eastern boundary of the promised land (Gen 15:18) and the symbolic boundary between the covenant people and the threatening empires of Assyria and Babylon. The four bound angels (bound = dedemenous, perfect passive participle, in a lasting state of restraint) are released at the specific moment designated — the precise timing of v15.
9.15: hoi hētoimasmenoi eis tēn hōran kai hēmeran kai mēna kai eniauton — "prepared for the hour, day, month, and year." The fourfold time-specification (hour, day, month, year) indicates absolute divine precision: this judgment was ordained for this exact moment in the cosmic calendar. Hētoimasmenoi (perfect passive participle: having been prepared) — the preparation was completed in the past; the angels have been waiting in their bound state for this moment to arrive.
9.16: dyo myriades myriadōn — "two myriads of myriads" = 200,000,000 (two hundred million). Myrias is ten thousand, the largest numeral in Greek; myriad of myriads is the limit of numerical expression. The army is beyond counting-in-practice, echoing the innumerable host of 5:11 but now for judgment rather than worship. ēkousa ton arithmon autōn — "I heard their number" (not counted it) — John receives the numerical revelation aurally.
9.17: houtōs eidon tous hippous en tē horasei kai tous kathēmenous ep' autōn — "this is how I saw the horses in my vision." The vision-qualifier (en tē horasei, in the vision) maintains the visionary frame around the description. purinos kai hyakinthinos kai theiōdeis — fire-colored, dark blue (hyakinthinos, the color of the hyacinth flower, or possibly smoke-dark), and sulfur-yellow: the three colors of fire, smoke, and sulfur that are named in v18 as the three plagues. The horses' appearance pre-figures the judgment they carry.
9.18: ek tōn triōn plēgōn toutōn apektanthē to triton tōn anthrōpōn — "by these three plagues a third of humanity was killed." The three plagues (fire, smoke, sulfur — pyr, kapnos, theion) are also the substances associated with the destruction of Sodom (Gen 19:24,28: fire and sulfur; smoke rising like smoke from a furnace). The echo of Sodom at the cosmic scale is deliberate: the sixth-trumpet judgment is Sodom writ large.
9.19: hē gar exousia tōn hippōn en tō stomati autōn estin kai en tais ourais autōn — "the power of the horses is in their mouths and in their tails." The serpent-tails recall the Eden serpent and anticipate the serpent-dragon of ch12. The power of the horse-cavalry is both offensive (mouths) and dangerous from behind (tails) — a military formation impossible in normal warfare, signaling the supernatural character of this judgment-force.
9.20: ou metenoēsan — "they did not repent" (aorist negated). The purpose of the plagues is repentance; the repeated failure to repent (metanoeō appears in vv20-21 twice, and in 16:9,11) is the defining response of "those who dwell on the earth" (the world-system) to divine judgment. ta erga tōn cheirōn autōn (the works of their hands) — the idols; Ps 115:4-7 and 135:15-17 describe idols as "the work of human hands" that cannot see, hear, or walk — the same three disabilities listed here.
9.21: phonōn ... pharmakōn ... porneias ... klemmaton — murders, sorceries, sexual immorality, thefts. Pharmakōn (sorceries) is from pharmakon (drug/poison/charm) — the word that gives English "pharmacy"; in the NT it consistently refers to occult practices that involve substances, spells, or enchantments (Gal 5:20; Rev 18:23; 21:8; 22:15). The fourfold list is a compressed moral inventory of the world-system's characteristic sins.

10.1: angelon ischuron katabainonta ek tou ouranou — "a mighty angel coming down from heaven." This angel is distinct from the seven trumpet-angels; his appearance (cloud-wrapped, rainbow crown, sun-face, fire-pillar legs) is theophanic — resembling the divine appearances of ch4 and Ezek 1. iris epi tēs kephalēs — "a rainbow above his head." The rainbow (iris) appears in Ezek 1:28 as the sign of the divine glory (the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD was like a rainbow in a cloud on a rainy day). The angel's rainbow-crown connects him to the covenant-faithfulness symbolized by the rainbow since Gen 9:13.
10.2: biblaridion ēneōgmenon — "a little scroll lying open." The diminutive biblaridion (little scroll) distinguishes it from the great sealed scroll of ch5 (biblion). This scroll is already open — the contents are accessible. John will eat it in v9-10. The small open scroll stands in contrast to the sealed closed scroll of ch5: a specific, limited prophetic commission rather than the entire cosmic plan.
10.3: hōsper leōn mykatai — "like a lion roaring" (mykaomai, the bellow of a lion or bull). The lion's roar as a divine-speech metaphor appears in Hos 11:10 and Joel 3:16 ("YHWH roars from Zion"). The seven thunders speak — the number seven signals completeness; John is about to record their speech when he is commanded to seal it.
10.4: sphragison ha elalēsan hoi hepta brontai kai mē auta grapsēs — "seal up what the seven thunders said and do not write it." The command to seal recalls Dan 8:26 ("seal up the vision, for it is for many days") and Dan 12:4,9 ("close up and seal the words of the scroll until the time of the end"). This is the only content in Revelation that is explicitly withheld from the reader — a deliberate gap in the revelation that resists the apocalyptic reader's desire for complete knowledge. Contrast Rev 22:10 ("Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this scroll, because the time is near").
10.5: ton dexion cheira autou eis ton ouranon — "raised his right hand toward heaven." The oath-gesture: lifting the right hand toward heaven in the swearing of an oath (Deut 32:40; Dan 12:7: "the man dressed in linen raised his right hand and his left hand toward heaven and swore by him who lives forever"). The angel's swearing by the living God (v6) is a direct allusion to Dan 12:7.
10.6: chronos ouketi estai — "there will be no more delay" (or: "time will be no more"). The Greek is ambiguous: chronos can mean "delay" (as in "no more waiting") or "time" (as in eschatological time ending). Most interpreters favor "delay" given the context of the imminent seventh trumpet and the completion of the mystery. hos ektisen ton ouranon ... gēn ... thalassan — the oath is sworn by the Creator of heaven, earth, and sea (the tripartite creation formula from Gen 1, repeated in the Decalogue: Exod 20:11).
10.7: etelesthē to mystērion tou Theou — "the mystery of God will be fulfilled." Mystērion in Paul refers to the previously hidden divine plan now revealed in Christ (Rom 16:25; Eph 1:9; 3:3-9; Col 1:26-27). Here, the mystery is the complete plan of salvation-through-judgment that the prophets announced but only partially saw. Euēngelisen (announced the good news) — the mystery was previewed as euangelion (gospel): even the judgment-plan is good news in Revelation's theodicy.
10.8: hypage labein to biblaridion — "go take the little scroll." John is commanded to take active possession: not to receive passively but to approach and take. The two-part transaction (request and grant, vv8-9) before the eating foreshadows the bitter-sweet experience — the prophet must want and accept the prophetic commission before experiencing its weight.
10.9: pikranei sou tēn koilian alla en tō stomati sou estai glykу hōs meli — "it will make your stomach bitter, but in your mouth it will be as sweet as honey." Ezek 2:8-3:3: Ezekiel ate a scroll and it was sweet as honey — but Ezekiel was then sent to speak hard words to a rebellious people. The bitterness-after-sweetness in John's commissioning adds the experiential weight that Ezekiel's version lacked: the prophetic word is genuinely sweet to receive (it is God's word) and genuinely bitter to carry (its content is judgment).
10.10: ēn glykу hōs meli ... epikranthē hē koilia mou — "it was sweet as honey... my stomach was made bitter" (aorist passive). The aorist epikranthē (was embittered) marks the bitterness as a specific, completed experience. The mouth-to-stomach progression maps the prophetic experience: the initial reception of God's word is joyful; the internalization and digestion of its meaning (the full weight of the judgments to come) is painful. The prophet does not remain at a distance from his message.
10.11: dei se palin prophēteusai — "it is necessary for you to prophesy again." Dei (it is necessary/must) is the divine-necessity verb in the NT (used for Christ's passion in Lk 24:7; for Paul's preaching in Acts 9:6; for apocalyptic events in Rev 1:1; 4:1). John's prophetic commission is renewed after the scroll-eating: the re-commissioning mid-vision (like Ezekiel's re-commissioning in Ezek 3:4-11) sends him forward with an expanded mandate: "peoples, nations, languages, and kings."

11.1: kalamos homoios rhabdō — "a measuring rod like a staff." The combination of kalamos (reed, the measurement tool) and rhabdos (staff, the shepherd's or authority-rod) merges two instruments. Ezek 40:3 uses a measuring rod (of six cubits); Zech 2:1-5 uses a measuring line for Jerusalem. In both prophetic texts, measuring signals divine care and preservation — the measured thing belongs to God. metron ton naon tou Theou — "measure the temple of God." The imperative commission: measurement as an act of claiming divine ownership over what is measured.
11.2: tēn aulēn tēn exōthen ... ekbale exōthen — "the court outside... throw it outside." The outer court is excluded from measurement: it is "given to the nations," and they will trample the holy city for mēnas tesserakonta [kai] dyo (42 months). The 42-month period (= 3.5 years = 1260 days = "time, times, and half a time" of Dan 7:25; 12:7) is the symbolic half-seven — the incomplete time of the nations' domination, bounded and limited. Patēsousin (will trample) echoes Lk 21:24 ("Jerusalem will be trampled on by the Gentiles until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled").
11.3: tois dysin martyrsin mou — "my two witnesses." The "my" (mou) marks them as belonging to the Lord: they are his witnesses, as Christ is the faithful witness (ho martys ho pistos, 1:5). sakkous (sackcloth) — the mourning garment of the prophets (Isa 20:2; Zech 13:4) signals that the witnesses prophesy in the mode of lamentation, not triumph. 1260 days of sackcloth-prophecy — the prophetic office in the period of the nations' trampling is marked by grief.
11.4: hai dyo elaiai kai hai dyo lychniai — "the two olive trees and the two lampstands." Zech 4:3,11,14: the two olive trees flanking the golden lampstand are "the two anointed ones who stand before the Lord of the whole earth" — in Zechariah's context, Zerubbabel (civil) and Joshua (priestly). In Revelation the two witnesses together embody both offices. The lampstand connection links them to the seven churches of chs 1-3 (which are lampstands, 1:20): the witnesses are the prophetic-witness function of the church.
11.5: pyr ekporeuetai ek tou stomatos autōn — "fire comes out of their mouths and devours their enemies." The fire-from-mouth is a prophetic power drawn from Elijah (2 Kgs 1:10-12: Elijah calling fire from heaven) and from Jer 5:14 (YHWH's words in the prophet's mouth as fire that devours). The fire is the fire of the word: the prophetic proclamation that executes judgment. apoktanthēnai autous — "must die in this way" (aorist passive infinitive of necessity); the judgment modality is fixed.
11.6: kleiein ton ouranon hina mē hyetos brēchē — "to shut the sky so that no rain falls." Explicitly Elijah's power (1 Kgs 17:1; Lk 4:25; Jas 5:17 — Elijah prayed that it would not rain for 3.5 years). strephousin ta hydata eis haima — Moses's power over water-to-blood (Exod 7:19-20). The two witnesses together embody the Elijah-Moses typology: the two great prophets of Israel whose ministries bracket the OT prophetic tradition. Their pairing at the Transfiguration (Matt 17:3) gives this identification christological precedent.
11.7: to thērion to anabainon ek tēs abyssou — "the beast that rises from the Abyss." This is the beast's first appearance in Revelation (it will be developed in chs 13, 17); the brevity here ("the beast") implies the audience already knows the referent, or that this proleptic mention sets up the fuller narrative. poiēsei meta autōn polemon kai nikēsei autous — "will make war against them and conquer them and kill them." The beast's temporary victory over the witnesses is stated with three aorist future forms (will make, will conquer, will kill) — decisive and brutal.
11.8: Sodoma kai Aigyptos — "Sodom and Egypt." The great city is identified figuratively (pneumatikōs, spiritually/allegorically) with both Sodom (the city of sexual immorality and wickedness, Gen 18-19) and Egypt (the house of slavery, Exod). The identification "where their Lord was crucified" unmistakably identifies the city as Jerusalem — yet its naming as Sodom and Egypt denies it the special status it might claim. Jerusalem has become what it condemned: the city of God's witnesses is now the city of anti-God empire.
11.9: blepousin to ptōma autōn hēmeras treis kai hēmisy — "they stare at their bodies for three and a half days." The 3.5-day period mirrors the 3.5-year (42-month/1260-day) ministry period: the same half-seven structure. ou aphiousin ta ptōmata autōn tethēnai eis mnēma — "they do not permit their bodies to be placed in a tomb." Refusing burial was the ultimate dishonor in Jewish culture (Deut 28:26; Ps 79:3; Jer 8:2) and in many ancient cultures — the ultimate humiliation of one's enemies after death.
11.10: euphrainontai ep' autois kai euphrosunēn poiousin — "they celebrate and exchange gifts." The gift-exchange (pempousin dōra allēlois) on the occasion of the prophets' deaths reads like a dark parody of a festival celebration — comparable to the Purim gift-exchange (Esth 9:22: "days of feasting and joy and giving presents to each other"). The world-system celebrates the silencing of the prophetic witness.
11.11: pneuma zōēs ek tou Theou eisēlthen en autois — "the breath/spirit of life from God entered them." The language is deliberately Gen 2:7 (God breathed the nishmat chayyim, the breath of life, into Adam) and Ezek 37:10 (the breath entered the dry bones and they stood on their feet — estēsan epi tous podas autōn). Both OT texts are cited almost verbatim: the resurrection of the witnesses is narrated in the vocabulary of creation and covenant-restoration. estēsan epi tous podas autōn — they stood on their feet, the exact phrase of Ezek 37:10.
11.12: anabate hōde — "Come up here!" — the same words spoken to John in 4:1 (his heavenly ascent to see the throne). The witnesses' ascension in a cloud (en tē nephelē) echoes both the Elijah ascent (2 Kgs 2:11: Elijah taken up in a whirlwind/chariot of fire) and the Christ-ascension (Acts 1:9: "he was taken up before their very eyes, and a cloud hid him from their sight"). etheōrēsan autous hoi echthroi autōn — their enemies watched: the public vindication mirrors the public humiliation of their death.
11.13: seismos megas egeneto — "a great earthquake occurred." Seismos (earthquake) in Revelation is always associated with divine judgment and theophanic activity (6:12; 8:5; 11:13,19; 16:18). to dekaton tēs poleōs epesen — "a tenth of the city collapsed." The specificity of "a tenth" is unusual in Revelation's symbolic arithmetic (normally thirds and quarters); some see it as reflecting the earthquake's partial scope — unlike the total judgment of the bowls. edothē doxa tō Theō tou ouranou — "they gave glory to the God of heaven" — rare in Revelation (contrast with 16:9,11 where they refuse to repent and give glory); this suggests genuine awe or partial repentance.
11.14: The transition marker "the second woe has passed; the third woe is coming soon (tachos)" closes the interlude of the two witnesses and the sixth-trumpet complex. Tachos (quickly/soon) maintains the sense of urgency that runs through the book (1:1; 22:6,7,12,20). The structural function of v14 is bridging: the woe-announcement of 9:12 is fulfilled, and the seventh trumpet (the third woe) is introduced.
11.15: egenonto hai basileiai tou kosmou tou Kyriou hēmōn kai tou Christou autou — "the kingdom of the world has become (aorist: egenonto) the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ." The aorist marks this as a completed transfer — not "is becoming" but "has become." The seventh trumpet sounds, and heaven announces the fait accompli of the eschatological kingdom. kai basileusei eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn — "he will reign forever and ever" (singular: the King, singular, reigns — though "our Lord and his Christ" are two).
11.16: epesan epi ta prosōpa autōn kai prosekunēsan tō Theō — the twenty-four elders prostrate themselves (piptō + prosekunēsan: fall + worship) exactly as in 4:10 and 5:14; the gesture of prostrate worship frames the throne-room scenes. The verb proskuneō in Revelation appears 24 times — the entire book can be read as a contest between whose worship is legitimate.
11.17: eilēphas tēn dynamin sou tēn megalēn kai ebasileusas — "you have taken your great power and have begun to reign." Ebasileusas (aorist: you have begun to reign/you have reigned) — the aorist of divine reign is the announcement of the accomplished fact from the heavenly perspective. ho ōn kai ho ēn — "who is and who was." Significantly the third element "who is to come" (ho erchomenos, present in 1:4,8; 4:8) is absent here — because he has now come: the coming is fulfilled.
11.18: hōrgisthēsan ta ethnē kai ēlthen hē orgē sou — "the nations raged and your wrath came." The verb orgizō is applied first to the nations' rage, then to God's wrath — the same root, mirroring each other: the nations' anger is met by divine anger. The verse condenses Ps 2:1,5 (why do the nations rage... he rebukes them in his wrath) into a single announcement. tous mikrous kai tous megalous — "both small and great" — the leveling formula: divine judgment/reward respects no hierarchy.
11.19: hē kibōtos tēs diathēkēs autou — "the ark of his covenant." The ark, which disappeared from the narrative of Israel's history after the Babylonian destruction of the Temple (its fate is unknown; 2 Macc 2:4-8 says Jeremiah hid it on Nebo; it was never recovered), is now revealed in the heavenly temple. The unveiling of the heavenly ark at the completion of the seventh trumpet announces the return of the fullness of covenant-presence: what Israel lost is now visible in its eternal, heavenly reality. The Sinai-theophany formula (lightning, thunder, earthquake, hail) frames the revelation as a new Sinai.

17.1: κρίμα τῆς πόρνης τῆς μεγάλης — krima (judicial verdict, executed sentence) rather than krisis (process of judgment); the angel announces not an ongoing trial but a completed verdict already pronounced in heaven. πόρνη (pornē, prostitute) carries the full weight of the OT prophetic tradition of covenant-unfaithfulness (Hos 1–3; Isa 23:17; Ezek 16; Nah 3:4), now applied to Rome as the empire that seduced the nations into idolatrous alliance. τῆς μεγάλης — the superlative marks her as the ultimate embodiment of imperial seduction.
17.2: ἐπόρνευσαν (aorist) — the kings of the earth committed fornication with her: a completed act of political-religious alliance treated as covenant betrayal in prophetic idiom. ἐμεθύσθησαν (aorist passive) — were made drunk: the passive voice is deliberate; the nations were intoxicated by Rome's wine, the seductive power of imperial culture that draws participants into its idolatrous economic system. The wine of passion (θυμός) is not measured desire but consuming ardor that leads to judgment.
17.3: εἰς ἔρημον — into a wilderness: erēmos is the same word used for the faithful woman's refuge in 12:6, 14; here the wilderness is the prostitute's domain, the anti-Eden where the harlot reigns. The deliberate echo inverts the two women: one flees into the wilderness for divine protection, the other sits enthroned in it. γέμον ὀνόματα βλασφημίας — the beast is covered with names of blasphemy; the programmatic nature of the beast's blasphemy is its claim to divine titles that belong to YHWH alone (the imperial cult's demand for dominus et deus, lord and god). ἑπτά κεφαλάς καί δέκα κέρατα — seven heads and ten horns echo Dan 7:7, 20.
17.4: πορφυροῦν καί κόκκινον — purple and scarlet: Tyrian purple (from murex snails) was the most expensive dye in antiquity, reserved for emperors and the wealthiest elite; the colors identify the prostitute as the empire itself. κεχρυσωμένη χρυσίῳ — adorned with gold (the cognate accusative intensifies: gilded with gold); the same luxury goods appear in the New Jerusalem's description (21:18–21) but as building material of a holy city, exposing the difference between divine and imperial beauty. βδελυγμάτων — abominations (bdelygmata), the LXX term for idols (Deut 29:17; Ezek 5:11); the prostitute's cup is filled with idolatry, making the economic-religious system of Rome explicitly cultic.
17.5: μυστήριον — mystery: not "mysterious" but an unveiled secret, the apocalyptic technical term for divine revelation disclosed to insiders (Rom 11:25; Eph 1:9; 1 Cor 15:51; Col 1:26–27). The forehead-inscription echoes both the Roman practice of branding enslaved persons and prostitutes, and the high priest's golden plate inscribed with YHWH's name (Exod 28:36–38); the prostitute is the anti-high-priest, bearing a counterfeit sacred inscription. Βαβυλών ἡ μεγάλη — Babylon the Great: the first empire to destroy Jerusalem and exile Israel provides the cipher for Rome; this identification was transparent to first-century readers (cf. 4 Ezra 3:1–2, 28–31; 2 Bar. 10–11; 1 Pet 5:13).
17.6: μεθύουσαν (present participle, ongoing state) — the prostitute who made others drunk on her wine is herself in a sustained state of drunkenness, but on blood; grotesque inversion of the intoxication theme of vv. 1–2. τῶν μαρτύρων Ἰησοῦ — those who bore witness to Jesus; martys (witness) was acquiring its specialized sense of one who dies for their testimony, giving us "martyr." ἐθαύμασα θαῦμα μέγα — the cognate accusative construction (I marveled a great marvel) intensifies John's astonishment to the point of literary redundancy, signaling the enormity of what he sees: an empire drunk on the blood of the innocent.
17.7: ἐρῶ σοι τό μυστήριον — I will disclose to you the mystery: the angel's interpretive role mirrors Gabriel's explanatory function in Daniel (Dan 7:16; 8:15–16); apocalyptic vision requires heavenly exposition for the seer to move from image to meaning. τοῦ βαστάζοντος αὐτήν — of the one carrying/bearing her: the beast supports the prostitute; Roman imperial power sustains the economic-religious system of Babylon. The relationship is parasitic: empire needs its commercial ideology, and the commercial system needs the empire's coercive power.
17.8: ἦν καί οὐκ ἔστιν καί μέλλει ἀναβαίνειν — was and is not and is about to come up: a direct parody of the divine formula ho ōn kai ho ēn kai ho erchomenos (who is and who was and who is to come; 1:4, 8; 4:8). The beast's pseudo-eternal formula is a corruption of YHWH's genuine eternal nature: where God's eternity is expressed as present-past-coming, the beast's is expressed as past-absent-future rising; the beast's "eternity" is a hollow imitation. εἰς ἀπώλειαν — into destruction: the beast's destination is fixed from the outset of the description, framing the entire vision as judgment-narrative. οἱ κατοικοῦντες ἐπί τῆς γῆς — earth-dwellers: Revelation's consistent term for those who orient their lives by the world-system rather than heaven (cf. 6:10; 8:13; 11:10; 13:8, 12, 14).
17.9: ὁ νοῦς ὁ ἔχων σοφίαν — the mind having wisdom: the same formula as 13:18 ("here is wisdom"); an encoded identification requiring discernment beyond surface reading. ἑπτά ὄρη — seven mountains: oros (mountain) rather than lophos (hill), though both can render Latin mons; the seven hills of Rome (Septemontium) were the universal identifier of the city in antiquity (Virgil, Propertius, Cicero all use it). John's audience in the seven cities of Asia Minor would have recognized the identification immediately while the text maintained deniability before Roman censors.
17.10: βασιλεῖς ἑπτά — seven kings/emperors: identifying the specific sequence has generated extensive scholarly debate; the most probable reading counts from Augustus (five fallen: Augustus, Tiberius, Caligula, Claudius, Nero; one is: Vespasian; one not yet come: Titus), though the seven may be typological rather than calendric. ὅταν ἔλθῃ ὀλίγον αὐτόν δεῖ μεῖναι — when he comes he must remain for a little while: the divine dei (it is necessary/must) constrains even the beast's ally; no imperial reign escapes divine governance of duration.
17.11: αὐτός ὄγδοός ἐστιν καί ἐκ τῶν ἑπτά ἐστιν — he himself is an eighth and is from among the seven: the beast both transcends and recapitulates the seven-king series; he is not a new entity but the oppressor-principle distilled. The Nero redivivus myth (the widespread expectation, post-68 CE, that Nero would return from death or from Parthian exile) likely underlies the eighth-king language; Domitian was widely seen as the "second Nero," combining Nero's cruelty with his demand for divine honors. καί εἰς ἀπώλειαν ὑπάγει — and goes to destruction: the refrain of the beast's destiny.
17.12: δέκα κέρατα δέκα βασιλεῖς — ten horns = ten kings: from Dan 7:24 (ten horns = ten kings arising from the fourth beast); the ten kings have not yet received their kingdoms (βασιλείαν οὐδέπω ἔλαβον), meaning their authority is still future and contingent. ὡς βασιλεῖς μίαν ὥραν — as kings for one hour: their authority is real but radically time-limited; "one hour" contrasts with the Lamb's eternal kingship (11:15). The delegation structure (beast grants authority to the ten) parodies the divine authority structure (God grants authority to the Lamb).
17.13: μίαν γνώμην ἔχουσιν — they have one resolve/purpose: gnōmē (deliberate resolve, considered opinion, decree); the unity of anti-God forces is ideological, not merely political. τήν ἑαυτῶν δύναμιν καί ἐξουσίαν τῷ θηρίῳ διδόασιν — they give their own power and authority to the beast: the delegated power of human kingdoms is voluntarily surrendered upward to the beast, completing a counterfeit divine-authority chain (God → Lamb → saints vs. dragon → beast → kings → Babylon).
17.14: πολεμήσουσιν μετά τοῦ ἀρνίου — they will make war with the Lamb (future; the conflict is certain but still coming). νικήσει αὐτούς τό ἀρνίον — the Lamb will conquer them: nikaō is Revelation's consistent victory-verb, appearing in the promise to each of the seven churches (2:7, 11, 17, 26; 3:5, 12, 21) and in 5:5 where the Lamb "has conquered" (enikēsen, aorist: by the cross). The future conquest of 17:14 is the manifestation of the past conquest of 5:5. κύριος κυρίων ἐστίν καί βασιλεύς βασιλέων — Lord of lords and King of kings: the title drawn from Deut 10:17 (God of gods and Lord of lords); here applied to the Lamb, anticipating 19:16 where it is inscribed on the rider's robe.
17.15: ὕδατα οὗ ἡ πόρνη κάθηται — the waters where the prostitute sits: decoded as λαοί καί ὄχλοι καί ἔθνη καί γλῶσσαι (peoples and multitudes and nations and languages), the fourfold formula for all humanity (5:9; 7:9; 11:9; 13:7). The cosmic ocean imagery comes from Dan 7:2–3 (the four winds stirring the great sea from which the beasts arise); the prostitute's power rests on the mass of humanity subject to imperial domination. The decoded image exposes Rome's universality as the ground of its power and the breadth of its culpability.
17.16: μισήσουσιν τήν πόρνην — they will hate the prostitute (future): the beast turns on its own ideological system; imperial power devours the commercial-religious culture it created. ἠρημωμένην ποιήσουσιν αὐτήν καί γυμνήν — they will make her desolate and naked: the stripping is judicial nakedness (Ezek 16:37–39; 23:26–29; Nah 3:5 — YHWH strips the unfaithful city before her lovers). τάς σάρκας αὐτῆς φάγονται καί αὐτήν κατακαύσουσιν ἐν πυρί — they will eat her flesh and burn her with fire: cannibalistic destruction echoes the siege-aftermath oracles of Jer 51:25, 58; the burning of Babylon was the climactic act of divine judgment in the prophets.
17.17: ὁ θεός ἔδωκεν εἰς τάς καρδίας αὐτῶν — God gave into their hearts: the divine sovereignty operating through and over the beast's coalition; the anti-God alliance unwittingly executes the divine decree. The parallel is Isa 10:5–7 (Assyria as YHWH's rod of anger, used against Israel without knowing it). μέχρι τελεσθήσονται οἱ λόγοι τοῦ θεοῦ — until the words of God are completed: teleō (to complete, fulfill, bring to the intended end); the divine word determines the terminus of the beast's permitted authority; the beast operates within a divinely bounded lease.
17.18: ἡ γυνή ἣν εἶδες ἔστιν ἡ πόλις ἡ μεγάλη — the woman you saw is the great city: the plain identification caps the extended encrypted vision; after 17:9's seven-hills decoding, this is the explicit confirmation. ἡ ἔχουσα βασιλείαν ἐπί τῶν βασιλέων τῆς γῆς — having dominion over the kings of the earth: basileia (kingdom/sovereignty) is the very thing the Lamb's kingdom (11:15; 12:10; 19:6) permanently displaces; the prostitute's political sovereignty is the negative that the new Jerusalem's divine reign reverses.

18.1: ἐξουσίαν μεγάλην — great authority: the angel's authority makes him the agent of the verdict pronounced in heaven. ἡ γῆ ἐφωτίσθη ἐκ τῆς δόξης αὐτοῦ — the earth was illumined from his glory: the angel's radiance echoes the vision of the divine glory returning to the temple in Ezek 43:2 ("the earth shone with his glory") — a judgment-of-Babylon oracle delivered through a glory-bearing divine agent.
18.2: ἔπεσεν ἔπεσεν Βαβυλών ἡ μεγάλη — Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the great: the doubled aorist is a direct verbal citation of Isa 21:9 LXX (ἔπεσεν ἔπεσεν Βαβυλών); the prophetic past (completed action before the event) signals divine certainty. By quoting Isaiah's Babylon oracle verbatim, Revelation claims that Rome's fall is the fulfillment of Isaiah's word. κατοικητήριον δαιμονίων — habitation of demons: the fallen city becomes the haunt of unclean spirits, fulfilling the pattern of OT judgment-desolation oracles (Isa 13:19–22; 34:11–15; Jer 50:39), where fallen cities become dwelling places for wild creatures and spirits of chaos.
