18.3: θυμοῦ τῆς πορνείας αὐτῆς — the passion/wrath of her sexual immorality: thymos can mean "wrath" (God's in 14:10, 19; 15:1, 7; 16:1, 19) or "fierce passion/desire" (the consuming ardor of idolatrous alliance); both senses may be active — the wine of passionate idolatry that itself produces wrath. ἐπλούτησαν ἐκ τῆς δυνάμεως τοῦ στρήνους αὐτῆς — grew rich from the power of her luxurious excess: strēnos (luxurious excess, sensuality in excess) appears only here and v. 7 in the NT; the merchants' wealth is not incidental but structurally derived from Rome's culture of excess.
18.4: ἐξέλθατε ἐξ αὐτῆς ὁ λαός μου — Come out from her, my people (aorist imperative, urgency): the divine call echoes the exodus-from-Babylon cluster of OT oracles — Isa 48:20 ("Go out from Babylon!"), Jer 50:8 ("Flee from the midst of Babylon"), Jer 51:6, 45 ("Flee from the midst of Babylon; save your lives"). The Exodus pattern (separation from the empire of oppression) is activated as the model for Christian non-participation in Rome's economic-religious system. ἵνα μή συγκοινωνήσητε ταῖς ἁμαρτίαις αὐτῆς — so that you will not share in her sins: synkoinōneō (to participate with, share in); the practical ethical demand: cultic and economic withdrawal from the imperial system is not optional for those who bear the Lamb's name.
18.5: ἐκολλήθησαν αὐτῆς αἱ ἁμαρτίαι ἄχρι τοῦ οὐρανοῦ — her sins were joined/piled up to heaven: ekollaō (to glue, join closely together; aorist passive) suggests accretion over time, a pile built course by course until it reaches divine attention; echoes Gen 18:20 (Sodom's outcry "has reached" YHWH) and Ezra 9:6 ("our iniquities have grown above our heads"). ἐμνημόνευσεν ὁ θεός τά ἀδικήματα αὐτῆς — God has remembered her crimes: the divine memory is judicial, not nostalgic; God's "remembering" in OT covenant theology activates response — saving for Israel (Gen 8:1; Exod 2:24) and judging for enemies (cf. Ps 137:7).
18.6: ἀπόδοτε αὐτῇ ὡς καί αὐτή ἀπέδωκεν — repay her as she herself repaid (aorist imperatives; divine command to the agents of judgment): lex talionis operating at the imperial scale. διπλώσατε τά διπλᾶ — double the double for her: the double repayment echoes Jer 50:15, 29 ("repay her according to her deeds") and Isa 40:2 ("she has received from the LORD's hand double for all her sins"). The cup imagery is sustained: the prostitute poured out her cup of abomination to the nations; now a double measure is poured back.
18.7: ὅσα ἐδόξασεν ἑαυτήν καί ἐστρηνίασεν — as much as she glorified herself and lived in luxury: the reflexive heautēn (glorified herself) is the root sin; divine glory (19:1 — salvation and glory belong to God) has been arrogated to the imperial city. βασίλισσα κάθημαι καί χήρα οὐκ εἰμί — I sit as queen and I am not a widow: the boast directly echoes Isa 47:7–8 (the fall of Babylon: "I shall be queen forever... I am, and there is no one besides me; I shall not sit as a widow"); Revelation applies Isaiah's Babylon oracle to Rome verse by verse.
18.8: ἐν μιᾷ ἡμέρᾳ — in one day: the suddenness of divine judgment against the empire's self-confident security; centuries of accumulated power collapse in a single day. θάνατος καί πένθος καί λιμός — death and mourning and famine: precisely the three divine judgments of Ezek 5:12; 14:21 (sword, famine, pestilence), here deployed against the city that boasted "I will not see grief" (v. 7). κύριος ὁ θεός ὁ κρίνας αὐτήν — Lord God who has judged her: the aorist krinas (having judged) grounds the entire judgment sequence in the divine character and judicial act; Babylon's destruction is not political accident but covenantal verdict.
18.9: κλαύσουσιν καί κόψονται ἐπ' αὐτήν — will weep and beat their breasts over her: koptomai (to beat oneself in grief) was the conventional mourning posture of the ancient world. οἱ μετ' αὐτῆς πορνεύσαντες καί στρηνιάσαντες — those who committed fornication with her and lived in luxury with her: the mourners are Rome's political clients who profited from the system; their grief is economic loss, not moral repentance — the same self-interest that joined them to Babylon now laments its passing.
18.10: ἀπό μακρόθεν ἑστηκότες — standing far off (present participle, sustained safe distance): the kings do not approach the burning city; their grief is calculated self-preservation disguised as mourning. ἐν μιᾷ ὥρᾳ ἦλθεν ἡ κρίσις σου — in one hour your judgment has come: the first of the three "one-hour" refrains (vv. 10, 17, 19) that punctuate the three laments (kings, merchants, sailors); the contrast between Rome's centuries-long dominion and the single hour of her collapse is the central irony of the chapter.
18.11: οἱ ἔμποροι τῆς γῆς κλαίουσιν καί πενθοῦσιν ἐπ' αὐτήν — the merchants of the earth weep and mourn over her (present tense — visionary immediacy): emporos (merchant, long-distance trader); the mourning is commercial — ὅτι τόν γόμον αὐτῶν οὐδεῖς ἀγοράζει οὐκέτι (because no one buys their cargo anymore). The merchants' grief is indistinguishable from the kings': both mourn the loss of a profitable patron, not the moral collapse of a corrupt system.
18.12: The catalog of luxury goods begins with the most precious (gold, silver, precious stones, pearls) and moves through textiles (fine linen, purple, silk, scarlet), then specialty woods and goods. ξύλον θύινον — citron/thyine wood: from the sandarac tree (Callitris articulata) of North Africa, prized for its fragrant, beautifully grained wood used in expensive furniture; Pliny the Elder (NH 13.29) describes the Roman obsession with citron-wood tables as a luxury mania. ἐλέφαντα — ivory: imported primarily from sub-Saharan Africa and India. The catalog maps the reach of Rome's trade network across the known world.
18.13: The catalog continues through aromatics (cinnamon, spice, incense, myrrh, frankincense — all imported from Arabia and India via the Red Sea trade), foodstuffs (wine, olive oil, fine flour, wheat), and livestock (cattle, sheep, horses, chariots). σώματα καί ψυχάς ἀνθρώπων — bodies and souls of human beings: the list climaxes with enslaved people. Sōmata (bodies) was standard commercial Greek for slaves; the addition of psychas anthrōpōn (souls of humans) is John's editorial exposure of the dehumanization: Rome's economy treats human souls as cargo. The slave trade was foundational to the Roman economy — estimates suggest 30% of Italy's population were enslaved in the first century.
18.14: ἡ ὀπώρα σου τῆς ἐπιθυμίας τῆς ψυχῆς — the ripe fruit of your soul's desire: opōra (late-summer ripe fruit, the harvest season's abundance); the word carries connotations of seasonal fullness and luxury now permanently ended (ἀπώλετο ἀπό σοῦ — perished from you). The merchants' lament personalizes the loss: they had identified their soul's longing with Babylon's luxury; now their psyche (soul) is exposed as having invested in what perishes.
18.15: ἀπό μακρόθεν στήσονται — they will stand far off: the merchant lament repeats the spatial image of the king lament (v. 10); commercial self-interest produces the same calculated distance as political self-preservation. The parallel structure of the three laments (kings vv. 9–10; merchants vv. 11–17; sailors vv. 17–19) mirrors the prophetic lament structure of Ezek 27 over Tyre, which Revelation deliberately echoes throughout chapter 18.
18.16: οὐαί οὐαί ἡ πόλις ἡ μεγάλη — Woe! Woe to the great city: the doubled ouai (woe) repeats the prophetic woe-oracle formula (Isa 5:8–23; Amos 5:18; 6:1); the merchants who dressed the city in her luxury now lament her stripping. ἡ περιβεβλημένη βύσσινον καί πορφυροῦν καί κόκκινον — clothed in fine linen and purple and scarlet: the specific textiles of 17:4 reappear in the lament, confirming the merchants' complicity in the prostitute's display; they supplied her dress and now mourn its destruction.
18.17: ἐν μιᾷ ὥρᾳ ἠρημώθη ὁ τοσοῦτος πλοῦτος — in one hour such great wealth was laid waste: second "one hour" refrain. Ērēmōthē (was laid waste, desolated; aorist passive) uses the same root as the wilderness (erēmos) of 17:3, where John first saw the prostitute seated in desolation; the fallen city returns to the wasteland state. κυβερνήτης καί πᾶς ὁ ἐπί τόπον πλέων — every helmsman and all who sail to any place: the shipping industry that profited from Mediterranean commerce.
18.18: τίς ὁμοία τῇ πόλει τῇ μεγάλῃ — What city was like the great city? The rhetorical question is the sailors' lament echoing Ezek 27:32 ("Who is like Tyre, like one destroyed in the midst of the sea?"); Tyre the great trading city of the ancient world serves as the prophetic template for Rome. The rhetorical form implies that no rival exists — and now the unrivaled city is gone. The question is unanswerable because the answer was always meant to be "none," and now the greatness that prompted the question has vanished.
18.19: ἔβαλον χοῦν ἐπί τάς κεφαλάς αὐτῶν — they threw dust on their heads: the ancient mourning rite (Josh 7:6; Job 2:12; Lam 2:10; Ezek 27:30 — explicitly in the Tyre lament); the sailors add dust to the weeping and wailing, completing the mourning-rite vocabulary. ἐκ τῆς τιμιότητος αὐτῆς — from her costliness: timiotēs (preciousness, high commercial value) appears only here in the NT; the word captures Rome's function as the center of Mediterranean commerce — her "value" was entirely economic. The third "one hour" refrain closes the triad.
18.20: εὐφραίνου ἐπ' αὐτῇ οὐρανέ — Rejoice over her, O heaven: the heaven/earth reversal crystallizes the chapter's structure: while earth's three classes of profiteers mourn (vv. 9–19), heaven is commanded to rejoice. The prophetic call echoes Isa 44:23; 49:13 (calls for cosmic rejoicing at divine deliverance). ἔκρινεν ὁ θεός τό κρίμα ὑμῶν ἐξ αὐτῆς — God has judged your judgment from her: the reflexive justice — the krima (verdict) that Babylon executed against the saints is the exact verdict executed against Babylon; the lex talionis operating at cosmic scale through the divine judge.
18.21: λίθον ὡς μύλινον μέγαν — a stone like a great millstone: the prophetic action-sign echoes Jer 51:63–64, where Jeremiah tied a stone to a scroll of Babylon's judgment and threw it into the Euphrates as a sign of the city's sinking to rise no more. Revelation intensifies: an angel of great authority throws the stone. ὁρμήματι βληθήσεται Βαβυλών ἡ μεγάλη πόλις — with violent force Babylon the great city will be thrown: hormēma (violent rushing force, momentum); the finality and speed of the destruction matches the "one hour" imagery of the laments.
18.22: The sevenfold "never again" announcement (ou mē + subjunctive — the strongest Greek negation) structures vv. 22–23: harpists, musicians, flutists, trumpeters; craftsmen of every craft; the sound of the millstone; the light of the lamp; the voice of bride and bridegroom. The silencing of music and craft echoes Jer 7:34; 25:10 (YHWH's judgment on Jerusalem removes the sounds of joy and human productivity); now applied to Rome. φωνή νυμφίου καί νύμφης — voice of bridegroom and bride: the joyful domestic life of the city permanently extinguished; the anti-type to the marriage supper of the Lamb (19:7–9) that replaces it.
18.23: φῶς λύχνου οὐ μή φάνῃ ἐν σοί ἔτι — the light of a lamp will never shine in you again: the domestic lamp, the most basic sign of inhabited life, is extinguished permanently. ὅτι οἱ ἔμποροί σου ἦσαν οἱ μεγιστᾶνες τῆς γῆς — because your merchants were the great ones of the earth: Rome elevated commercial power to political status; the economic lords were the political lords. ἐν τῇ φαρμακείᾳ σου ἐπλανήθησαν πάντα τά ἔθνη — by your sorcery all the nations were deceived: pharmakeia (the use of drugs, potions, or occult arts; whence "pharmacy") — in apocalyptic context, Rome's seductive imperial cult with its rituals, spectacle, and economic coercion is named as sorcery. Epananēthēsan (were deceived/led astray): the same verb used for Satan's activity (12:9; 20:3, 8).
18.24: ἐν αὐτῇ αἷμα προφητῶν καί ἁγίων εὑρέθη — in her was found the blood of prophets and holy ones: heurethē (was found, divine passive) — the forensic finding establishes legal guilt; divine search has located the evidence. πάντων τῶν ἐσφαγμένων ἐπί τῆς γῆς — of all who have been slaughtered on the earth: esphagmenōn (from sphazō = to slaughter, the same verb used for the Lamb at 5:6, 9, 12; 13:8) — the martyred saints share the vocabulary of the Lamb's slaughter. Rome's guilt extends beyond Christian martyrs to all the slaughtered throughout history; the empire of blood is held accountable for blood.

19.1: ἁλληλούϊα — Alleluia: Hebrew hallelu Yah (praise YHWH); the only four occurrences in the NT are all in this chapter (vv. 1, 3, 4, 6). The preservation of the Hebrew exclamation in a Greek text signals direct continuity with Israel's Passover liturgy (Pss 111–118, the Hallel psalms, were sung at Passover); the redeemed community in heaven joins the ancient liturgy of Israel. ἡ σωτηρία καί ἡ δόξα καί ἡ δύναμις τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν — salvation and glory and power belong to our God: the threefold doxology grounds the heavenly rejoicing in divine attributes, not merely divine acts; these qualities belong essentially to God, not achieved by the angels' worship.
19.2: ἀληθιναί καί δίκαιαι αἱ κρίσεις αὐτοῦ — his judgments are true and just: alēthinos (genuine, ontologically real, not merely accurate) qualifies the judgments as corresponding to ultimate reality; dikaios (just, righteous) qualifies them as morally correct. Together they preempt any charge that God's judgment of Babylon was disproportionate, capricious, or mistaken. ἔκρινεν τήν πόρνην τήν μεγάλην — he has judged the great prostitute (aorist: the judgment is complete); the heavenly community attributes the fall of Rome directly to divine judicial action.
19.3: ὁ καπνός αὐτῆς ἀναβαίνει εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων — her smoke goes up forever and ever: the permanence of Babylon's destruction; echoes Isa 34:9–10 (Edom's judgment: the smoke "goes up forever" and "from generation to generation it shall lie waste"). The eternal smoke is not an eternal burning but the permanent evidence of completed judgment — the site of empire becomes perpetually marked desolation. The second Hallelujah confirms and extends the first.
19.4: ἔπεσαν οἱ πρεσβύτεροι οἱ εἴκοσι τέσσαρες καί τά τέσσαρα ζῷα — the twenty-four elders and four living creatures fell down (aorist; the prostration is total and immediate): the same heavenly court that has witnessed all the seal, trumpet, and bowl judgments now worships in response to Babylon's fall. ἀμήν καί ἁλληλούϊα — Amen and Alleluia: the Hebrew Amen (so be it; affirmation of truth) combined with hallelu Yah creates the liturgical double affirmation; the elders and living creatures ratify the heavenly chorus's praise.
19.5: φωνή ἀπό τοῦ θρόνου — a voice from the throne: authoritative but unidentified; not Christ (he does not call himself a fellow-servant with John in v. 10) and not the Father (the community is told to praise "our God"), so most likely an angel of the throne. αἰνεῖτε τῷ θεῷ ἡμῶν πάντες οἱ δοῦλοι αὐτοῦ — praise our God, all his servants: the dative tō theō (to/for God) directs the praise toward its object; doulos (slave/servant) is the highest designation the redeemed bear — Paul's (Rom 1:1), John's (Rev 1:1), and Christ's servants' shared identity.
19.6: ὡς φωνήν ὄχλου πολλοῦ καί ὡς φωνήν ὑδάτων πολλῶν καί ὡς φωνήν βροντῶν ἰσχυρῶν — like the voice of a great multitude and like the sound of many waters and like the sound of mighty thunders: the triple simile echoes the voice of YHWH in Ezek 1:24; 43:2 and the glorified Christ in Rev 1:15; all three are combined, signaling that the praise of the redeemed carries divine authority. ἐβασίλευσεν κύριος ὁ θεός ὁ παντοκράτωρ — the Lord God Almighty has begun to reign (ingressive aorist: has become king, not "became king" but the moment of universal acknowledgment of what always was true); after Babylon's fall the divine reign is manifest.
19.7: ὁ γάμος τοῦ ἀρνίου ἦλθεν — the wedding of the Lamb has come (aorist: arrived, is here): the marriage metaphor pervades the OT covenant relationship (Isa 54:5–6; Jer 2:2; Ezek 16; Hos 2:19–20 — YHWH as husband of Israel); the Lamb marries his bride = the covenant relationship reaches its eschatological consummation. ἡτοίμασεν ἑαυτήν — she prepared herself (reflexive heautēn): the bride's preparation is her own active response; the passive edothē (it was given to her) in v. 8 preserves divine initiative, but the preparedness is the bride's genuine own action within that gift.
19.8: βύσσινον λαμπρόν καθαρόν — fine linen bright and pure: the bridal garment of the church directly contrasts with the prostitute's dress (17:4: purple and scarlet; 18:16). The fabric is the same (byssinon = fine linen appears in 18:12, 16 of Babylon's garments) but the moral status is opposite: the church's linen is bright and pure, the prostitute's is stained with blood and abomination. τά δικαιώματα τῶν ἁγίων — the righteous deeds/acts of the saints: dikaiōma (righteous deed, requirement fulfilled); the garment is woven from the saints' actual righteous acts — not credited righteousness but enacted righteousness, the fruit of the Spirit in the community.
19.9: μακάριοι οἱ εἰς τό δεῖπνον τοῦ γάμου τοῦ ἀρνίου κεκλημένοι — blessed are those called to the marriage supper of the Lamb: the fourth beatitude in Revelation (1:3; 14:13; 16:15; 19:9); keklēmenoi = those who have been called (perfect passive participle: the completed divine calling with ongoing present state of being-called). The marriage supper stands in deliberate contrast to the "great supper of God" (v. 17), where the carrion birds feast on the slain armies: one supper is the feast of redemption, the other the feast of judgment. οὗτοι οἱ λόγοι ἀληθινοί τοῦ θεοῦ εἰσίν — these are the true/genuine words of God: the angelic affirmation uses alēthinos (ontologically real, not merely accurate); the vision corresponds to ultimate reality.
19.10: σύνδουλός σού εἰμι — I am your fellow slave: the angel's refusal of worship (hora mē = watch out, stop it!) establishes the absolute distinction between creature and Creator; however exalted, the angel is a doulos (slave) as John is. ἡ γάρ μαρτυρία Ἰησοῦ ἐστιν τό πνεῦμα τῆς προφητείας — for the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy: the genitive Iēsou is most likely both subjective (the testimony Jesus himself gives — he is the chief prophet, the Word of God, 19:13) and objective (testimony about Jesus — the content that all genuine prophecy aims at); all true prophecy has Jesus as its source and its subject.
19.11: ἠνεῳγμένον τόν οὐρανόν — heaven opened (perfect passive participle: the state of being-open following a completed opening; the heavenly realm stands permanently accessible for this final revelation). λεγόμενος Πιστός καί Ἀληθινός — called Faithful and True: the rider's first named title; pistos (faithful, trustworthy) echoes 3:14 (Jesus the "faithful and true witness"); alēthinos (genuine, the real thing) distinguishes him from all pretenders who claim divine authority. ἐν δικαιοσύνῃ κρίνει καί πολεμεῖ — in righteousness he judges and makes war: dikaiosynē governs both the judicial and military action; the warfare is just precisely because it is judicial, not arbitrary conquest.
19.12: ὀφθαλμοί αὐτοῦ ὡς φλόξ πυρός — his eyes like a flame of fire: repeated from the Son-of-Man vision of 1:14; the divine scrutiny that penetrates all pretense and self-deception. στεφάνους πολλούς — many diadems: diadēma (royal diadem, the headband of sovereignty) rather than stephanos (victor's wreath); the many diadems against the dragon's seven (12:3) and the beast's ten (13:1) — Christ's crown-count exceeds all rivals. ὄνομα γεγραμμένον ὃ οὐδεῖς οἶδεν εἰ μή αὐτός — a name written which no one knew except himself: the divine name beyond human language; in OT tradition, knowing a being's true name meant mastery over it (Gen 32:29; Exod 3:13–14); Christ's name exceeds human comprehension and cannot be controlled by creature-knowledge.
19.13: ῥεραντισμένον αἵματι — sprinkled with blood: the majority manuscript reading from rhantizō (to sprinkle/besprinkle) vs. bebammenon (dipped, from baptō) in a minority of manuscripts. The "sprinkled" reading is to be preferred as the harder reading and as the more theologically loaded: the high priest sprinkled blood on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:14–15); Isa 52:15 uses "sprinkle" for the Servant's action on many nations. The blood on the robe may be the rider's own atoning blood — worn before any battle, identifying him as the Lamb before he is the warrior. ὁ Λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ — the Word of God: the only occurrence of the Logos title outside the Johannine prologue (John 1:1, 14); in the Apocalypse, the Word of God is the active divine speech that creates, judges, and renews — the rider is divine speech made visible and armed.
19.14: τά στρατεύματα τά ἐν τῷ οὐρανῷ ἠκολούθει αὐτῷ — the armies in heaven were following him (imperfect: ongoing march, the sustained movement of the heavenly host). ἐνδεδυμένοι βύσσινον λευκόν καθαρόν — clothed in fine linen white and pure: the heavenly armies wear the same garment as the bride (v. 8, same words byssinon, katharon); the armies and the bride are the same community viewed from different perspectives — the church is both the wedding party and the host that follows the rider.
19.15: ἐκπορεύεται ῥομφαία ὀξεῖα — a sharp sword goes out (present: the ongoing reality of divine speech-as-weapon); rhomphaia (the large Thracian broadsword) rather than machaira (short dagger); the sword from the mouth (1:16; 2:12, 16) identifies the warfare as by the word of God (Isa 11:4; 49:2; Heb 4:12 — the word sharper than any two-edged sword). ποιμανεῖ αὐτούς ἐν ῥάβδῳ σιδηρᾷ — he will shepherd them with an iron rod (Ps 2:9): poimainō (to shepherd) is unexpected in a battle context — governing and shepherding cannot be separated for the Lamb-who-is-Lion. πατεῖ τήν ληνόν τοῦ οἴνου τοῦ θυμοῦ τῆς ὀργῆς τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ παντοκράτορος — treads the winepress of the wine of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty: the accumulation of genitives conveys rhetorical intensity; the winepress of divine judgment from Isa 63:3 and Joel 3:13 is trodden by the rider himself.
19.16: ἐπί τόν μηρόν αὐτοῦ — on his thigh: the warrior straps his sword to his thigh (Ps 45:3: "Gird your sword on your thigh, O warrior"); the name is inscribed where the weapon hangs, uniting his sovereign title and his fighting posture. Βασιλεύς βασιλέων καί Κύριος κυρίων — King of kings and Lord of lords: the title first attributed to the Lamb at 17:14 is now visibly inscribed on the rider's robe; YHWH is "God of gods and Lord of lords" in Deut 10:17 and Dan 2:47. The rider bears the exclusive divine superlative as his war-name, the counter-claim to every imperial title.
19.17: ἑστῶτα ἐν τῷ ἡλίῳ — standing in the sun: the angel's position in the source of all natural light signals maximal visibility and authority; the great supper announcement is made at cosmic volume. εἰς τό δεῖπνον τό μέγα τοῦ θεοῦ — to the great supper of God: the deliberate anti-type to the marriage supper of the Lamb (v. 9); both are feasts, but one is the feast of redemption and the other the feast of judgment (from Ezek 39:17–20, the great sacrificial feast on the mountains of Israel after the defeat of Gog).
19.18: σάρκας βασιλέων ... σάρκας χιλιάρχων ... σάρκας ἰσχυρῶν — flesh of kings, flesh of commanders, flesh of the mighty: the social order is completely inverted; those who ordered the deaths of the saints are themselves food for carrion birds. ἐλευθέρων τε καί δούλων — free and slave: the social distinction that structured Roman life (free, freed, slave — the three legal categories) is irrelevant in divine judgment; every social rank is equally subject to the rider's word.
19.19: συνηγμένα ποιῆσαι τόν πόλεμον μετά τοῦ καθημένου ἐπί τοῦ ἵππου — gathered to make war against the rider on the horse (perfect passive participle synēgmena: the state of having been gathered, implying a prior act of gathering): the passive voice asks "by whom?" and implies divine agency in summoning even the beast's final coalition. The gathering of nations against YHWH and his anointed (Ps 2:1–2) and the Gog-Magog oracle (Ezek 38–39) both underlie this final confrontation.
19.20: ἐπιάσθη τό θηρίον — the beast was captured (aorist passive: a single completed divine act); no battle is described, no struggle narrated; the capture is absolute and immediate. ὁ ψευδοπροφήτης ὁ ποιήσας τά σημεῖα ἐνώπιον αὐτοῦ — the false prophet who performed the signs before it: the beast of 13:11–17 is here definitively identified as a false prophet; his signs are exposed as deception, not divine authentication. ζῶντες ἐβλήθησαν — they were thrown alive (without death, without the intermediate state): the immediate transition to the lake of fire without passing through death and Hades marks their judgment as categorically different from the general judgment of chapter 20.
19.21: ἀπεκτάνθησαν ἐν τῇ ῥομφαίᾳ τοῦ καθημένου ἐπί τοῦ ἵππου τῇ ἐξελθούσῃ ἐκ τοῦ στόματος αὐτοῦ — were killed by the sword of the one seated on the horse, the sword going out of his mouth: the instrument of judgment is specified — not a physical military weapon but the word of Christ (the rhomphaia from his mouth, v. 15); the victory is accomplished by divine speech before any conventional battle is joined. ἐχορτάσθησαν πάντα τά ὄρνεα ἐκ τῶν σαρκῶν αὐτῶν — all the birds were gorged on their flesh (echortasthēsan = were filled to satisfaction, aorist passive of chortazō): the prophetic bird-feast of Ezek 39:17–20 is fulfilled; Gog's armies are consumed in the very manner the oracle promised.

20.1: τήν κλεῖν τῆς ἀβύσσου — the key of the Abyss: the Abyss (abyssos) is the demonic underworld-prison (9:1–2, 11; 11:7; 17:8); the key signals total authority over access. Christ holds "the keys of Death and Hades" (1:18); this angel acts with delegated authority from the one who holds all keys. ἅλυσιν μεγάλην — a great chain: the imagery of supernatural binding echoes 1 Enoch 18:16; 21:3–6 (fallen angels bound in chains of darkness in a place of desolation); Jewish apocalyptic angelology provided the conceptual framework for constraining demonic powers.
20.2: ἐκράτησεν τόν δράκοντα — seized the dragon (aorist: a single decisive act); the fourfold identification follows: ho ophis ho archaios (the ancient serpent = Gen 3), ho diabolos (the devil = slanderer/accuser, the Greek translation of Hebrew satan), ho Satanas (Satan = the adversary). All four titles converge on one entity, exposing the continuity of the enemy across the entire biblical narrative. χίλια ἔτη — a thousand years: the term "millennium" derives from Latin mille (thousand) + annum (year); whether the period is literal or symbolic is the central dispute between premillennialism, amillennialism, and postmillennialism; Revelation's Greek does not adjudicate this debate but frames the binding as purposeful (v. 3: so that he could no longer deceive the nations) and temporary.
20.3: ἔβαλεν ... ἔκλεισεν ... ἐσφράγισεν — threw, shut, sealed (three aorists in sequence): the three verbs indicate progressive intensity of confinement; the sealing (sphragizō) echoes Matt 27:66 (the sealing of Jesus's tomb by the authorities) — ironic reversal: the one whose tomb was sealed now seals the Abyss. ἵνα μή πλανήσῃ τά ἔθνη ἔτι — so that he might no longer deceive the nations: planaō (to lead astray, deceive) is the dragon's defining activity throughout Revelation (12:9; 20:8, 10); the binding is purposeful — neutralizing the deceiver's capacity to mislead. δεῖ αὐτόν λυθῆναι μικρόν χρόνον — it is necessary for him to be released for a little while: the divine dei (must/it is necessary) frames even the devil's release as serving the divine purpose.
20.4: κρίμα ἐδόθη αὐτοῖς — judgment was given to them (divine passive: God granted judicial authority): the saints who were judged by earthly tribunals now hold judicial authority themselves; the reversal of Dan 7:22 (judgment was given for the holy ones of the Most High). τάς ψυχάς τῶν πεπελεκισμένων — the souls of those beheaded (pelekizō = to behead with an axe; only here in the NT): the axe-beheading was the Roman method of capital punishment for condemned enemies of the state; the specific instrument of the martyrs' deaths becomes the credential for their co-reign. ἔζησαν καί ἐβασίλευσαν μετά τοῦ Χριστοῦ χίλια ἔτη — they lived and reigned with Christ for a thousand years: ezēsan (they lived = resurrection verb); the first resurrection is participation in Christ's reign.
20.5: οἱ λοιποί τῶν νεκρῶν οὐκ ἔζησαν ἄχρι τελεσθῇ τά χίλια ἔτη — the rest of the dead did not live until the thousand years were completed: the two-stage resurrection implied here — the martyred saints reign first (vv. 4, 6 = the "first resurrection"), then the general resurrection at the end (vv. 12–13) before the great white throne. αὕτη ἡ ἀνάστασις ἡ πρώτη — this is the first resurrection: the definite article and demonstrative (hautē = this one specifically) identify what was just described (the living-and-reigning of the martyrs with Christ) as "the first resurrection"; the term is unique to this passage in the NT.
20.6: μακάριος καί ἅγιος ὁ ἔχων μέρος ἐν τῇ ἀναστάσει τῇ πρώτῃ — blessed and holy is the one having a share in the first resurrection: the fifth beatitude of Revelation; makarios (blessed, in the condition of divine favor) + hagios (holy, set apart for God) together denote the eschatological status of those in the first resurrection. ὁ δεύτερος θάνατος οὐκ ἔχει ἐξουσίαν ἐπ' αὐτούς — the second death has no authority over them: exousia (authority, delegated power); the second death (defined at 20:14 as the lake of fire) is itself personified as having authority that it exercises over the condemned — but not over those who share the first resurrection. ἱερεῖς τοῦ θεοῦ καί τοῦ Χριστοῦ — priests of God and of Christ: the priestly identity of the redeemed (Exod 19:6; Rev 1:6; 5:10) extends through the millennium — direct, unmediated access to the divine presence.
20.7: ὅταν τελεσθῇ τά χίλια ἔτη — when the thousand years are completed (aorist subjunctive: at the divinely fixed point of completion; teleō = to bring to its designed end): the duration is not open-ended but has a purpose-defined terminus. λυθήσεται ὁ Σατανᾶς ἐκ τῆς φυλακῆς αὐτοῦ — Satan will be released from his prison (future passive: the same divine agency that imprisoned also releases, for purposes that serve the plan — cf. v. 3's dei [it is necessary]). The prison language (phylakē) frames the Abyss as a detention, not a permanent sentence; the final judgment of 20:10 is the permanent sentence.
20.8: πλανῆσαι τά ἔθνη — to deceive the nations (aorist infinitive of purpose): Satan's resumed activity after release is identical to his pre-binding activity (planaō = to lead astray; cf. 12:9; 20:3, 10); the millennium's end does not reform Satan. τόν Γώγ καί Μαγώγ — Gog and Magog: from Ezek 38–39 where Gog of Magog leads a great coalition against the restored Israel; in Revelation these names function symbolically for all anti-God forces gathered for the final assault (cf. Sib. Or. 3.319, 512). ὁ ἀριθμός αὐτῶν ὡς ἡ ἄμμος τῆς θαλάσσης — their number like the sand of the sea: the Abrahamic covenant promise (Gen 22:17: descendants like the sand of the sea) is grotesquely parodied; the final anti-God army matches the promised people in number.
20.9: ἀνέβησαν ἐπί τό πλάτος τῆς γῆς — they went up over the breadth/width of the earth: platos (breadth) implies the army spreads comprehensively across all the earth. τήν παρεμβολήν τῶν ἁγίων καί τήν πόλιν τήν ἠγαπημένην — the camp of the holy ones and the beloved city: parembolē (military encampment; the people of God figured as the wilderness camp of Exodus); hē ēgapēmenē polis (the beloved city) echoes Ps 78:68; 87:2 for Jerusalem — the eschatological people of God figured both as a mobile camp and a beloved city. κατέβη πῦρ ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καί κατέφαγεν αὐτούς — fire came down from God out of heaven and consumed them: no battle, no struggle; the divine fire alone destroys the vast army in a moment (Elijah's pattern, 2 Kgs 1:10–12, at cosmic scale).
20.10: ὁ διάβολος ὁ πλανῶν αὐτούς ἐβλήθη εἰς τήν λίμνην τοῦ πυρός καί θείου — the devil who had been deceiving them was thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur (theion = sulfur/brimstone; the sulfurous fire recalls the destruction of Sodom, Gen 19:24, and the judgment oracles of Isa 30:33; 34:9). ὅπου καί τό θηρίον καί ὁ ψευδοπροφήτης — where also the beast and the false prophet: already there since 19:20; the unholy trinity (dragon + beast + false prophet = Satan + political power + religious propaganda) converge in the same final state. βασανισθήσονται ἡμέρας καί νυκτός εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων — will be tormented day and night forever and ever: the strongest formulation of duration available in Greek, matching the eternal smoke of 14:11.
20.11: θρόνον λευκόν μέγαν — a great white throne: white (leukos) throughout Revelation signals holiness, victory, and divine purity; this throne is neither the twenty-four elders' thrones (4:4) nor the beast's throne (16:10) but the supreme judicial tribunal. ἔφυγεν ἡ γῆ καί ὁ οὐρανός ἀπό τοῦ προσώπου αὐτοῦ — earth and heaven fled from his face: the dissolution of the present created order before the Judge; the flight of heaven and earth anticipates the new heaven and new earth (21:1) and signals the inadequacy of the present order to sustain direct divine presence. τόπος οὐχ εὑρέθη αὐτοῖς — no place was found for them (heurethē = divine passive: was found); the old creation has nowhere to exist in the presence of the holy Judge.
20.12: νεκρούς τούς μεγάλους καί τούς μικρούς — the dead the great and the small: universal scope; no social hierarchy, no temporal power, no religious status alters standing before this throne. βιβλία ἠνοίχθησαν — books were opened (divine passive): the heavenly records of human deeds; cf. Dan 7:10 ("the books were opened") in the Son-of-Man vision. βίβλος τῆς ζωῆς — the book of life: the single book of life stands alongside the many books of works; throughout Revelation the book of life is the register of those who belong to the Lamb (3:5; 13:8; 17:8; 20:12, 15; 21:27). ἐκρίθησαν κατά τά ἔργα αὐτῶν — they were judged according to their works: the works are the evidence of character and allegiance, not the meritorious basis of salvation; the book of life (grace) and the books of works (accountability) together constitute the judgment.
20.13: ἔδωκεν ἡ θάλασσα τούς νεκρούς τούς ἐν αὐτῇ — the sea gave up the dead in it: the sea was a place of the dead in ancient cosmology (Ps 88:5–6; Job 26:5); death at sea — with no recoverable body — does not exempt from resurrection. ὁ θάνατος καί ὁ ᾅδης ἔδωκαν — Death and Hades gave up: Death and Hades are personified powers (6:8; 20:14) that temporarily hold the dead; at the general resurrection, they must surrender every person they hold. The threefold source of the dead (sea, Death, Hades) is comprehensive — every category of the dead is raised for judgment. κατά τά ἔργα αὐτῶν — according to their works: the refrain of universal accountability.
20.14: ὁ θάνατος καί ὁ ᾅδης ἐβλήθησαν εἰς τήν λίμνην τοῦ πυρός — Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire (aorist passive: divine act): the personified powers of death are themselves subjected to final judgment; after the general resurrection, they have no more function and are permanently destroyed. οὗτος ὁ θάνατος ὁ δεύτερός ἐστιν ἡ λίμνη τοῦ πυρός — this is the second death, the lake of fire: the definitional statement; ho thanatos ho deuteros (the second death) has precedent in Aramaic/rabbinic tradition (Targum on Deut 33:6; y. Ber. 9:3; the concept of a "second death" for the wicked appears in pre-Christian Aramaic texts), making the term likely familiar to John's Jewish-background readers.
20.15: εἴ τις οὐχ εὑρέθη ἐν τῇ βίβλῳ τῆς ζωῆς γεγραμμένος — if anyone was not found written in the book of life: heurethē (divine passive: was found by the divine searcher); gegramenos (perfect passive participle: having been written, enrolled in the permanent record — the perfect tense emphasizing the enduring state of enrollment). The conditional form (ei tis = if anyone) applies the criterion universally without naming individuals. ἐβλήθη εἰς τήν λίμνην τοῦ πυρός — was thrown into the lake of fire (aorist passive: the final judicial sentence): the passive voices throughout the judgment sequence (was found, was judged, was thrown) keep divine agency central while the human record (the books of works) provides the evidentiary basis.

12.1: sēmeion mega ōphthē en tō ouranō: "a great sign appeared in heaven." Sēmeion (sign, portent) signals that what follows is symbolic vision, not literal astronomy. Revelation has used sēmeion for its major symbolic visions (12:1, 3; 15:1). The woman clothed with sun, moon under her feet, and twelve stars: the imagery of Gen 37:9 (Joseph's dream — sun, moon, and eleven stars bowing to him) now applied to the covenant people as a cosmic figure. The twelve stars = twelve tribes of Israel, though in Revelation's typological reading, the woman is simultaneously OT Israel (through whom the Messiah came) and the NT church (the ongoing covenant community).
12.2: ōdinousan kai basanizomenēn tekein: in labor pains (ōdinousan) and torment (basanizomenēn) to give birth. The birth-pang imagery activates Isa 66:7-8 (before she was in labor she gave birth; before her pain came upon her she delivered a son — the miraculous Zion-birth) and Mic 5:2-3 (she who is in labor will give birth, and the rest of his brothers will return). These OT texts describe Zion's eschatological birth of the Messiah. The pain signals that the birth is historical, not triumphalist — the covenant community suffers in bringing forth the Messiah.
12.3: drakon megas pyrros: a great red dragon. Drakon in the LXX regularly translates Hebrew תַּנִּין (tannin), the sea-monster of primordial chaos (Ps 74:13-14; Isa 27:1; 51:9; Ezek 29:3). The seven heads and ten horns combine features from Daniel's four beasts (Dan 7:3-7 — lion, bear, leopard, terrible beast with ten horns) into one super-beast. The seven diadems (hepta diademata) are royal crowns — the dragon claims the sovereignty that belongs to God alone. The color red (pyrros) signals bloodshed and violence.
12.4: hē oura autou syrei to triton tōn asterōn tou ouranou: his tail drags (syrei, drags along forcibly) a third of the stars and hurls them to earth. Stars in Revelation and Daniel can represent angelic beings (Dan 8:10; Job 38:7); the dragon's expulsion of a third of the heavenly host is the mythological background for the origin of demonic beings — the fallen angels who follow Satan's rebellion. The dragon's positioning (hestēken, standing, present tense: stationed himself) before the woman is the posture of the predator waiting to devour the newborn — echoing Pharaoh's infanticide (Exod 1:15-22) and Herod's (Matt 2:16).
12.5:
arsen: the masculine form emphasizing the child's gender identity as the Messiah-king. Ho mellōn poimainein panta ta ethnē en rhabdō sidēra (who is to rule all the nations with a rod of iron) — the direct citation of Ps 2:9, the royal enthronement psalm that speaks of the anointed king's dominion. The male child's destiny is established by psalm before he acts.

hērpasthē pros ton theon: hērpasthē (aorist passive of harpazō) — was snatched up, caught away. The Ascension compressed into one violent verb: Christ's exaltation is not a peaceful gradual lifting but an eschatological rescue from the dragon's reach. The rapture of the child to God's throne (pros ton theon kai pros ton thronon autou) bypasses the entire earthly ministry to focus on the Incarnation-Death-Resurrection-Ascension as one act of deliverance.
12.6:
eis tēn erēmon: the wilderness — the Exodus typology. The protection of the woman in the wilderness echoes God's protection of Israel in the Sinai wilderness (Exod 19:4; Deut 32:10-12; Isa 40:3; Jer 31:2). The erēmos in Revelation is not desolation but the place of divine preparation and preservation. Ετοίμασεν (prepared) — God prepared the place, not circumstance: the community's protection is by divine provision, not human ingenuity.

chilias diakosioi hexēkonta hēmerai: 1,260 days = 42 months = 3½ years = "a time, times, and half a time" (Dan 7:25; 12:7). This figure from Daniel represents the duration of the Antichrist's oppression — the eschatological half-week (half of Daniel's symbolic seven). In Revelation it frames the entire period between Christ's ascension and parousia as simultaneously the time of the beast's apparent triumph and the community's protected tribulation.
12.7: Michaēl kai hoi angeloi autou: Michael and his angels. Michaēl = Hebrew מִיכָאֵל (Mika'el, "who is like God?") — the archangel who is Israel's guardian prince in Dan 10:13, 21; 12:1, and in Jude 9 fights over Moses' body. The war in heaven (polemos en tō ouranō) is not a cosmological event prior to creation but a consequence of the Messiah's ascension (v. 5): Christ's exaltation to God's throne triggers the expulsion of the accuser from the heavenly court. The timing is theologically precise: the war follows the coronation.
12.8: ouk ischysen: he was not strong enough — a flat, unqualified statement of the dragon's defeat. Ouk ischysen (imperfect of ischyō: he kept not being strong enough, he proved insufficient). Oude topos heurethē autois eti en tō ouranō: no place (topos) was found for them any longer in heaven. The denial of topos is significant: in Job 1-2, Satan had ongoing access to the heavenly court. The Resurrection-Ascension removes this access permanently — the accuser is evicted from the divine courtroom where he prosecuted the saints.
12.9: ho ophis ho archaios: the ancient (archaios = original, from the beginning) serpent — explicitly identifying the dragon with the Eden serpent of Gen 3. Revelation's interpretive key: the cosmic dragon behind world history is the same creature that deceived Eve. The three titles given: diabolos (devil = slanderer, false accuser), Satanas (from Hebrew שָׂטָן, satan = adversary, accuser), ho planōn tēn oikoumenēn holēn (the one who deceives the whole inhabited world — present participle: ongoing deception). The Greek diabolos and Hebrew satan are nearly synonymous: both mean adversarial accuser. Revelation gives both names to mark continuity across both testaments.
12.10: katēgōr tōn adelphōn hēmōn: the accuser (katēgōr) of our brothers. Katēgōr is a rare form (also katēgoros elsewhere), possibly a Greek loan from Aramaic katēgora. The picture is legal: the dragon has been prosecuting the saints before God's court day and night (hēmeras kai nyktos). This matches the Job 1-2 and Zech 3:1 pattern where the adversary brings charges against God's people before the divine throne. The defeat announced: eblēthē (was thrown down, aorist: a completed expulsion) — the accuser has been permanently removed from his prosecutorial role.
12.11: enikēsan auton dia to haima tou Arniou kai dia ton logon tēs martyrias autōn: they conquered him by two instruments — the blood of the Lamb (objective) and the word of their testimony (subjective). The Lamb's blood answers every charge the accuser can bring; the saints' faithful testimony (even unto death) corroborates the verdict. Ouk ēgapēsan tēn psychēn autōn achri thanatou: they did not love their psychē (life-force, not just the immortal soul) to the point of death — the paradox of martyrdom: by losing their lives they win the war. The ψυχή they sacrifice is precisely what the dragon attacks.
12.12: euphrainesthē hoi ouranoi: let the heavens rejoice — plural imperative addressed to the heavenly realm. The doubled response: rejoicing for heaven, ouai (woe) for the earth and sea. Ouai is the lament/woe cry used in prophetic oracles (Isa 5:8-23; Amos 5:18; Matt 23). The dragon's expulsion means heaven's peace but earth's intensified conflict: katebē ho diabolos pros hymas echōn thymon megan eidōs hoti oligon kairon echei (knowing he has a short time). Thymos (rage, fury — also the word for the wrath of God in the bowl judgments) now characterizes the defeated dragon: fury born from desperation, not power.
12.13: ote eiden ho drakōn hoti eblēthē eis tēn gēn: when the dragon saw (eidon) that he was thrown to the earth, he pursued (ediōxen) the woman. The dragon's response to defeat is not repentance but intensified persecution — the knowledge of defeat does not produce humility but rage. The pursuit of the woman on earth = the historical persecution of the covenant community after Christ's ascension. The dragon can no longer access the heavenly court so redirects his hostility to earth.
12.14: hai dyo pteryx tou aetou tou megalou: the two wings of the great eagle — directly quoting the Exodus deliverance language of Exod 19:4 (I bore you on eagles' wings and brought you to myself) and Deut 32:11 (like an eagle that stirs up its nest, that hovers over its young, spreading out its wings, catching them, bearing them on its pinions). The eagle-wings = divine protection and transport through tribulation. The wilderness period (time, times, and half a time = 3½ years = the same duration as v. 6) repeats: the protected period is the entire church age, not a literal 3½ years.
12.15: ebalen ho ophis ek tou stomatos autou opisō tēs gynaikos hydōr hōs potamon: the serpent poured water (hydōr) like a river from his mouth after the woman. The flood of persecution — the river (potamos) as weapon of destruction echoes Ps 18:4 (the torrents of destruction assailed me), Ps 124:4-5 (then the flood would have swept us away), and Isa 43:2 (when you pass through the rivers, they shall not overwhelm you). The serpent's flood = the overwhelming power of empire-backed persecution attempting to sweep away the covenant community.
12.16: ēnoixen hē gē to stoma autēs kai katepien ton potamon: the earth opened its mouth and swallowed the river. Katepien (swallowed) — the same verb used in Num 16:30, 32, 34 for the earth swallowing Korah's company who rebelled against Moses. The earth, which could be a venue for persecution, here becomes the protector of the covenant community — creation itself defends the people of God. The subversion of the serpent's plot by the creature he had corrupted carries ironic theological weight.
12.17: to loipon tou spermatos autēs: the rest of her offspring (sperma = seed). The seed (sperma) language deliberately echoes Gen 3:15 (I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your seed and her seed). Revelation identifies the church as the woman's seed — those who keep the commandments of God and hold the testimony of Jesus (echontōn tēn martyrian Iēsou). The dragon's war against the rest of the seed is the ongoing conflict of Gen 3:15, now understood as the persecution of Christ's followers.

14.1: to Arnion hestōs epi to oros Siōn: the Lamb standing (hestōs — perfect participle, standing in the resurrection state, same as 5:6) on Mount Zion. The contrast with 13:1 (beast from the sea) is deliberate: where the beast rises from the chaos of the sea, the Lamb stands on Zion — the OT eschatological locus of divine deliverance (Ps 2:6; 48:2; Isa 4:5; Joel 2:32; Obad 17, 21; Mic 4:7). Zion was the place where God enthroned his king (Ps 2:6); now the Lamb stands there as the enthroned conqueror. The 144,000 with the Lamb bear to onoma autou kai to onoma tou patros autou (his name and his Father's name) on their foreheads — the seal of divine ownership contrasting with the beast's mark.
14.2: phōnēn ek tou ouranou hōs phōnēn hydatōn pollōn kai hōs phōnēn brontēs megalēs: "like the sound of many waters and of loud thunder." This triple simile for the heavenly song echoes Ezek 1:24 (the sound of many waters, like the voice of the Almighty, when they moved). The sound is described before the singers are identified, emphasizing the overwhelming auditory reality of the heavenly worship. Hōs kitharōdōn kitharizontōn en tais kitharais autōn: like harpers harping — the harp (kithara) is the instrument of praise (Ps 33:2; 150:3).
14.3: ōdēn kainēn: a new song. The "new song" (ōdē kainē) in the Psalter (Ps 33:3; 40:3; 96:1; 98:1; 144:9; 149:1; Isa 42:10) always celebrates a new act of divine salvation — a song specific to this redemption, not a recycled doxology. Oudeis edynato mathein tēn ōdēn: no one could learn (manthanō, to learn by study and practice) the song except the 144,000 — because the song expresses their specific experience. Redemption gives knowledge that cannot be acquired otherwise; only the redeemed-through-tribulation can sing the redeemed-through-tribulation song.
14.4:
houtoi eisin hoi meta gynaikōn ouk emolynthēsan parthenoi gar eisin: these are those who have not defiled themselves with women, for they are virgins. The porneia/purity language in Revelation is consistently used for spiritual apostasy and idolatry (the great prostitute of ch. 17; Jezebel in 2:20-22; Babylon making all nations drink the wine of her sexual immorality). The 144,000's virgin purity (parthenoi) = they have not committed spiritual adultery with the beast-cult. In prophetic literature, idolatry is consistently framed as harlotry (Hos 1-3; Ezek 16; 23; Jer 2-3). Literal celibacy is possible but less consistent with Revelation's symbolic register.

aparchē: firstfruits — the 144,000 are the firstfruits (aparchē) of God's great harvest. The harvest metaphor unites the 144,000 (firstfruits, v. 4) with the great grain and grape harvest of vv. 14-20: they are the beginning of the full redeemed humanity.
14.5: en tō stomati autōn ouch heurethē pseudos: in their mouth no lie was found. Pseudos (falsehood, lie) is the defining characteristic of the beast's false prophet (13:14: plana) and of those excluded from the new Jerusalem (21:27; 22:15). The 144,000's freedom from falsehood marks them as authentic witnesses — their testimony (martyria) is truthful because they have not compromised with the beast's deceptions. Amōmoi: blameless, without blemish — the same word used for the unblemished sacrificial animal (Num 19:2 LXX; Heb 9:14; 1 Pet 1:19). The 144,000 are themselves the spotless offering — they are both worshippers and the sacrifice.
14.6: euangelion aiōnion: the eternal gospel (euangelion aiōnion) — the only occurrence of this phrase in the NT. Aiōnios (eternal, age-spanning) qualifies the gospel as transcending any particular historical moment: the good news of God's reign is not time-conditioned. The angel proclaims it to epi panta ethnē kai phylas kai glōssas kai laous (every nation, tribe, tongue, and people) — the fourfold universal formula from Dan 3:7 LXX, marking the complete universality of the proclamation. The scope of the gospel matches the scope of the beast's dominion (13:7) — every people group hears.
14.7: phobēthēte ton theon kai dote autō doxan: Fear God and give him glory — two imperatives establishing the fundamental posture of true worship. Phobeomai (reverential awe, not mere terror) and didōmi doxan (give glory, attribute honour) are both explicitly commanded in contrast to the earth-dwellers' reflexive glorification of the beast (13:4). Hoti ēlthen hē hōra tēs kriseōs autou: the hour of his judgment has come — ēlthen aorist: it has arrived. The eschatological hour is here, not impending. The gospel call goes out precisely because judgment is imminent: the euangelion and the krisis are simultaneous.
14.8: Epesen, epesen Babulōn hē megalē: "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great" — the doubled epesen (fell, fell) reproduces exactly the LXX form of Isa 21:9 (fallen is Babylon; and all the carved images of her gods he has shattered to the ground) and Jer 51:8 (suddenly Babylon has fallen and been broken). The prophetic perfect: the fall is announced as already accomplished from the heavenly perspective before it is historically described in chs. 17-18. Hē ek tou oinou tou thymou tēs porneias autēs pepotiken panta ta ethnē: the wine of the passion of her sexual immorality — Babylon's seduction is economic and religious intoxication that makes resistance seem impossible.
14.9: ei tis proskunei to thērion kai tēn eikona autou: if anyone worships the beast and its image — the condition for receiving God's wrath. The three-part object (beast, image, mark) = the full apparatus of the beast-cult: the political power (beast), the religious apparatus (image), and the economic integration (mark). Worship of any one implies the full system. The third angel's proclamation (vv. 9-11) is the most severe warning in Revelation, matching the severity of the beast's claim.
14.10: pinei ek tou oinou tou thymou tou theou tou kekerasmenou akratou: he will drink the wine of God's wrath poured full strength (akraton = unmixed, undiluted — wine was normally diluted with water; undiluted wine = maximum potency). The cup of divine wrath from Ps 75:8; Isa 51:17; Jer 25:15-17 is here poured akraton — no dilution, no mitigation. En pyri kai theiō: with fire and sulfur — the Sodom judgment formula (Gen 19:24; Ezek 38:22). The torment is not incidental but the just response to beast-worship.
14.11: ho kapnos tou basanismou autōn eis aiōnas aiōnōn anabainei: the smoke of their torment ascends forever and ever. This echoes Isa 34:10 (Edom's judgment: its smoke shall go up forever; from generation to generation it shall lie waste) applied now to those who worship the beast at the final judgment. Ouk echousin anapausin hēmeras kai nyktos: they have no rest (anapausis) day or night — the permanent inversion of the Sabbath rest promised to the faithful (14:13). The eternal persistence of judgment is stated here with the strongest language in the NT.
14.12: hōde hē hypomonē tōn hagiōn estin: here (hōde) is the patient endurance of the saints. Hypomonē (steadfast endurance, active perseverance under pressure) is the virtue that connects 14:9-11 to 14:12: the eternal severity of judgment on beast-worshippers is the theological ground for the saints' endurance. Knowing what beast-worship costs (eternally) makes the temporal cost of refusal bearable. Hoi tērountes tas entolas tou theou kai tēn pistin Iēsou: those who keep God's commandments and the faith of Jesus — both commitments define the saint who endures.
14.13: Makarioi hoi nekroi hoi en Kyriō apothnēskontes ap' arti: "Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on." The third beatitude in Revelation (1:3; 14:13; 16:15; 19:9; 20:6; 22:7, 14). Ap' arti (from now on) — specifically the martyrs of the eschatological tribulation. Nai, legei to Pneuma: Yes, says the Spirit — the Spirit authenticates the beatitude with divine affirmation. Hina anapausontai ek tōn kopōn autōn: they rest (anapauō) from their labors — the same rest denied to beast-worshippers (v. 11). The two destinies: rest for the faithful dead, no rest for beast-worshippers.
14.14: homoion hyion anthrōpou: one like a son of man — the Danielic figure of Dan 7:13 (one like a son of man came with the clouds of heaven and came to the Ancient of Days) now enthroned on a white cloud. The golden crown (stephanos chrysous) signals royal authority; the sharp sickle (drepanon oxy) is the harvest tool. The figure is Christ himself — combining Dan 7's Son of Man with Joel 3:13 (put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe). The harvest image from the Parable of the Weeds (Matt 13:39-43) is here expanded to cosmic scale.
14.15: ēlthen hē hōra therisai: the hour to reap has come — ēlthen aorist: arrived. Exēranthē ho therismos tēs gēs: the harvest of the earth is ripe/dried out. Xērainō (to dry, wither) applied to grain means it has reached full maturity and is ready to harvest — not rotting but fully ripe. The harvest language echoes Isa 17:5 (the gleanings of the grain), Joel 3:13 (the harvest is ripe, go in and tread, for the winepress is full), and Matt 13:39-43. This grain harvest (vv. 15-16) represents the gathering of the righteous.
14.16: ebalen ho kathēmenos epi tēs nephelēs to drepanon autou epi tēn gēn: the one seated on the cloud swung (ebalon) his sickle over the earth, and the earth was reaped (etheristhē). The brevity of the description signals the sovereignty and efficiency of divine judgment: a single act, worldwide scope, instantaneous completion. Etheristhē — the harvest passive: the earth is harvested as the object, not the agent. The great reaping of history comes to completion in one decisive act.
14.17: allos angelos exēlthen ek tou naou tou en tō ouranō: another angel came out of the temple (naos) in heaven. The grape harvest angel (vv. 17-20) is distinct from the one-like-a-son-of-man (vv. 14-16), and both come from the heavenly temple — the source of the eschatological harvest is divine, authorized from the heavenly sanctuary. The naos (inner sanctuary) houses the divine presence; these angels emerge from the place of God's holiness to execute the harvest of judgment.
14.18: allos angelos ek tou thusiastēriou: another angel from the altar (thusiastērion). This is the altar that holds the souls of the martyred (6:9-10) — those who cried out for justice. The angel who has authority over fire (exousian epi tou pyros) calls for the grape harvest. The fire-angel from the altar of incense (8:3-5) earlier hurled fire to earth as divine judgment; now the altar-angel summons the grape harvest of wrath. The martyrs' cry for justice (6:10: "How long?") is being answered.
14.19: tēn lēnon tou thymou tou theou ton megan: the great winepress of the fury (thymos) of God — the OT winepress of judgment from Isa 63:3 (I have trodden the winepress alone; from the peoples no one was with me) and Joel 3:13 (tread, for the winepress is full). The one who treads the winepress in Isa 63 is YHWH himself, the divine warrior; in Rev 19:15 Christ treads the winepress. The judgment is not impersonal force but the direct action of the holy God against evil.
14.20: exō tēs poleōs: outside the city. In Isa 63:1-6, the divine warrior comes from Edom, outside Jerusalem. More significantly, Christ was crucified outside the city walls (John 19:20; Heb 13:12-13) — the blood of Calvary shed outside Jerusalem prefigures the blood of divine judgment poured out outside. Haima ek tēs lēnou achri tōn chalinōn tōn hippōn apo stadiōn chiliadōn hexakosiōn deka tessarōn: blood up to the horses' bridles for 1,600 stadia. 1,600 = 4 × 4 × 100 — the number four (four corners of the earth) squared and magnified a hundredfold: universal, complete, maximal judgment.

15.1: sēmeion allo en tō ouranō mega kai thaumaston: another great and marvelous sign in heaven. Thaumaston (marvelous, wonderful) — the same word used for the works of God in v. 3. The seven last plagues complete (etelesthē — aorist passive, proleptic announcement: it stands completed) the wrath of God. Etelesthē is the same root as Jesus' tetelestai ("it is finished," John 19:30) — the completion of wrath corresponds to the completion of atonement. The seven bowls are the final expression of divine judgment, bookending history with the cross.
15.2: thalassan hyalinēn memigmenēn pyri: a sea of glass (thalassa hyalinē, 4:6) now mixed with fire (memigmenēn pyri). In 4:6 the sea of glass was crystal-clear, before the throne, symbolizing divine transcendence and purity. Now mixed with fire — the community of overcomers stands on it, having passed through the fire of tribulation. Kithāras tou theou: harps of God — instruments of praise (Ps 33:2; 150:3; 1 Chr 25:6). The victors stand (hestōtas) in triumph with the instrument of worship.
15.3: ōdēn Mōyseōs tou doulou tou theou kai tēn ōdēn tou Arniou: the song of Moses the servant of God and the song of the Lamb — a unified song, not two songs. The Song of Moses (Exod 15) celebrated Israel's deliverance through the Red Sea; the Song of the Lamb celebrates the new exodus through the great tribulation. Both deliverances are celebrated as one: the same God who split the sea saves his people from every beast. The content is a mosaic of OT texts: Megala kai thaumasta ta erga sou, Kyrie ho theos ho Pantokratōr (Ps 111:2; 139:14) — Dikaiai kai alēthinai hai hodoi sou (Deut 32:4; Ps 145:17).
15.4: Tis ou mē phobēthē, Kyrie, kai doxasei to onoma sou: "Who will not fear you, O Lord, and glorify your name?" — the rhetorical question from Jer 10:7 (Who would not fear you, O King of the nations? For this is your due). Hoti monos hosios: for you alone are holy (monos hosios) — a claim that none of the beast's divine titles can match. Panta ta ethnē hēxousin kai proskunēsousin enōpion sou: all nations will come and worship before you — the eschatological universalism of Ps 86:9; Isa 66:23; Zeph 2:11. The song anticipates the fulfillment before the bowls that will bring it about.
15.5: ho naos tēs skēnēs tou martyriou en tō ouranō ēnoigē: the temple of the tabernacle of testimony in heaven was opened. Skēnē tou martyriou is specifically the wilderness tabernacle — the tent of meeting that housed the ark of the covenant with the tablets of the law (testimony). The opening of the heavenly tabernacle reveals its contents: the seven angels with the seven plagues emerge from inside. The judgment proceeds from within the holy place — from the law's demands being met by the wrath that the law prescribes for covenant violation.
15.6: endedymenoi linon katharon lampron kai periezōsmenoi peri ta stēthē zōnas chrysas: clothed in pure bright linen with golden sashes. The priestly garments echo Dan 10:5 (the heavenly man clothed in linen with a belt of fine gold). The seven angels are priest-warriors: they come from the temple (v. 6) dressed in priestly regalia to pour out judgment. The phialai (bowls) they carry are themselves priestly vessels — wide, shallow libation bowls used in temple worship. The judgment has a liturgical character: it is the divine worship service of holy war.
15.7: phialas chrysas gemas tou thymou tou theou tou zōntos eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn: golden bowls (phialai) full of the wrath (thymos) of the God who lives forever and ever. Phialē is a wide, shallow bowl used for libations — the shape signals that the contents will be poured quickly and completely, not slowly dispensed. The wrath belongs to the living God (tou zōntos eis tous aiōnas) — in contrast to the dead idols of the beast-cult. The living God's wrath is executed by the living God: it has eschatological permanence and moral authority.
15.8: eplēsthē ho naos kapnou ek tēs doxēs tou theou kai ek tēs dynameōs autou: the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God and from his power. This is the smoke-of-Theophany from Isa 6:4 (the house was filled with smoke at YHWH's appearance) and Exod 40:34-35 (Moses was unable to enter the tent of meeting because the cloud settled on it and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle). The divine presence overwhelms the temple — making it inaccessible. Oudeis edynato eiselthein eis ton naon: no one could enter until the seven plagues were completed. The theology of holy war: God fights alone; no human intercession is possible or needed during the final judgment. The closed temple = the completion of the age of intercession.

16.1: megalēs ek tou naou legousēs tois hepta angelois: a loud voice from the temple commanding the seven angels. The voice from the temple (ek tou naou) — the divine command originates from the inaccessible sanctuary of 15:8. The seven bowls are poured at explicit divine command, not on the angels' initiative. The bowl judgments are the most direct expression of divine wrath in Revelation: no human or angelic mediating action, just the direct pouring of God's fury onto the earth.
16.2: helkos kakon kai ponēron: ugly and painful sores (helkos). The Greek word helkos is used in the LXX for the boils of the sixth Egyptian plague (Exod 9:9-11: helkē phlyktidēs, sores of blisters) — the direct connection to the exodus plagues is explicit. The first bowl targets those who have the mark of the beast and worship its image — the judgment is specific and just: those who aligned themselves with the beast system receive the first plague. The Egyptian plagues distinguished between Israel and Egypt (Exod 8:22-23); the bowls distinguish between beast-worshippers and the sealed.
16.3: hōs nekrou: like blood of a dead person — nekros (corpse) modifies the blood: not fresh arterial blood but the thick, dark, clotted blood of a corpse. Echoes the first Egyptian plague (Exod 7:17-21) but amplified: not just one river but the entire sea. Pasa psychē zōēs apethanen: every living soul (psychē zōēs — the Hebraic phrase nefesh chayyah from Gen 1:20-21, 24; 2:7) died. The universal mortality of marine life signals that the old creation's life-sustaining systems are failing under divine judgment.
16.4: egenonto haima: they became blood. The third bowl turns rivers and springs to blood — now the fresh water supply joins the salt water. The escalation from sea to rivers to springs makes the judgment total: no water source is unaffected. Taken together with the second bowl, the entire hydrological system of the planet becomes the instrument of judgment — the waters that sustain life become the blood that witnesses to death.
16.5: dikaios ei, ho ōn kai ho ēn, ho hosios: "You are just, the one who is and who was, the Holy One" — the angel of the waters affirms divine justice using the Revelation formula for God's eternal being (1:4, 8; 4:8). Hosios (holy, set apart for God) applied to God himself — the only being who is intrinsically holy. Hoti tauta ekrinas: because you judged these things — the judgment is legally grounded (krinō), not arbitrary revenge. The angel of the waters (ho angelos tōn hydatōn) testifies from within the judgment he experiences: even the created order acknowledges the justice of its own transformation into blood.
16.6: hoti haima hagiōn kai prophētōn execheran kai haima autois dedōkas pein: "for they poured out the blood of saints and prophets, and you have given them blood to drink — they deserve it." Axios eisin: they are worthy/deserving — the same axios used of the Lamb's worthiness to receive worship (5:12) now applied to the persecutors' worthiness to receive judgment. The lex talionis operates at cosmic scale: those who shed blood receive blood. The rivers-to-blood judgment is the precise inversion of their crime.
16.7: ēkousa tou thusiastēriou legontos: I heard the altar (thusiastērion) saying. The altar speaks — the altar that held the souls of the martyrs (6:9-10) who cried "How long?" now responds. The altar's voice affirms: Nai, Kyrie ho theos ho Pantokratōr, alēthinai kai dikaiai hai kriseis sou (Yes, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are your judgments). The martyred souls have been answered: the God of justice has acted. The altar's response closes the loop opened in 6:9-10.
16.8: edothē autō kaumatisai tous anthrōpous en pyri: it was given to the sun to scorch people with fire. Edothē — the divine passive again: the fourth plague is divinely authorized. The sun, which sustains life, becomes the instrument of judgment — creation's benevolent functions inverted. Kauma mega: intense heat — a judgment that cannot be hidden from or escaped; it falls on all exposed to it.
16.9: eblasphēmēsan to onoma tou theou tou echontos tēn exousian epi tas plēgas tautas kai ou metenoesan dounai autō doxan: they blasphemed the name of God who has authority over these plagues, and they refused to repent to give him glory. The response to judgment is the diagnostic: repentance or hardening. The plague-recipients know the source of the plagues (tou echontos tēn exousian = God) and choose blasphemy over worship. This is Pharaoh's hardening under the Egyptian plagues (Exod 7:3; 9:12; 10:1) reproduced at cosmic scale — the final plagues confirm the final hardening of those who will not repent.
16.10: ton thronon tou thēriou: the throne of the beast — the political power center attacked directly. Egeneto hē basileia autou eskotōmenē: its kingdom was plunged into darkness (skotizō). The ninth Egyptian plague was three days of darkness (Exod 10:21-23); here the darkness falls on the beast's entire kingdom. Emasōnto tas glōssas autōn ek tou ponou: they gnawed their tongues from the pain — an image of anguish with no remedy. The darkness does not produce reflection but intensified suffering.
16.11: eblasphēmēsan ton theon tou ouranou ek tōn ponōn autōn kai ek tōn helkōn autōn kai ou metenoesan ek tōn ergōn autōn: they blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pain and sores, and refused to repent of what they had done. The refusal to repent is repeated (cf. v. 9) as the defining response. Ek tōn ergōn autōn: from their works — repentance would require acknowledging that their works were wrong. The beast-worshippers are defined by the works they have chosen; repentance would mean abandoning the entire framework of identity they have built around the beast-cult. Final judgment hardens rather than softens.
16.12: ton potamon ton megan ton Euphratēn: the great river Euphrates. The drying of the Euphrates to prepare the way for the kings from the east echoes multiple OT passages: Isa 41:2, 25 (the one from the east whom YHWH has raised up); Isa 44:28-45:1 (Cyrus from the east who dries the rivers); the crossing of the Red Sea (Exod 14) and Jordan (Josh 3-4). Hina hetoimasthē hē hodos tōn basileōn tōn apo anatolēs hēliou: the way is prepared for the kings from the rising of the sun. The irony: the drying of the Euphrates (the boundary of the empire) allows the assault that will destroy Babylon.
16.13: pneumata tria akatharta homoia batrachois: three impure spirits like frogs. The frog plague is the second Egyptian plague (Exod 8:1-15) — the unclean animal associated in Jewish thought with impurity and noise. The three frog-spirits emerge from the unholy trinity: the dragon, the beast, and the false prophet. This demonic triad parodies the Holy Trinity — as the Spirit proceeds from the Father and the Son, these spirits proceed from the dragon's unholy delegation. Ekporeuomena ek tou stomatos: proceeding from the mouths — from speech, propaganda, and deceptive proclamation rather than from power.
16.14: pneumata daimoniōn poiountas sēmeia: spirits of demons performing signs (sēmeia). The miraculous signs authenticate the false assembly call — just as the false prophet performed signs to compel beast-worship (13:13-14). The target: tous basileis tēs oikoumenēs holēs (the kings of the whole inhabited world) — assembled for battle (polemon) on the great day of God Almighty (tēs hēmeras tēs megalēs tou theou tou Pantokratoros). The cosmic battle is not fought by human forces but assembled by demonic deception — the kings think they are gathering for conquest but are being gathered for their own destruction.
16.15: Idou erchomai hōs kleptēs: "Look, I come like a thief" — a parenthetical interruption of Christ in the narrative. The same image as 1 Thess 5:2; 2 Pet 3:10; Matt 24:43-44: the thief's coming is sudden and unannounced. Makarios ho grēgorōn kai tērōn ta himatia autou: blessed is the one who stays awake and keeps his garments — the sixth beatitude in Revelation. Keeping garments (himatia) = spiritual alertness and prepared readiness. The naked shame (gymnos) that follows is Adam's shame (Gen 3:7) and the Laodiceans' (3:18): spiritual unprepared exposure. The beatitude is addressed directly to readers in the churches: the battle of Armageddon is the background, the call to watchfulness is the pastoral word.
16.16: ton topon ton kaloumenon Ebraisti Harmagedōn: the place called in Hebrew Harmagedōn. The Greek transliterates Hebrew הַר מְגִדּוֹ (Har Megiddo) = Mount of Megiddo. Megiddo in the Valley of Jezreel (Esdraelon) was the site of decisive historical battles: Deborah and Barak defeated Sisera near Megiddo (Judg 5:19); the good king Josiah died at Megiddo (2 Kgs 23:29). Zech 12:11 speaks of the great mourning of Megiddo as a figure for eschatological lamentation. Revelation uses the name as a symbolic assembly point — the place of decisive battle becomes the world-stage for the final conflict between the Lamb and the beast.
16.17: Gegonen: "It is done!" — the perfect of ginomai: it has happened and stands accomplished. The same announcement formula as 21:6 at the completion of the new creation, here marking the completion of the bowl judgments. The voice from the throne, from the temple (apo tou naou apo tou thronou) — the divine sanctuary is the source of both the command (v. 1) and this completion announcement. Apo tou thronou: from the throne where God and the Lamb are enthroned (22:1, 3) — the completion of judgment is declared from the seat of sovereign authority.
16.18: seismos megas oios ouk egeneto aph' hou anthrōpos egeneto epi tēs gēs: an earthquake (seismos) such as has never occurred since humans were on the earth. The superlative earthquake — exceeding all previous seismic events in human history — marks the eschatological finality of the seventh bowl. Tēlikoutos seismos houtōs megas: so great an earthquake. The earthquake as divine theophany (Exod 19:18; Ps 68:8; Isa 29:6; Ezek 38:19-20) here reaches its ultimate expression — the entire geophysical order disrupted by the presence of God's final judgment.
16.19: hē polis hē megalē eis tria merē: the great city split into three parts. The great city (hē polis hē megalē) = Babylon/Rome (16:19 confirms: Babulōn hē megalē). The tripartite division signals total structural collapse — not damage but disintegration. Emnēsthē Babulōn hē megalē enōpion tou theou dounai autē to potērion tou oinou tou thymou tēs orgēs autou: God remembered Babylon — emnēsthē (remembered) is the divine memory that is coextensive with judgment: when God remembers an evil, he acts against it. The cup of wrath (potērion tou oinou tou thymou) that Babylon forced others to drink (14:8; 17:2; 18:3) is now given to her.
16.20: pasa nēsos ephugen kai orē ouch heurethēsan: every island fled and the mountains could not be found. The disappearance of the geological features — islands and mountains — signals the dissolution of the physical order that has provided hiding places and boundaries. Compare 6:14 (every mountain and island was removed from its place) — the sixth seal previewed what the seventh bowl completes. The mountains and islands that the kings of the earth had retreated to for refuge (6:15-16) are no longer available. The creation's landmarks are erased.
16.21: chalaza megalē hōs talentiaia katabainen ek tou ouranou epi tous anthrōpous: great hailstones, about a talent's weight (talentiaia), falling from heaven on people. A talent = approximately 75-130 pounds. The seventh Egyptian plague was hail (Exod 9:23-25) — here magnified to catastrophic weight. Eblasphēmēsan hoi anthrōpoi ton theon ek tēs plēgēs tēs chalazēs: people blasphemed God because of the plague of hail. The final response to the final plague is still blasphemy — the hardening is complete. The bowl judgment cycle ends where it began (v. 9): with people who know the source of the plagues and curse rather than worship. No repentance occurs under the bowls; this is the judgment, not the call to repentance.
